unit.name: Isolator System
unit.cfj: Provide a closed, decontaminated aseptic processing zone separating product from the surrounding environment

meta.method: Ulwick ODI (Importance × Satisfaction → Opportunity), needs scoring v2

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of operator contact with hazardous compounds through a breached handling interface during active manipulation
rows.plain: Prevent workers from touching dangerous substances through a damaged barrier while handling them. Keeping the barrier intact protects people from harmful exposure.
rows.imp_rat: Operator contact through a breached handling interface during active manipulation is the core exposure event the isolator exists to prevent, making relevance maximal. Severity is catastrophic — direct contact with hazardous compounds during handling causes acute personnel harm.
rows.sat_rat: Glove/half-suit interfaces are the recognized weakest containment link, and pinholes or seam failures during active manipulation remain a real recurring exposure risk. Glove and gauntlet manipulation is inherently slower but does not add exposure delay during normal use. Safe glove-port manipulation depends heavily on trained operator technique and disciplined attention to avoid a breach. Glove replacement and integrity testing consumables carry recurring cost that is largely predictable.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a leaking glove or sleeve assembly passing the integrity test
rows.plain: Make sure a glove or flexible sleeve with a leak cannot pass the safety check. This protects workers and keeps the clean area from being exposed to unwanted material.
rows.imp_rat: A leaking glove passing integrity testing defeats the primary barrier and the verification meant to guard it; this false-negative allows undetected breaches during handling of potent or sterile material, risking both operator exposure and batch contamination.
rows.sat_rat: Automated pressure-decay glove testing reliably catches most leaks, though very small pinholes near detection thresholds can still occasionally pass. Glove integrity testing is quick with automated pressure-decay glove testers. Automated glove leak testers reduce reliance on operator judgement compared with manual visual inspection. Glove testers are a capital cost but reduce costly undetected-leak deviations.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of releasing contained process material into the technician's work area during sample withdrawal
rows.plain: Prevent process material from escaping into the technician’s work area while a sample is being taken. This helps protect the technician and keep the surrounding area clean.
rows.imp_rat: Releasing contained hazardous material into the technician's work area during sampling is central to the containment CFJ (high relevance) and carries catastrophic operator-exposure consequences (max severity).
rows.sat_rat: Closed sampling systems substantially contain process material during withdrawal, though residual release into the work area still occurs when connections are imperfect. Sample withdrawal through contained ports adds modest handling time. Preventing release of process material during sampling depends on careful closed-system technique. Closed sampling devices and consumables represent recurring expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of an aseptic intervention carrying viable contamination to exposed sterile product
rows.plain: Prevent a hands-on action in the sterile work area from carrying germs to product that is exposed. This helps keep the product sterile during necessary work.
rows.imp_rat: An aseptic intervention carrying viable contamination to exposed sterile product directly defeats the CFJ of maintaining an aseptic zone; relevance is maximal and severity catastrophic since contaminated injectable product endangers patients.
rows.sat_rat: Isolator separation plus unidirectional airflow and validated technique dramatically lowers viable transfer versus open RABS, though rare glove-related breaches still occur. Interventions still take real time inside gloves, but validated intervention procedures keep exposure windows bounded. Aseptic technique inside an isolator demands trained operators and disciplined glove work, so residual dependence on human finesse remains. Isolators, glove ports and first-air management are costly but standard capital for this job.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the processing zone is released with contamination above the acceptance limit
rows.plain: Do not release the processing area until testing shows that contamination is below the allowed limit. This helps ensure the area is safe for the next operation.
rows.imp_rat: Releasing a zone with contamination above the acceptance limit means product is filled in a knowingly-compromised state; central to CFJ and severe, as it defeats the acceptance gate protecting patients.
rows.sat_rat: Parametric plus BI release is a mature gate, yet the risk of releasing above the acceptance limit persists where monitoring coverage is incomplete. Release decisions wait on defined criteria checks that add modest but predictable time. Acceptance limits are procedurally defined so release rarely rests on ad hoc operator judgement. BI and chemical-indicator consumables plus review labor are recurring but budgeted costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of tearing or puncturing a barrier glove during an aseptic intervention
rows.plain: Avoid ripping or puncturing the protective glove while reaching into the clean work area. A damaged glove could let germs reach the medicine or equipment.
rows.imp_rat: Tearing/puncturing a barrier glove during intervention creates a direct breach path to product; highly relevant to CFJ and severe as glove failures are a leading contamination cause.
rows.sat_rat: Glove tears and pinholes remain a leading isolator failure mode despite inspection programs, so this need is only partially served. Glove inspection and change-out interrupt operations when damage is suspected. Recognizing and avoiding glove stress points requires trained, attentive handling. Gloves are consumables and pinholes can force costly batch decisions.

rows.source_job: Return isolator through applicable return program
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that residual hazardous material remains when the isolator is released to the approved recipient
rows.plain: Make sure no dangerous material is left inside the enclosed work chamber before it is returned to the approved recipient. This helps protect the people who receive and handle it.
rows.imp_rat: Residual hazardous material remaining when the isolator is released exposes downstream recipients directly, defeating the containment job (high relevance) with potentially irreversible exposure harm (max severity).
rows.sat_rat: Formal cleaning verification and release certificates make confirmed-clean release fairly dependable when the program is followed. Final verification swabbing and hold for lab results delay release to the recipient. Interpreting release criteria and documentation requires qualified QA judgement. Analytical testing and certification add cost per unit released.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the processing zone fails to achieve the required decontamination state at cycle completion
rows.plain: When the cleaning cycle ends, the work area should have reached the required level of germ removal. This helps ensure the area is safe for the next operation.
rows.imp_rat: Failure to achieve the required decontamination state at cycle completion undermines the very foundation of the aseptic zone; relevance is maximal and consequences catastrophic for product sterility.
rows.sat_rat: Mature H2O2 isolator cycles with biological-indicator validation reliably achieve the decontaminated state, though occasional efficacy failures still surface at load-dependent cold spots. Validated automated cycles run in fixed durations that operators do not need to shorten to succeed at this need. Validated recipes and automated sequencing remove most operator judgement from achieving the required end state. Validation and periodic requalification carry real cost but are a fixed part of established practice.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a decontamination cycle leaves viable contamination on isolator interior surfaces
rows.plain: Prevent the cleaning and germ-killing cycle from leaving living germs on the inside surfaces of the sealed work enclosure. This helps keep the enclosure safe for future production.
rows.imp_rat: A decontamination cycle leaving viable contamination on interior surfaces means the zone was never truly decontaminated, central to the CFJ; severity is severe as the whole batch processes in a compromised zone.
rows.sat_rat: Vaporized hydrogen peroxide cycles validated to -log BI kill are a mature, widely deployed practice that reliably decontaminates interior surfaces when properly developed for shadowed areas. Validated H2O2 decontamination cycles run on defined durations that are well characterized, though cycle development and revalidation consume significant time. Cycle development requires specialized aseptic and validation expertise, but routine execution is largely automated with recipe control. Biological indicator studies, cycle development and revalidation are resource-intensive but broadly budgeted as standard practice.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of an isolator boundary breach remaining undetected during production
rows.plain: Prevent a break in the wall of a sealed work enclosure from going unnoticed during production. This helps stop outside germs from reaching the product.
rows.imp_rat: An undetected boundary breach during production means product is filled through an open barrier without warning; central to CFJ and severe.
rows.sat_rat: Pressure decay and continuous monitoring catch many breaches, but small in-run breaches (e.g. glove pinholes) can go undetected until periodic testing. Continuous monitoring gives near-real-time boundary status. Interpreting integrity indicators requires some training. Integrity monitoring instrumentation adds cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of glove or sleeve breaches during aseptic manipulations inside the enclosed zone
rows.plain: Reduce tears or holes in gloves and protective sleeves while work is being done in the enclosed work area. This helps stop germs from getting into the product.
rows.imp_rat: Glove/sleeve breaches during manipulation create contamination paths into the zone; highly relevant to CFJ and severe as breaches directly threaten sterility.
rows.sat_rat: Automated glove leak testing between batches is standard, but micro-breaches occurring mid-manipulation often go undetected until the next test, leaving residual risk. Glove integrity testing is periodic and quick relative to production. Detecting and responding to breaches during manipulation depends on operator awareness. Glove replacement and testing consumables are a recurring but manageable cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that failed aseptic-zone decontamination is accepted for batch authorization
rows.plain: Do not approve a batch when the enclosed work area was not successfully cleaned of harmful germs. This prevents a contaminated product from being authorized.
rows.imp_rat: Accepting failed decontamination for batch authorization is a critical gate failure that releases a non-sterile zone; highly relevant to CFJ and catastrophic in consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Defined acceptance criteria and independent QA sign-off make accepting a clearly failed cycle rare, though borderline results still slip through occasionally. Decontamination cycle results are reviewed on a defined schedule before authorization, adding predictable but bounded delay. Interpreting biological and chemical indicator results against acceptance criteria requires qualified QA judgement that occasionally misjudges marginal cases. Accepting a failed decontamination risks whole-batch loss, so the status quo errs toward re-running cycles at added cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing contaminants into the sterile process during sample withdrawal
rows.plain: Keep dirt or germs from entering the sterile process while taking a sample. This protects both the sample and the remaining product.
rows.imp_rat: Introducing contaminants during sample withdrawal directly attacks the isolator's core sterility-preservation job; a breach here compromises the sterile process (high relevance) and can force batch loss or patient-harming contamination (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Sampling remains a recognized contamination-introduction risk despite sterile sampling devices, since the act breaches the process boundary and depends on operator aseptic skill. Aseptic sampling through dedicated ports is a deliberate, moderately time-consuming step. Sterile sample withdrawal is highly technique-dependent and a frequent source of operator-induced contamination. Sampling systems, sterile connectors and any contaminated repeats add cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of isolator interior surface area retaining particulate, chemical, or microbial residues after cleaning and decontamination
rows.plain: Remove as much dust, chemicals, and germs as possible from the inside surfaces after cleaning and treatment. This helps prevent the next product from being contaminated.
rows.imp_rat: Residual particulate, chemical, or microbial contamination on interior surfaces after cleaning directly defeats decontamination and threatens every subsequent batch; a core measurable indicator of the isolator's sterility-assurance job.
rows.sat_rat: Cleaning validation and vapor decontamination cover most surfaces, but shadowed areas, crevices and hard-to-reach geometry routinely retain measurable residues that standard practice struggles to fully eliminate. Achieving and verifying near-total residue removal requires extended cleaning and sampling cycles. Thorough manual cleaning of complex interior geometry through gloveports depends heavily on operator technique and diligence. Cleaning agents, swab sampling and analytical testing are significant recurring costs.

rows.source_job: Decontaminate the isolator prior to disposal
rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of hazardous contamination remaining on the isolator interior, pass-throughs, and external contact zones after decontamination
rows.plain: Remove as much dangerous contamination as possible from the inside, transfer openings, and outside touch points before the equipment is thrown away. This helps protect people and the surrounding area.
rows.imp_rat: Hazardous contamination remaining on interior, pass-throughs, and external contact zones after decontamination is central to the isolator's cleaning/containment job (relevance high); residual potent contamination causes cross-contamination and exposure across subsequent operations, a severe consequence (severity high).
rows.sat_rat: Validated decon plus surface sampling reliably lowers contamination, though pass-throughs and crevices leave recurring residual gaps. Achieving low residual levels requires repeated decon and swab cycles that extend the timeline. Reaching pass-throughs and external contact zones thoroughly depends on meticulous, trained technicians. Repeat cleaning, swab testing and lab analysis consume real resources.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an adjusted alarm threshold suppresses detection of a genuine enclosure excursion
rows.plain: Avoid changing an alarm limit so much that it hides a real loss of protection inside the sealed enclosure. The warning should still appear when conditions become unsafe.
rows.imp_rat: An adjusted alarm threshold that suppresses detection of a genuine excursion silently disables the safety net, letting real containment failures go unnoticed; central to reliable monitoring with severe consequences given the loss of any warning.
rows.sat_rat: Change control and alarm rationalization exist but nuisance-alarm suppression practices still let poorly chosen thresholds mask real excursions. A suppressed threshold delays or eliminates timely detection, which is the very failure mode in question. Judging safe alarm-threshold adjustments demands expert understanding of the process envelope, a scarce and fallible competency. A missed genuine excursion can cost an entire batch, and current safeguards only partly guard against ill-set thresholds.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of unintended contact between manipulated items and critical sterile surfaces
rows.plain: Prevent handled items from accidentally touching surfaces that must stay germ-free. This helps keep the items and the work area safe.
rows.imp_rat: Unintended contact between manipulated items and critical sterile surfaces is a direct contamination transfer; highly relevant to CFJ and severe as it compromises product-contact sterility.
rows.sat_rat: Constrained glove reach reduces some contact but inadvertent brushing of critical surfaces still happens and is hard to prevent. Careful movements to avoid contact slow interventions somewhat. Avoiding contact with critical sterile surfaces through stiff gloves relies almost entirely on operator care and spatial awareness. Contact errors can scrap product, an occasional cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an intervention exposes sterile product-contact areas to contamination
rows.plain: Prevent a work step from exposing surfaces that touch the germ-free product to contamination. This keeps the product safe during the work.
rows.imp_rat: An intervention exposing product-contact areas to contamination directly defeats aseptic protection; relevance high and severity severe due to direct product risk.
rows.sat_rat: Glove-port interventions in isolators substantially reduce contamination versus open manipulation, yet outcomes still vary with individual operator discipline. Interventions add time but are procedurally bounded within the isolator. Aseptic interventions through gloves rely heavily on operator technique and vigilance, making this a persistent skill-dependent failure point. Training, gowning and qualification programs represent recurring costs but are established.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of product processed while product-environment separation is not assured
rows.plain: Limit how much product is made while it is uncertain whether the product is protected from the surrounding environment. This reduces the amount that might later need to be discarded.
rows.imp_rat: Processing product while separation is not assured directly produces potentially contaminated units; highly relevant to the CFJ and severe because the volume of at-risk product scales harm.
rows.sat_rat: Continuous monitoring and stop rules limit exposure, but detection lag means some at-risk product is routinely processed before the halt. Once separation loss is suspected, stopping the line is fast, but the interval before detection lets some product continue processing. Recognizing that separation is not assured mid-run demands trained operators to spot subtle indicators, so it leans on vigilance. Product processed under compromised conditions is scrapped, a recurring material loss the status quo only partially prevents.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of accepting an enclosure with an undetected boundary breach
rows.plain: Avoid approving a sealed work area that has a hidden opening or leak in its protective boundary. Finding such damage first helps keep contamination out.
rows.imp_rat: Accepting an isolator with an undetected boundary breach directly undermines containment, so relevance is very high. Severity is severe because a breach shipped into service means silent, ongoing exposure risk that may go unnoticed until harm occurs.
rows.sat_rat: Pressure-decay and constant-pressure methods per ISO 10648 catch gross and moderate leaks reliably, yet undetected small breaches at acceptance remain a recognized residual risk. Acceptance leak testing adds hours to commissioning but is a scheduled, budgeted step that rarely stalls the stage. Interpreting borderline pressure-decay results still leans on experienced qualification engineers, so subtle breaches can slip past less-seasoned staff. Instrumented pressure-decay rigs and re-test cycles cost money but are minor against total isolator commissioning spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of contact between sterile product-contact surfaces and contaminated surfaces during interventions
rows.plain: Limit how often surfaces that touch the sterile product come into contact with dirty surfaces during hands-on work, so germs are not transferred to the product.
rows.imp_rat: Contact between sterile and contaminated surfaces during interventions transfers contamination to product-contact areas; highly relevant to CFJ and severe.
rows.sat_rat: Decontaminated interiors and layout help, but contact-frequency control still leans on human technique. Avoiding cross-contact adds no time if planned well. Keeping product-contact surfaces away from contaminated ones depends on operator discipline and layout awareness. Cross-contact incidents can waste product and materials.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of hazardous residues escaping the isolator into adjacent controlled areas during disassembly or removal
rows.plain: Keep harmful leftover material contained while the enclosed unit is being taken apart or removed. This protects nearby work areas and the people in them.
rows.imp_rat: Hazardous residue escaping into adjacent controlled areas during disassembly directly undermines the containment mission (relevance high); escape of potent APIs into surrounding areas risks worker exposure and facility contamination with serious health/compliance consequences (severity high).
rows.sat_rat: Transfer between rooms during disassembly is a known weak point where residues can migrate despite standard precautions. Moving components out through airlocks under containment adds handling time to disassembly. Preventing spread to adjacent controlled areas relies on trained crews maintaining bagging and transfer discipline. Wrapping, double-bagging and dedicated transfer routes consume consumables and labor.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of hazardous compounds escaping from the enclosure into the surrounding cleanroom
rows.plain: Keep dangerous chemicals from escaping the enclosed area into the surrounding clean room. This protects people and keeps the room clean and safe.
rows.imp_rat: Preventing hazardous compounds from escaping into the cleanroom is the very definition of containment — the CFJ fails outright if this occurs. Severity is catastrophic: operator exposure to potent/toxic compounds and facility-wide contamination causing irreversible harm.
rows.sat_rat: Mature negative-pressure isolator design with validated OEB containment routinely achieves sub-microgram escape levels, making leakage into the cleanroom uncommon. Continuous pressure and particle monitoring provide real-time containment status without slowing usage. Once validated and installed, maintaining negative-pressure containment demands little operator expertise beyond following SOPs. HEPA filtration, pressure cascades and monitoring add operating cost but are amortized across long service life.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of authorizing production resumption while product-environment separation remains compromised
rows.plain: Do not restart production while the product is still not properly separated from the surrounding environment, because this could expose it to contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Authorizing resumption while separation remains compromised is a decision-gate failure that greenlights contaminated production; highly relevant to CFJ integrity and severe in consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Formal batch-release and resumption approval procedures with signed checklists are mature and widely deployed, making an authorization while separation is compromised uncommon though not impossible when evidence is misread. Authorization gates run at line-clearance speed but the checklist review adds real minutes that overseers accept to avoid premature restart. Sound resumption decisions still hinge on an experienced overseer manually correlating integrity, decontamination, and environmental evidence, so quality depends heavily on individual judgement. A wrong resumption is enormously costly through batch loss and recalls, so the status quo over-invests review effort rather than risk it.

rows.source_job: Verify containment integrity of assembled isolator
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a leak in a fixed enclosure joint or penetration remains undetected before first use
rows.plain: Find any leak in the enclosure's fixed connections or openings before the enclosure is used for the first time. This confirms that it can keep materials safely contained.
rows.imp_rat: An undetected leak in a fixed joint or penetration before first use defeats containment from the outset, so relevance is very high. Severity is severe — a latent leak means exposure and contamination risk goes unmitigated across all subsequent operations.
rows.sat_rat: Whole-enclosure pressure testing plus soap/tracer-gas techniques on penetrations catches most fixed-joint leaks, though a slow leak below detection sensitivity can persist to first use. Joint and penetration leak checks are integrated into the standard integrity test window without meaningful added delay. Locating which specific joint leaks after a failed decay test requires skilled fault-tracing that not all commissioning teams possess. Sealant, gaskets and rework on penetrations are inexpensive relative to the value of a compliant enclosure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing isolator containment while operating installed equipment
rows.plain: Keep the sealed enclosure intact while the built-in equipment is running. This prevents hazardous material from escaping or outside contamination from entering.
rows.imp_rat: Losing containment while operating installed equipment strikes at the isolator's core operational job during normal production. Severity is severe because continuous operation means exposure and contamination can escalate before detection.
rows.sat_rat: Dynamic equipment penetrations (shafts, seals, cabling) create containment vulnerabilities during operation that are managed but not fully eliminated. Installed equipment operation proceeds without added exposure delay under normal conditions. Operating in-isolator equipment through gloves demands dexterity and attention that can lapse. Sealed penetrations and equipment integration add engineering cost to the isolator.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of containment conditions remaining outside validated limits during processing
rows.plain: Shorten the time that the conditions needed to safely contain dangerous chemicals are outside their approved safe limits during processing. This reduces the chance of chemicals escaping.
rows.imp_rat: Prolonged operation outside validated containment limits directly undermines the isolator's core job of protecting product and operator; the longer the excursion persists, the greater the odds of contaminated product reaching patients or operator exposure to potent compounds.
rows.sat_rat: Feedback control brings conditions back reliably for common upsets, but complex faults extend the out-of-limit duration unpredictably. Once an excursion is detected, automated controls restore validated conditions within a bounded interval, but the correction is not instantaneous. Restoring conditions is largely automated, though operator intervention for root causes still requires expertise. Prolonged out-of-limit time can force product holds or rejections, creating cost exposure that current controls only partly limit.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a glove or sleeve breach during enclosed manipulation
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that a glove or sleeve tears or develops a hole while someone works inside a closed enclosure. This helps keep the worker and the materials protected.
rows.imp_rat: A glove or sleeve breach during enclosed manipulation is the most direct failure of the isolator's containment barrier (relevance high) and causes both product contamination and operator exposure to hazardous material (severity high).
rows.sat_rat: Physical glove and sleeve breaches during enclosed work remain a frequent maintenance and deviation trigger despite pressure-decay integrity testing. Manipulation continues at normal pace as breaches are usually detected only at scheduled tests. Avoiding glove/sleeve breach depends on careful handling and awareness of sharp or abrasive contact. Glove and sleeve replacement plus testing represent ongoing modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of environmental contaminants entering the enclosed zone through an enclosure integrity breach
rows.plain: Keep outside dirt and germs from entering the enclosed work area through a hole, tear, or other loss of enclosure. Such an opening could contaminate the product.
rows.imp_rat: Environmental contaminants entering via an integrity breach is a core failure of the barrier CFJ; relevance high and severity severe due to direct product contamination.
rows.sat_rat: Positive-pressure isolators with automated pressure-decay leak testing are a mature standard that keeps environmental ingress low, though gasket and glove degradation between tests leaves a residual gap. Leak testing takes measurable time per campaign but is a scheduled, bounded activity. Integrity testing and interpretation require trained technicians but follow established protocols. Automated pressure-decay leak test equipment and glove testing are standard fixed costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of viable contamination entering the processing zone with transferred material
rows.plain: Keep live germs from entering the processing area along with transferred materials. This protects the product while materials are moved into the work area.
rows.imp_rat: Viable contamination entering with transferred material is a primary breach of the aseptic boundary; relevance high and severity severe as viable organisms can compromise product sterility.
rows.sat_rat: Established transfer-decon practices catch most contamination but shadowed surfaces and manual wipe-downs still let some through. Surface decontamination of transferred items adds cycle time before entry. Judging what needs decontamination and loading it correctly requires trained personnel. Sporicidal agents and transfer decontamination consumables add recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of aseptic processing conducted while sterile conditions remain outside required limits
rows.plain: Shorten the time that sterile processing continues while clean conditions are outside the required limits. This matters to reduce the chance that the product becomes contaminated.
rows.imp_rat: Aseptic processing conducted while sterile conditions are out of limits directly produces at-risk product; highly relevant to CFJ and severe.
rows.sat_rat: Monitoring and alarms drive timely stops, though response latency varies. The need is to shorten out-of-limit processing time, and alarms prompt fairly quick action. Recognizing that conditions are out of limit and halting requires trained response. Processing under out-of-limit conditions risks product cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of aseptic processing resuming before required sterile conditions are restored
rows.plain: Do not restart the germ-free process until the required clean conditions have been restored. Restarting too soon could expose the product to contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Resuming aseptic processing before sterile conditions are restored fills product in a non-ready zone; highly relevant to CFJ and severe.
rows.sat_rat: Environmental and pressure checks gate resumption reasonably well, but reliance on operator judgment leaves gaps. Waiting for confirmed sterile conditions before resuming adds recovery time. Judging when conditions are truly restored requires trained assessment. Premature resumption risks costly batch loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contaminated components or materials entering the enclosed zone during transfer
rows.plain: Keep contaminated parts or materials from entering the enclosed work area during transfer. Contaminated items could make the products unsafe.
rows.imp_rat: Contaminated components entering during transfer directly seeds contamination into the closed zone; central to CFJ and severe consequences for the batch.
rows.sat_rat: Rapid transfer ports and validated material decontamination reliably manage most transfers, but transfer steps remain a leading residual contamination pathway in practice. Transfer through RTPs or decon pass-throughs adds handling time but is routine. Correct aseptic transfer technique demands trained operators and remains a recurring source of error-prone finesse. Rapid transfer ports, VHP airlocks and pre-sterilized components carry meaningful cost but are standard.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that loss of isolator barrier integrity remains undetected before batch authorization
rows.plain: Make it less likely that a loss of the isolator’s protective separation goes unnoticed before a batch is approved. This matters because the product may no longer be protected from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected loss of barrier integrity before authorization means a breached zone is unknowingly released; central to CFJ assurance and severe because the defect propagates to product.
rows.sat_rat: Automated leak tests and pressure monitoring are standard and catch gross failures reliably, but slow-developing or intermittent breaches can still evade pre-authorization detection. Integrity tests (glove and pressure-decay) take time to run and results are awaited before authorization, adding a genuine delay. Detecting subtle barrier breaches like micro-leaks depends on correctly run tests and skilled interpretation, leaving room for missed defects. An undetected breach reaching authorization triggers costly investigation and batch loss, which drives investment in routine testing.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the glove barrier is breached during aseptic manipulation
rows.plain: Avoid tearing or opening the glove barrier while handling materials in the germ-free area. A damaged barrier could let germs reach the product.
rows.imp_rat: A glove barrier breach during aseptic manipulation is a primary containment failure central to the isolator's protective job (relevance high); it simultaneously exposes the operator to hazardous material and compromises product sterility, a severe dual consequence (severity high).
rows.sat_rat: Glove barrier breaches during aseptic work are a well-known failure mode; integrity testing catches many but in-process undetected pinholes still occur. Aseptic glove manipulation is slow but does not directly delay the process. Aseptic technique through gloves is highly skill-dependent and error-prone even for trained operators. Glove integrity testing and sterile glove replacement are recurring consumable costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of installing filtration media with specifications incompatible with the isolator's containment requirements
rows.plain: Install air filters that provide the level of protection the enclosed work area needs. The wrong filters may not keep unwanted material contained.
rows.imp_rat: Installing filtration media incompatible with containment requirements directly defeats the isolator's core job of containing hazardous product; relevance is high because filtration integrity is central to containment, and severity is high because breached containment exposes operators to potent compounds and compromises product sterility.
rows.sat_rat: Part-number control, change-control paperwork and receiving QC catch most mismatches, yet look-alike media and substitution still produce occasional incompatible installs. Verifying filter specs against the isolator's containment requirements adds documentation time but is not a bottleneck. Matching HEPA/ULPA grade, seals and mounting to the isolator's classification requires a technician who understands the containment spec, so errors track experience. A wrong filter means scrapping media and repeating installation, a moderate but real material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a boundary breach exposing the internal environment to surrounding air
rows.plain: The chance that a damaged boundary lets outside air enter the protected area should be as small as possible. This helps keep the area free of germs.
rows.imp_rat: A boundary breach exposing the interior to surrounding air directly negates the closed-separation CFJ; relevance maximal and severity high because it admits environmental contamination onto product.
rows.sat_rat: Positive-pressure sealed isolators reliably keep surrounding air out, a mature and well-proven design. Positive pressure and sealed construction maintain boundary integrity continuously without added operator time. Boundary integrity is largely engineered into the enclosure rather than depending on operator skill. Sealed isolator hardware and pressure control represent significant but well-justified spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing microbial or particulate contamination into the isolator during glove and sleeve assembly replacement
rows.plain: Make it less likely that germs or particles enter the enclosed clean chamber while its flexible gloves and sleeves are replaced. This helps keep the product and work area clean.
rows.imp_rat: Glove/sleeve replacement is a known high-risk breach point; introducing microbial or particulate contamination during this operation directly defeats the aseptic barrier and can contaminate subsequent batches, a core failure mode for isolators.
rows.sat_rat: Established change-out ports and aseptic procedures limit ingress, but manual glove replacement remains a recognized recurring source of microbial and particulate contamination. Glove replacement typically requires a decontamination cycle before and requalification after, adding time. Aseptic glove-change technique to avoid introducing contamination is skill-intensive and error-prone if rushed. Replacement assemblies plus post-change decontamination are recurring costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of closed-zone conditions exceeding applicable sterility limits during production
rows.plain: Reduce how often the enclosed work area goes above the allowed level of contamination during production. This helps keep the product safe to make and use.
rows.imp_rat: Closed-zone conditions exceeding sterility limits during production directly threaten product sterility; central to CFJ and severe consequences for the batch.
rows.sat_rat: Real-time particle and pressure monitoring with alarms keeps most closed-zone conditions within limits reliably, though microbial excursions are only confirmed after incubation delay. Continuous monitoring provides near-real-time visibility during production. Setting and interpreting sterility-relevant limits requires expertise but is codified in procedures. Environmental monitoring and continuous sensors carry ongoing cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that protective-control testing exposes the technician to a hazardous state
rows.plain: Make it very unlikely that testing a safety feature puts the person doing the test in danger. The test should confirm the feature works without creating a hazardous situation.
rows.imp_rat: Protective-control testing that exposes a technician to a hazardous state is directly relevant to safe operation of the containment system; severity is maximal because it can cause acute personnel exposure to highly potent or toxic agents, an irreversible harm to a human.
rows.sat_rat: LOTO, permits and interlock design reduce exposure, but ad-hoc test setups and human shortcuts leave a meaningful residual hazard. Safe protective-control testing requires deliberate lockout/setup steps that lengthen the task. Deliberately driving a control toward its trip state without exposing the technician demands strong hazard awareness and procedure discipline, a real skill dependency. PPE, interlocks and safe-test rigs add modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of compromised isolator containment after the utility interruption
rows.plain: Shorten the time that the enclosed clean area remains unprotected after a loss of electricity or another needed service. Faster recovery helps keep the product from being contaminated.
rows.imp_rat: Prolonged compromised containment after a utility interruption leaves the barrier down during an unplanned event; severe because operator exposure and product loss accumulate with duration, and central to resilient containment.
rows.sat_rat: UPS and backup provisions help but the duration of compromised containment after a utility hit varies widely and is not tightly bounded. Restoring containment after a utility loss depends on utility recovery plus re-establishment sequences, which can take a prolonged interval. Recovery choreography needs experienced operators to bring systems back in the right order. Extended compromised periods risk product loss and rework, a meaningful cost exposure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an adverse environmental trend remains unrecognized until after product exposure in the isolator
rows.plain: Avoid missing a worsening change in the surrounding conditions until after the product has been exposed inside the sealed enclosure. Early recognition helps prevent affected product from being used.
rows.imp_rat: An unrecognized adverse environmental trend until after product exposure means contamination is caught too late; relevant to detection assurance and severe due to already-exposed product.
rows.sat_rat: Trending programs exist, but the fundamental incubation delay means adverse conditions are often confirmed too late to protect the exposed lot. Microbial EM results lag production by the incubation period, so adverse trends are frequently recognized only after product exposure. Trend analysis requires skilled microbiologists interpreting lagging data. Trending software and lab capacity are standard costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of breaching separation between the processing zone and surrounding environment during transfer
rows.plain: Make sure the protective separation between the processing area and the surrounding space is not broken while materials are moved. This helps keep outside contamination away from the product.
rows.imp_rat: Breaching separation during transfer is a common contamination pathway directly opposing the CFJ; relevance high and severity severe as it can expose product mid-process.
rows.sat_rat: Rapid transfer ports and interlocked airlocks maintain separation well, though manual bag/port handling leaves occasional exposure risk. Transfer through RTP or airlocks adds handling time but is routine. Correct transfer sequences require training and attention to avoid exposing the boundary. RTP ports and pass-through chambers are expensive validated components.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that isolator airflow patterns expose sterile product to contamination
rows.plain: Make sure the movement of air inside the protected area does not carry contamination onto the sterile product. This helps keep the product clean and safe.
rows.imp_rat: Airflow patterns exposing sterile product to contamination is a core aseptic-zone failure mode; relevance high and severity severe as compromised airflow directly contaminates open product.
rows.sat_rat: Unidirectional airflow with smoke-study qualification is a mature practice, though turbulence around equipment and interventions still creates localized risk that visualization only partly captures. Airflow qualification via smoke studies is time-consuming but performed at defined intervals. Interpreting airflow visualization and designing unidirectional flow requires specialist judgement. Smoke study execution and CFD analysis are notable but accepted qualification costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a failed protective control incorrectly passes verification
rows.plain: Make it very unlikely that a broken safety feature is incorrectly judged to be working. Testing must clearly identify failures before the equipment is used.
rows.imp_rat: A failed protective control that wrongly passes verification leaves a latent safety gap in the containment system; when relied upon during an actual event it fails to protect, exposing operators and product with no warning.
rows.sat_rat: Standardized functional test protocols catch most failures, but a control that fails in an undetected mode or a poorly designed test can still yield a false pass. Protective-control verification is a defined test step that adds routine time. Correctly verifying interlocks and alarms requires a qualified technician who understands failure modes to avoid false passes. Test tooling and qualified-tester time are modest recurring costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a loss of product-environment separation remains undetected through disposition review
rows.plain: Make sure any loss of protection between the product and its surroundings is noticed during the final review. This helps prevent an unsafe product from being approved.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected separation loss through disposition review is a detection-gate failure allowing contaminated batches to pass; highly relevant and severe in consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Batch record and deviation review catch many separation losses, but subtle or undocumented breaches can slip through review, leaving a real detection gap. Disposition review adds cycle time before batch release but is a scheduled step. Detecting a lost separation event during review demands experienced quality reviewers correlating disparate records. Quality review labor and documentation systems are standard overhead.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of overlooking credible contamination pathways across the proposed isolator-zone boundary
rows.plain: Make sure all realistic ways that germs could cross the planned barrier around the protected area are considered. This helps prevent important contamination risks from being overlooked.
rows.imp_rat: Overlooking credible contamination pathways across the boundary leaves unmitigated ingress routes; relevant to CFJ design assurance and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Even with risk assessments, credible pathways are routinely overlooked, making this a chronic weak spot. Systematic contamination-pathway review is slow, and abbreviated timelines lead to superficial coverage. Identifying credible pathways requires seasoned aseptic risk expertise that is genuinely scarce. Missed pathways cost catastrophically in contamination events but flagging them early is cheap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of disrupted airflow carrying contaminants to exposed sterile product
rows.plain: Prevent changes in air movement from carrying dirt or germs onto exposed sterile products. This helps keep the products free from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Disrupted airflow carrying contaminants to exposed product directly defeats first-air protection; highly relevant to CFJ and severe as it contaminates open product.
rows.sat_rat: Smoke-studied unidirectional airflow reliably sweeps contaminants, though operator movements can still transiently disrupt it. Airflow recovery after disturbance is quick by design. Operators must move deliberately to avoid disturbing first air, requiring some training. Qualified unidirectional airflow systems are costly infrastructure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of harmful decontamination residue remaining in the closed zone before sterile filling
rows.plain: Leave as little harmful cleaning residue as possible in the sealed work area before sterile filling begins. This helps protect the product and the people who may handle it.
rows.imp_rat: Harmful decontamination residue (e.g., H2O2) before filling contaminates product chemically; highly relevant to a safe closed zone with severe patient-safety consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Established VHP aeration and validated H2O2 residual limits make consistent residue clearance a mature, well-served outcome for most standard configurations. Aeration cycles are time-consuming to develop but well-characterized once set, so residual clearance rarely adds unexpected delay for the specifier. Setting residue limits requires toxicological and analytical expertise that is available but concentrated in a few specialists the influencer must rely on. Residue verification testing consumes analytical resources and cycle-development time, a moderate recurring cost the buyer must budget for.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of recovery interventions introducing contamination into the processing zone
rows.plain: Make sure efforts to restart the process do not bring germs or other unwanted material into the work area. This protects the material being made.
rows.imp_rat: Recovery interventions introducing contamination reverse the restoration effort; relevant to CFJ and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Interventions are a recognized high-risk contamination pathway that procedures only partially mitigate. Recovery interventions extend the cycle timeline. Performing interventions without introducing contamination demands high aseptic skill and judgement. Failed interventions can force costly full reruns.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that favorable isolator indicators conceal an adverse condition in a critical zone
rows.plain: Do not let reassuring equipment readings hide a harmful condition in an important work area. The area should be checked in ways that can reveal problems the readings miss.
rows.imp_rat: Favorable indicators concealing an adverse condition is a false-negative detection failure that masks real contamination; relevant to CFJ assurance and severe.
rows.sat_rat: Sensor placement rarely covers every critical microenvironment, so favorable aggregate indicators can conceal localized adverse conditions. Real-time indicators are timely but may not represent the true critical zone state. Recognizing when favorable readings mask a hidden problem requires deep process expertise. Additional sensors at critical points add cost but are optionally deployed.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an aseptic procedural deviation remains undetected before the affected batch advances
rows.plain: Make sure a mistake in the steps used to keep the product germ-free is found before the affected batch moves forward. This prevents a possibly unsafe batch from continuing.
rows.imp_rat: An undetected aseptic procedural deviation before the batch advances allows compromised product through; relevant to CFJ detection and severe.
rows.sat_rat: Batch-record review and in-process supervision catch many deviations, but real-time detection of subtle procedural errors remains inconsistent. Procedural deviations may go unnoticed for an entire operation before review catches them, so detection is not timely. Spotting an aseptic procedural deviation relies on operator self-awareness or supervisor observation, which is attention-intensive and error-prone. An undetected deviation advancing with the batch risks costly downstream rejection.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of critical processing-zone surfaces retaining viable contamination after preparation
rows.plain: Reduce the amount of living contamination left on important work surfaces after preparation. This helps keep the product from becoming contaminated.
rows.imp_rat: Critical surfaces retaining viable contamination after preparation directly compromises the aseptic zone; highly relevant to CFJ and severe.
rows.sat_rat: Validated cycles with biological indicators verify surface decontamination effectiveness reliably. Surface decontamination cycles are time-bounded. Cycle-based decon reduces reliance on operator skill. Decontaminant cost is recurring.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a physical boundary breach exposing the decontaminated zone to external contamination
rows.plain: Prevent breaks in the physical barrier around a cleaned area that could let outside contamination enter. This helps keep the area clean after it has been decontaminated.
rows.imp_rat: A physical boundary breach exposing the decontaminated zone to external contamination directly defeats the barrier CFJ; highly relevant and severe.
rows.sat_rat: Positive pressure and robust seals make physical boundary breaches exposing the zone uncommon. Boundary integrity checks add little time. Physical boundary integrity relies on design and periodic testing, not per-cycle operator skill. Gasket and seal maintenance is a modest recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the candidate zone loses product separation under intended-use conditions
rows.plain: Make sure the proposed work area keeps the product separated from surrounding materials during normal use. This helps prevent the product from being contaminated or mixed with something else.
rows.imp_rat: Losing product separation under intended-use conditions is a fundamental CFJ failure; relevance maximal and severity severe since it means the barrier fails during normal operation.
rows.sat_rat: Mature isolator qualification (leak, glove integrity, pressure) makes separation loss uncommon under intended use, so this is comparatively well served. Demonstrating separation robustness across use conditions takes extended qualification campaigns. Assessing loss-of-separation risk demands specialized aseptic and containment expertise. Failures in the field are extremely costly, driving heavy up-front investment.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of breaching the isolator containment barrier during material transfer
rows.plain: Prevent the sealed protective enclosure from being opened or damaged while materials are moved through its openings. This helps keep harmful substances or germs from getting in or out.
rows.imp_rat: Breaching the containment barrier during material transfer is a frequent, high-exposure failure point central to the isolator's job. Severity is severe because transfers are routine and a breach exposes operators directly to hazardous material.
rows.sat_rat: Rapid transfer port technology provides well-established containment during transfer, though procedural error and improper docking still occasionally breach the barrier. Validated transfer procedures through RTPs or airlocks add cycle time but are routine. Correct alpha-beta port docking and airlock sequencing require trained operators who can err under pressure. Rapid transfer ports and validated transfer containers are costly consumables and hardware.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a separation failure remains undetected under a defined operating condition
rows.plain: Lower the chance that a failure to keep things separated goes unnoticed under a specified operating condition. This helps provide clear proof that the separation works when it is needed.
rows.imp_rat: An undetected separation failure under a defined condition means the barrier fails silently; relevant to CFJ assurance and severe since undetected failure exposes product without warning.
rows.sat_rat: Continuous pressure and environmental monitoring catch most failures, but blind spots for slow or intermittent failures persist. Building detection into qualification adds monitoring design time but is well-precedented. Designing detection for undetected failure modes requires strong instrumentation and monitoring know-how. Undetected failures cause batch loss, so investment in monitoring is justified and common.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of operator exposure to hazardous residual materials during removal
rows.plain: Prevent the operator from coming into contact with dangerous leftover material while removing it. This helps protect the operator from harm.
rows.imp_rat: Operator exposure to hazardous residuals during removal is central to the containment job's protective purpose. Severity is high — direct dermal/inhalation exposure to potent compounds can cause serious, potentially irreversible harm to personnel.
rows.sat_rat: Mature CIP/wipe-down protocols and airlock discipline substantially reduce exposure, but residual pockets and human slips still cause occasional incidents. Full decontamination and controlled removal add days to the disposal timeline but the schedule is generally accommodated. Safe residue removal depends heavily on trained, gowned operators following validated SOPs, so outcomes hinge on scarce expertise and vigilance. PPE, containment consumables and specialist labor make each removal expensive, though the spend is budgeted as routine.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of contacts between contaminated glove surfaces and critical zone surfaces
rows.plain: Have fewer touches between dirty gloves and surfaces in areas that must stay especially clean. This helps prevent contamination from spreading.
rows.imp_rat: Contacts between contaminated glove surfaces and critical surfaces transfer contamination to product-contact zones; highly relevant to CFJ and severe.
rows.sat_rat: Glove-to-surface contacts remain a leading, only partially controlled contamination risk dependent on human technique. Careful glove handling slows manipulations within the zone. Avoiding glove contact with critical surfaces depends entirely on operator technique and aseptic training. Glove integrity testing and replacement add cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that sterile product, components, or critical surfaces become contaminated during the interruption and recovery
rows.plain: Keep the product, its parts, and important clean surfaces free of germs while the interrupted process is stopped and restarted. This protects product safety during recovery.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination of sterile product or critical surfaces during interruption and recovery covers the vulnerable non-steady-state period central to sterility assurance (high relevance) with batch-integrity consequences (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: SOPs and interlocks help, but transitions and recoveries remain among the least deterministic phases, so contamination during recovery recurs. Recovery from an interruption can stretch across an extended period, prolonging exposure of sterile surfaces to potential contamination. Safe recovery relies on operators executing complex re-sanitization and line-clearance judgement calls that are hard to standardize. Compromised components and product during recovery often must be scrapped, driving material loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of viable contamination remaining in the enclosed zone after restoration
rows.plain: Make sure no living germs remain in the enclosed work area after it has been restored. This helps keep the next operation clean and safe.
rows.imp_rat: Viable contamination remaining after restoration means the zone is not truly aseptic when processing resumes; highly relevant to CFJ and severe.
rows.sat_rat: Validated bio-decontamination reliably achieves specified log reduction, making residual viable contamination rare. Validated decontamination cycles have defined durations. Automated VHP cycles reduce operator skill dependence. Decontaminant and cycle energy are recurring costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of overlooking early glove or isolator-barrier damage signs during an intervention
rows.plain: Help workers notice the first signs that a glove or protective enclosure may be damaged during an intervention. Finding damage early can prevent germs from reaching the product.
rows.imp_rat: Overlooking early glove/barrier damage signs allows a breach to progress undetected; relevant to CFJ detection and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Visual checks are inconsistent and early-stage damage is frequently overlooked until a formal test. Spotting early damage in real time doesn't add time if noticed. Detecting subtle glove or barrier damage mid-intervention relies heavily on operator vigilance and experience. Missed early damage can escalate into costly loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the designated operating mode fails to establish the required pressure direction and airflow conditions
rows.plain: Make sure the selected operating setting creates the needed direction of pressure and movement of air. This helps keep contamination from entering the protected area.
rows.imp_rat: Failure to establish the required pressure direction and airflow is the fundamental mechanism by which the isolator maintains both sterility and containment (very high relevance), and its loss can breach containment or sterility catastrophically (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Modern isolators use interlocked automated control of pressure direction and airflow with alarms, so failure to establish required conditions is uncommon and usually flagged. Automated mode changeovers establish airflow and pressure conditions quickly on command. Automated control systems handle pressure/airflow setup with little operator judgement required. PLC control, sensors and validation add capital cost but not high running cost.

rows.source_job: Define and document material transfer protocols
rows.stmt: Minimize the amount by which specified decontamination dwell periods fall short of required exposure
rows.plain: Make sure materials are exposed to the cleaning treatment for nearly all of the required time, without falling short. This helps ensure harmful contamination is removed before materials are moved.
rows.imp_rat: Shortfalls in decontamination dwell time leave viable microbial or chemical residues, directly compromising the sterility assurance central to the isolator's job; consequences include contaminated batches and failed sterility with potential patient harm.
rows.sat_rat: PLC-controlled decontamination recipes enforce dwell setpoints consistently, so shortfalls against specified exposure are rare and alarmed. Dwell periods are pre-defined recipe parameters, so meeting them adds no unplanned time. Automated cycle control removes reliance on operator timing judgement for dwell exposure. Meeting dwell requirements consumes agent and cycle time but at negligible marginal cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician exposure to residual hazardous material during glove and sleeve assembly removal
rows.plain: Protect the technician from contact with dangerous material left inside the equipment when protective gloves and sleeves are removed. This reduces the risk of harmful exposure.
rows.imp_rat: Technician exposure to residual hazardous material during glove/sleeve removal is a well-known high-risk task central to worker protection (high relevance) with direct toxic-exposure consequences (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Decontamination-before-removal and careful technique reduce exposure, but residual hazardous material on worn gloves is a persistent variable that technique alone cannot fully eliminate. Glove/sleeve removal is preceded by decontamination cycles, adding scheduled time before handling. Safe glove change-out requires trained technique to fold contaminated surfaces inward and avoid touch-contamination. Replacement gloves, sleeves and PPE are frequent recurring consumables.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of sterile batch contamination while the operator diagnoses or resolves an alarm or deviation
rows.plain: Prevent the sterile batch from becoming contaminated while a worker investigates or fixes a warning or problem. This protects the product while the issue is being handled.
rows.imp_rat: Sterile batch contamination while diagnosing an alarm or deviation targets a high-risk intervention window central to maintaining sterility (high relevance), with batch-loss and patient-safety consequences (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: First-air protection and closed-isolator design reduce risk, but any prolonged diagnostic activity inside a sterile zone still yields recurring contamination events. Diagnosing an alarm takes minutes to hours during which the sterile field remains exposed, extending the window of vulnerability. Protecting the batch while troubleshooting depends heavily on operator discipline and aseptic technique, a scarce and error-prone competency. A contaminated sterile batch is a very high-value loss, and current practice offers only partial protection against it during deviations.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which the required containment response remains inactive after activation
rows.plain: Make sure the required response to a breach starts fully and quickly after it is turned on. This helps limit the effects of a loss of containment.
rows.imp_rat: A required containment response that remains inactive after activation means protection is nominally engaged but ineffective, directly defeating the safety job (high relevance) with severe undetected-exposure consequences (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Response actuation is usually completed as designed, but incomplete or partial activation after command remains a possible failure that verification only partly catches. Once initiated, the response reaches full effect within a designed short interval. Confirming the response fully activated requires minimal operator skill given status feedback. Redundant actuators and verification add modest cost over base design.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of incoming transfers that carry contaminants into the sterile isolator chamber
rows.plain: Prevent incoming materials from carrying dirt or germs into the germ-free chamber. This helps protect the product from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Incoming transfers carrying contaminants into the sterile chamber is one of the leading real-world routes of isolator contamination, central to the sterility job (relevance high); contaminated transfers compromise the entire chamber and product (severity high).
rows.sat_rat: Surface bio-decontamination of incoming transfers is a mature validated practice, though shadowed surfaces and procedural lapses still let some contaminants enter. Incoming transfer decontamination cycles (VHP, sporicidal wipe) add setup time before entry. Loading and decontaminating incoming items follows procedures that require trained but not expert execution. Sporicidal agents, VHP consumables and transfer hardware carry ongoing cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of chamber locations that remain above the viable-contamination acceptance threshold after the initial cycle
rows.plain: After the first cleaning cycle, reduce the number of chamber areas where living germs are still above the allowed level. This helps make the chamber safe for the process.
rows.imp_rat: Chamber locations remaining above the viable-contamination threshold after the initial decontamination cycle directly defeats the isolator's core job of maintaining a sterile/contained environment (relevance high); undetected residual bioburden can contaminate product batches leading to recalls or patient harm (severity high).
rows.sat_rat: Vaporized hydrogen peroxide cycles with BI mapping reliably achieve the acceptance threshold across most locations, with cold/shadowed spots being the well-known residual challenge. Bio-decontamination cycle development and settling take significant time, but the mapping runs on a defined qualification schedule. Selecting representative biological-indicator locations to prove worst-case kill still demands microbiological and cycle-development expertise. Biological indicators, media and repeated cycle runs add cost but are standard qualification expenditure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of personnel interventions that violate approved closed-zone constraints
rows.plain: Reduce how often workers take actions that break the approved rules for working in the protected area. This helps maintain safety and meet required standards.
rows.imp_rat: Personnel interventions violating closed-zone constraints introduce contamination risk; relevant to CFJ and moderately-high severity since it depends on the specific violation.
rows.sat_rat: Procedural controls and video review reduce unauthorized interventions, yet human behavior remains variable and hard to fully constrain. Intervention logging adds minor time but does not prevent occurrence. Preventing constraint-violating interventions depends on operator discipline and supervision, a chronic vigilance burden. Training and supervision costs are ongoing but standard.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the containment response fails to initiate after the operator command
rows.plain: Make sure the system starts containing a leak or other breach when the operator gives the command. This limits the spread of hazardous material.
rows.imp_rat: A containment response that fails to initiate after operator command leaves an active breach uncontrolled, central to protective function (high relevance) with severe exposure/release consequences (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Interlocked control systems generally initiate the containment response reliably on command, though sensor or actuator faults occasionally cause a failure to start. Command-driven containment initiation is designed to trigger promptly with minimal delay. Operator command of the containment response is straightforward once trained, requiring little skill. Control interlocks and response hardware add moderate cost but are standard.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of items introduced into the zone without confirmed decontaminated status
rows.plain: Reduce the share of items brought into the protected area without confirmation that they have been cleaned to remove contamination. This helps preserve the area’s cleanliness.
rows.imp_rat: Items introduced without confirmed decontaminated status carry unknown contamination into the zone; relevant to CFJ and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Item-status control relies on procedural tracking that is error-prone, so unconfirmed items still enter at a meaningful rate. Confirming decontaminated status of introduced items adds transfer time. Tracking which items are decontaminated depends on operator discipline and procedures. Rework of improperly introduced items wastes resources.

rows.source_job: Decontaminate the isolator prior to disposal
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of hazardous material escaping from the isolator into the surrounding facility during decontamination
rows.plain: Prevent harmful material from escaping the sealed enclosure into the building while it is being cleaned before disposal.
rows.imp_rat: Hazardous material escaping into the facility during decontamination is a direct containment breach undermining the core job. Severity is severe as it can contaminate the surrounding facility and expose multiple personnel.
rows.sat_rat: Because the isolator itself is designed to contain, decontaminating in-situ with intact seals is highly reliable at preventing facility escape. Containment-preserving decon adds time but is a predictable step within the shutdown window. Maintaining negative pressure and integrity during decon relies on skilled operators and functioning controls. Running the isolator's own containment during decon uses energy and monitoring resources at moderate ongoing cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing isolator containment during mechanical repair
rows.plain: Keep the sealed enclosure secure while fixing mechanical problems, so harmful material does not escape or enter.
rows.imp_rat: Losing containment during mechanical repair is a peripheral maintenance-phase event rather than routine operation (relevance moderate-high); however, an uncontrolled breach during repair can release potent compounds and heavily contaminate the room, a severe outcome (severity high).
rows.sat_rat: There is no standardized engineering control that guarantees containment once the enclosure is physically opened, so outcomes depend on ad hoc containment measures and technique. Mechanical repairs often require breaking the enclosure and re-decontaminating afterward, extending downtime considerably. Maintaining containment during physical repair depends heavily on experienced technicians' judgement and improvised barriers, which varies by situation. Extended downtime and re-qualification after opening the isolator make repairs costly.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing isolator containment during an extended high-throughput campaign
rows.plain: Keep the sealed enclosure secure during a long period of high-volume work, so harmful material does not escape or enter.
rows.imp_rat: Losing containment during an extended high-throughput campaign is central because sustained operation is where seals and interfaces degrade. Severity is high due to prolonged, potentially unnoticed exposure across a long production run.
rows.sat_rat: Prolonged high-throughput operation accumulates glove fatigue and seal degradation, and containment loss over an extended campaign is a recognized cumulative risk. Extended campaigns run continuously, and containment monitoring keeps pace without pausing throughput. Sustained vigilance over long shifts fatigues operators, increasing chance of technique-driven containment loss. Long campaigns increase glove wear and consumable consumption, raising running cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of sterility-relevant conditions outside environmental monitoring detection capability in the controlled zone
rows.plain: Make sure the monitoring system can detect every condition that could threaten cleanliness in the controlled area. This helps identify problems before they affect sterile production.
rows.imp_rat: Sterility-relevant conditions outside monitoring detection capability create blind spots; relevant to CFJ assurance and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Conventional EM methods leave a known fraction of sterility-relevant conditions undetectable, a persistent methodological limitation. Some conditions are only detectable retrospectively, adding delay. Defining detection coverage requires specialized microbiological expertise. Expanding detection methods raises cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the deviation response is directed using incomplete facts about product impact and aseptic-zone status
rows.plain: Make sure the response to an unexpected problem is based on complete information about which products may be affected and whether the sterile work area is still protected. This helps people choose the right corrective action.
rows.imp_rat: Directing deviation response with incomplete facts about product impact and zone status can misdirect corrective action; relevant to CFJ decision quality and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: No integrated real-time picture exists, so overseers often act on partial information and outcomes vary with who is on shift. Assembling product-impact and zone-status facts under deviation pressure takes time, delaying directed response. Directing a good response depends on the overseer synthesizing fragmented data from multiple systems, a demanding expert judgement that is often the weak point. Decisions made on incomplete facts cause misdirected effort and wasted product, a recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time containment loss remains undetected during active processing
rows.plain: Detect as quickly as possible if hazardous material starts escaping during processing. This limits how long the escape can continue unnoticed.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected containment loss during active processing means exposure or contamination proceeds unchecked, central to the isolator's protective job (high relevance) and severe because harm accumulates before anyone reacts (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Redundant sensors make detection reliable for gross breaches, but slow or intermittent leaks can evade thresholds and linger undetected. Continuous differential-pressure and particle monitoring flag containment loss within seconds to minutes, keeping undetected duration short during active processing. Detection is largely instrument-driven so it does not lean heavily on operator expertise, though interpreting subtle drifts still needs judgement. Sensor arrays and continuous monitoring are standard fitted infrastructure, so detection carries no marginal cost per batch.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of loading incompatible or out-of-spec sterilant agent into the reservoir
rows.plain: Make sure the correct sterilizing chemical, in the required condition, is put into the supply tank. The wrong chemical or quality could prevent proper sterilization.
rows.imp_rat: Loading incompatible or out-of-spec sterilant undermines the decontamination cycle that is central to the isolator delivering an aseptic environment; severity is high because failed sterilization can release non-sterile or hazardous product and expose personnel, with potential recalls.
rows.sat_rat: Barcoding, dedicated single-agent supply chains and label checks make wrong-agent loading rare, though manual concentration errors remain possible. Checking sterilant lot, concentration and expiry before charging the reservoir is quick and built into the procedure. Reading the CoA and matching agent grade is a low-to-moderate skill task once trained. Loading wrong sterilant wastes the charge and any affected cycle, a contained cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of loss of containment while transitioning into the designated operating mode
rows.plain: Prevent the protected area or material from escaping while the system switches into its assigned operating state. This helps maintain protection during the changeover.
rows.imp_rat: Loss of containment during transition into operating mode is a critical transient window where the barrier can fail before production; directly tied to the CFJ with high severity as it can expose the process at startup.
rows.sat_rat: Interlocked mode changes reduce breaches, yet transitions remain a recognized higher-risk moment where containment loss recurs. Mode transitions run through timed automated sequences, so the transition window itself is short. Transitions still involve operator actions and confirmations where mistakes can breach containment. The sequencing logic is built in, but a transition breach can waste in-process material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that residual decontaminant exceeds the release limit before product separation
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that too much cleaning chemical remains before the product is separated from the equipment. This helps prevent the chemical from affecting the product.
rows.imp_rat: Residual decontaminant exceeding the release limit before separation chemically contaminates product; relevant to safe-zone CFJ and severe for patient safety.
rows.sat_rat: Catalytic aeration and inline sensors reliably bring H2O2 under limits, though sensor drift can occasionally allow residuals above the release threshold. Aeration phases are timed to drive residual below limits, adding predictable duration. Automated aeration endpoints reduce reliance on operator judgement about when residuals are safe. Extended aeration consumes energy and time but is a standard operating cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of deviation in critical chamber conditions from validated limits during sustained operation
rows.plain: Keep important conditions inside the processing chamber close to their approved safe ranges during long periods of high-volume production.
