rows.imp_rat: Deviation of critical chamber conditions from validated limits during sustained runs erodes the controlled environment the isolator exists to maintain; severe drift can compromise long production batches, though gradual deviations may be caught before catastrophic loss.
rows.sat_rat: Mature PID/environmental control keeps critical parameters near setpoint consistently during steady operation, with small residual drift the main gap. Continuous control acts in real time to hold chamber conditions steady, so deviations are corrected without meaningful delay. Sustained control is automated and needs little operator attention once validated setpoints are running. Tight control avoids wasted product from drift, and the regulating equipment is already capitalized.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of contact between aseptic-contact and non-aseptic surfaces during transfer
rows.plain: Have fewer occasions when surfaces that must stay germ-free touch surfaces that are not germ-free during moving or transferring materials. This lowers the chance of contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Contact between aseptic and non-aseptic surfaces during transfer transfers contamination; relevant to CFJ and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Transfer protocols reduce cross-contact but manual handling leaves recurring risk. Sequenced transfer handling adds modest time. Keeping aseptic and non-aseptic surfaces apart during transfer is technique-dependent. Contact errors can cause material loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that efficacy-affecting deviations remain unresolved at processing-zone release
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that problems affecting how well cleaning works are still unresolved when the processing area is declared ready. This helps prevent products from being made in an inadequately cleaned area.
rows.imp_rat: Efficacy-affecting deviations unresolved at release mean the zone's sterility assurance is uncertain at go-time; relevant to CFJ and severe since unresolved deviations can mask real contamination.
rows.sat_rat: Deviation-management systems exist but unresolved efficacy-relevant deviations still slip to release when significance is misjudged. Resolving efficacy-affecting deviations before release frequently delays the cycle while investigation proceeds. Judging whether a deviation affects efficacy demands scarce QA and validation expertise the executor cannot supply alone. Deviation investigation labor and potential batch delay are significant costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that sterile product or product-contact surfaces touch nonsterile surfaces during handling
rows.plain: Keep the germ-free product and surfaces that touch it from touching unclean surfaces during handling. This helps prevent the product from becoming contaminated.
rows.imp_rat: Sterile product contacting nonsterile surfaces is a direct violation of the aseptic containment the isolator exists to provide (relevance high); such contact compromises sterility and can render batches unusable or unsafe (severity high).
rows.sat_rat: Inadvertent contact between sterile product-contact surfaces and nonsterile ones during manipulation is a common aseptic-processing deviation only partly controlled by layout and technique. Careful handling to avoid contact adds no significant delay under normal pace. Preventing sterile-to-nonsterile contact demands spatial awareness and skilled manipulation in confined space. Any contact-driven rejects waste product and materials, a recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of residual hazardous material escaping from the isolator into the cleanroom during dismantling
rows.plain: Keep dangerous leftover material from escaping the enclosed machine into the cleanroom while it is taken apart. This protects the people and equipment nearby.
rows.imp_rat: Residual hazardous material escaping into the cleanroom during dismantling is a genuine end-of-life containment breach. Severity is high because dismantling disturbs accumulated contamination and can expose personnel and the cleanroom.
rows.sat_rat: Dismantling breaches the engineered containment, so escape of residues into the cleanroom is a real recurring risk that workarounds only partly control. Careful staged dismantling with local containment slows the work considerably. Once seals are broken, protection depends entirely on skilled crews using temporary enclosures and bagging techniques with high judgement. Temporary containment tents, HEPA units and extra labor for dismantling drive up cost noticeably.

rows.source_job: Define and document material transfer protocols
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the documented transfer sequence creates an open pathway between the isolator and surrounding environment
rows.plain: Make sure the written material-moving steps never create an uncovered route between the enclosed clean space and the outside air.
rows.imp_rat: A documented transfer sequence that creates an open pathway to the environment is a systemic procedural breach of containment (relevance high); an open pathway allows contamination ingress/egress with serious sterility and exposure consequences (severity high).
rows.sat_rat: Alpha-beta port and airlock interlock conventions make direct open pathways unlikely, though poorly documented or exception sequences remain a known deviation source. Reviewing and validating transfer sequences takes engineering time upfront but does not delay routine preparation. Designing an interlocked transfer sequence that never opens a direct pathway requires experienced process and containment engineers. Airlocks, RTPs and interlocks are costly hardware but standard to the isolator design.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing sterile containment at higher material throughput
rows.plain: Keep the product protected from germs and other contamination when handling batches of different sizes.
rows.imp_rat: Losing sterile containment at higher throughput ties directly to the isolator's job of sustaining sterility under real production loads (relevance high); loss of containment risks batch loss and contamination at scale (severity high).
rows.sat_rat: Sterile containment is well handled at moderate rates but higher throughput increases transfer frequency and correspondingly the chance of a sterility breach. Higher throughput compresses transfer cycles but validated procedures still keep pace. Maintaining sterile technique at higher speed strains operator attention and increases error likelihood. Faster throughput raises consumable turnover and cleaning frequency costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that unit-level environmental monitoring indicators conceal a location-specific or batch-specific detection failure
rows.plain: Lower the chance that overall environmental checks appear normal even though a particular place or batch was not properly checked for contamination. This helps reveal problems that broad results might hide.
rows.imp_rat: Unit-level monitoring indicators concealing a location- or batch-specific detection failure is a false-assurance gap; relevant to CFJ detection and severe due to masked contamination.
rows.sat_rat: Unit-level rollups can hide location- or batch-specific detection failures, and current practice only partially disaggregates this. Aggregated indicators are timely but obscure location detail. Recognizing masked location- or batch-specific failures requires expert data interrogation. Granular monitoring and data systems add cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of unauthorized departures from approved procedures during critical aseptic manipulations
rows.plain: Reduce how often workers make unauthorized changes to approved procedures during important sterile handling steps. This helps keep the process controlled and reduces contamination risk.
rows.imp_rat: Unauthorized departures from approved procedures during critical manipulations introduce contamination risk; relevant to CFJ and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Training, supervision, and SOPs reduce departures, but human variability keeps them a recurring occurrence during critical steps. Departures from procedure happen in real time and are not slowed by any timing control. Preventing unauthorized departures during critical manipulations depends entirely on operator discipline and training. Such departures can contaminate product, generating loss when they occur.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of introduced materials and components carrying contamination
rows.plain: Reduce the share of materials and parts brought into the work area that carry dirt or germs. This helps prevent the product from becoming contaminated.
rows.imp_rat: Introduced materials carrying contamination directly seed the closed zone; highly relevant to CFJ and severe consequences for product.
rows.sat_rat: Transfer decontamination reduces incoming contamination substantially but is not fully validated for every geometry. Incoming decontamination adds preparation time. Effective surface decon requires trained, thorough execution. Sporicides and controlled supply chains add cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of subsequent restoration activity recontaminating treated zone surfaces
rows.plain: Make sure putting the area back in order after processing does not put germs back onto surfaces that were treated. This helps keep the cleaned area safe for later use.
rows.imp_rat: Restoration activity recontaminating treated surfaces reverses the decontamination just achieved; relevant to CFJ and severe.
rows.sat_rat: Procedures address recontamination risk but post-decon manual work still occasionally reintroduces contamination. Sequencing restoration steps to avoid recontamination adds time. Ordering activities to protect treated surfaces requires trained judgment. Recontamination forces costly re-decontamination.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of transferring contaminants from glove surfaces to sterile materials during manipulation
rows.plain: Prevent germs or other unwanted material on the gloves from getting onto clean materials while they are being handled. This helps keep the materials safe to use.
rows.imp_rat: Transferring contaminants from glove surfaces to sterile materials is a core aseptic-integrity failure mode within the isolator (relevance high); it directly contaminates product with potential batch loss and patient risk (severity high).
rows.sat_rat: Glove surfaces are a recognized contamination vector to sterile materials and control relies on technique, so transfer of contaminants still occurs at low but nonzero rates. Glove decontamination between operations adds brief steps to the workflow. Avoiding glove-to-product cross-contamination depends on aseptic discipline and constant vigilance. Glove sanitization consumables and gloves add recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time from detection of an aseptic procedural deviation to restriction of the affected production activity
rows.plain: Reduce the time between noticing that a required procedure was not followed and limiting the affected production work. This helps prevent the problem from spreading or continuing.
rows.imp_rat: Delay from detecting a procedural deviation to restricting the activity extends production of at-risk product; relevant to CFJ containment and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Escalation procedures exist but response speed varies with shift staffing and communication paths, so restriction is not consistently rapid. Once a deviation is detected, restricting the affected activity depends on manual escalation that can lag by minutes to hours. Deciding what to restrict quickly requires experienced judgement about scope under time pressure. Delay in restriction means more product exposed, increasing loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of nonsterile intervention items introduced into the closed zone
rows.plain: Reduce the number of items that may carry germs brought into the protected work area during an intervention. This helps keep the area and product clean.
rows.imp_rat: Nonsterile intervention items introduced into the closed zone directly seed contamination; highly relevant to CFJ and severe.
rows.sat_rat: Kitting and material lists control introduced items well, though ad hoc additions still occur. Pre-staging only needed items saves in-process time. Deciding and limiting what enters requires planning discipline. Excess disposables are a minor cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the decontamination cycle is interrupted before completion
rows.plain: Prevent the cleaning process from stopping before it is finished. An incomplete cleaning cycle may leave harmful residue behind.
rows.imp_rat: An interrupted decontamination cycle may leave the zone incompletely decontaminated; relevant to reliable CFJ delivery and severe if uncaught.
rows.sat_rat: Power and utility conditioning reduce interruptions but unexpected trips and faults still abort cycles at a nontrivial rate. Interrupted cycles force full restarts, costing significant additional time. Restart-versus-resume decisions after interruption require experienced judgement. Aborted cycles waste decontaminant, utilities and occupancy time.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contaminants entering the decontaminated zone through connected process paths
rows.plain: Prevent germs and other unwanted material from entering the cleaned area through connected pipes or other paths. This helps keep the area and product protected.
rows.imp_rat: Contaminants entering through connected process paths bypass the visible barrier; relevant to CFJ and severe as a hidden ingress route.
rows.sat_rat: Connected process paths such as transfer ports and drains remain a recurring contamination-ingress concern that is managed but not eliminated. Verifying connected paths adds setup time. Understanding all connected process paths and their risk demands system expertise. Valve and barrier hardware to protect paths adds cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of processing-zone surfaces inadequately exposed because of internal obstructions
rows.plain: Make sure cleaning treatment reaches enough of the work-area surfaces, without equipment or other objects blocking those surfaces. This helps remove contamination from the whole area.
rows.imp_rat: Surfaces inadequately exposed due to internal obstructions leave un-decontaminated areas; relevant to CFJ and moderately-high severity as residual contamination can persist.
rows.sat_rat: Obstruction-driven exposure failures are a well-known recurring weakness with only procedural load-mapping as mitigation. Re-loading to correct obstructions before a cycle adds setup time. Recognizing which internal obstructions block exposure relies heavily on trained operator knowledge. Reworking loads and failed cycles wastes resources.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that directed actions create an additional contamination pathway within the isolator system
rows.plain: Ensure that corrective actions do not create another route for germs to enter the sealed work enclosure. This keeps the response from causing a new contamination problem.
rows.imp_rat: Directed actions creating an additional contamination pathway is a self-inflicted breach during response; relevant to CFJ and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Intervention procedures and aseptic technique training reduce this, but improvised corrective actions still sometimes introduce new risks. Assessing whether a directed action opens a new contamination path takes deliberate review time that is sometimes skipped under pressure. Foreseeing that an intervention could itself breach separation demands deep aseptic expertise and is a frequent judgement failure. A self-inflicted contamination pathway causes fresh loss and investigation cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration between contamination excursion onset and overseer awareness
rows.plain: Shorten the time between the start of a contamination problem and the supervisor becoming aware of it. Earlier awareness allows a faster response.
rows.imp_rat: Delay between contamination excursion onset and overseer awareness extends undetected at-risk production; relevant to CFJ response and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Non-viable alarms give fast signals but microbial confirmation lag means true contamination awareness is often delayed by days. Microbial contamination awareness is delayed by incubation, making onset-to-awareness duration inherently long. Interpreting emerging signals requires skilled reviewers. Rapid microbial methods that shorten delay carry higher cost and are not yet widespread.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of airflow disruption around exposed sterile product during intervention movements
rows.plain: Keep air movement around exposed germ-free product as steady as possible when moving or working nearby. This helps avoid carrying contamination onto the product.
rows.imp_rat: Airflow disruption around exposed product during intervention movements raises contamination probability; relevant to CFJ and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Smoke studies define safe movement patterns, but real-time compliance varies with the operator. Deliberate slow movements to protect airflow extend intervention time. Minimizing airflow disruption during movement is a skill-dependent aspect of aseptic technique. Little direct cost beyond training investment.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of critical fill-finish activity without environmental monitoring coverage
rows.plain: Shorten the time that important filling work continues without checks for germs and other unwanted contamination. This helps protect the product while it is being filled.
rows.imp_rat: Fill-finish activity without monitoring coverage leaves the most critical operation unwatched; relevant to CFJ detection and severe due to unmonitored product exposure.
rows.sat_rat: Continuous non-viable monitoring covers fill-finish reliably, though viable air sampling coverage may have brief gaps. Continuous monitoring covers most fill-finish duration, minimizing uncovered intervals. Coverage planning needs modest expertise once designed. Continuous coverage instrumentation adds cost but is standard.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of transferring material with unresolved aseptic status into the processing zone
rows.plain: Only material confirmed to be clean and safe for germ-free processing should enter the processing area. This helps prevent contamination of the product.
rows.imp_rat: Transferring material with unresolved aseptic status introduces uncertain contamination into the zone; highly relevant to CFJ and severe due to unknown risk on product.
rows.sat_rat: Transfer procedures and interlocks generally block unresolved-status material, though documentation gaps occasionally slip. Status verification before transfer adds a checkpoint delay. Confirming aseptic status requires procedural discipline and material knowledge. Rejected or reprocessed material is an occasional cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that decontaminant concentration remains outside its validated range during exposure
rows.plain: The amount of cleaning chemical in the air should return to its approved safe level as quickly as possible when it goes outside that level. This helps keep the cleaning process effective and controlled.
rows.imp_rat: Decontaminant concentration outside validated range during exposure directly affects kill efficacy; relevant to reliable decontamination and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Vapor generators with feedback control hold concentration reasonably, but distribution and sensor lag allow short out-of-range periods. Concentration excursions can extend exposure phases when the controller compensates. Maintaining vapor concentration is automated, reducing operator finesse required. Excess decontaminant use during excursions adds consumable cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time between excursion onset and operator recognition
rows.plain: Workers should notice an unusual change as soon as it begins. Faster recognition allows them to respond before the situation worsens.
rows.imp_rat: Time between excursion onset and operator recognition determines undetected product exposure; relevant to CFJ detection and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Continuous monitoring helps but slow-developing excursions are often recognized late. Detecting excursion onset quickly depends on monitoring latency and alarm response. Recognizing subtle onset requires attentive, experienced operators. Late recognition raises exposure cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of uncertainty about whether validated separation still holds while sterile product is exposed
rows.plain: Shorten the time spent unsure whether the barrier keeping sterile product separate is still working while the product is exposed. This helps reduce the chance that the product becomes contaminated.
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about whether validated separation still holds while product is exposed leaves risk undecided during the critical window; relevant to CFJ assurance and moderately-high severity.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of residual pharmaceutical material released beyond the isolator during removal
rows.plain: Keep leftover medicine from escaping the enclosed work area when it is removed. This protects people and the surrounding area from exposure.
rows.imp_rat: Residual pharmaceutical material released beyond the isolator during removal is important to the containment purpose (relevance high); released potent residues create cross-contamination and exposure hazards with serious consequences (severity high).
rows.sat_rat: Good decon plus containment keeps most residue in, but small quantities still routinely escape during physical removal. Thorough pre-removal cleaning to minimize releasable residue lengthens the removal step. Judging when surfaces are clean enough to move relies on operator experience and swab feedback. Extra cleaning cycles and waste handling add material and disposal cost.

rows.source_job: Integrate process equipment within isolator enclosure
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of damaging or leaving the containment boundary unsealed during equipment integration
rows.plain: Avoid damaging the enclosed work area and make sure every connection remains tightly sealed when equipment is added. This keeps the barrier protecting the product and workers intact.
rows.imp_rat: Damaging or leaving the containment boundary unsealed during equipment integration undermines the fundamental barrier before use (relevance high); an unsealed boundary silently defeats containment leading to contamination and exposure (severity high).
rows.sat_rat: Post-integration integrity re-tests catch many introduced breaches, but the act of integration itself remains an error-prone manual step. Careful equipment integration and re-verification of seals extends installation time modestly. Avoiding seal damage during through-wall equipment integration is highly dependent on the fitter's craftsmanship and care, making it a skill-limited error. Damaged seals and boundary rework during integration are cheap parts but can trigger costly re-qualification.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of pharmaceutical residues escaping into the facility during ventilation or drainage disconnection
rows.plain: Prevent drug-product residue from escaping into the building when air or liquid connections are disconnected.
rows.imp_rat: Residues escaping during ventilation or drainage disconnection is a maintenance-interface concern rather than routine operation (relevance moderate); uncontrolled release of potent residues into the facility carries serious contamination and exposure consequences (severity high).
rows.sat_rat: Ventilation and drainage connections are a chronic escape path with weak standard practice, so residue release here is a persistent problem. Disconnecting drains and HVAC under containment is slow and often improvised. Sealing and capping contaminated ductwork and drain lines demands specialist knowledge that is not always on hand. Capping, filtering and treating disconnected lines adds unplanned cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of an unsealed closed-zone access boundary during manipulation
rows.plain: Reduce the time that an opening into the protected enclosed area remains unsealed while work is being done. A shorter opening helps limit the chance of contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Duration of an unsealed access boundary during manipulation is proportional to breach exposure time; relevant to CFJ and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Fixed sealed access interfaces keep the boundary sealed during manipulation reliably by design. Sealed glove ports mean the boundary is never truly open, so unsealed duration is minimal. Little skill needed since access is through fixed sealed interfaces. Glove-port design cost is embedded in the isolator.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the selected response failing to control the excursion
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that the chosen action fails to bring an unexpected problem back under control. The response should effectively restore safe conditions.
rows.imp_rat: A selected response failing to control the excursion allows contamination to persist/spread; relevant to CFJ containment and severe consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Response effectiveness is variable because scenarios differ and playbooks are incomplete. Selecting an effective response takes decision time under pressure. Choosing a response that actually controls the excursion depends on experienced judgment. Ineffective responses waste effort and product.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of response activity compromising separation from the surrounding environment
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that actions taken during a problem allow outside air, dirt, or germs into the protected work area. This helps keep the area separated from its surroundings.
rows.imp_rat: Response activity compromising separation is a corrective action that itself breaches the barrier; relevant to CFJ and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Response procedures exist but improvised interventions during upsets frequently strain separation integrity. Response actions can be hurried, but restoring separation may take time. Executing a response without breaching separation demands experienced judgment under pressure. Poor response can escalate into costly contamination events.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved control, alarm, or interlock faults after supporting utilities return
rows.plain: Reduce the number of control, warning, or safety-device problems that remain unsolved after supporting services come back on. This helps the equipment return to safe operation.
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved control, alarm, or interlock faults after utility restoration are relevant because interlocks safeguard containment and sterility integrity; severity is high since latent faults can allow undetected breaches or unsafe restarts affecting both operators and product.
rows.sat_rat: Alarm-management systems flag faults, but ensuring none remain unresolved before restart still depends on manual review and recurringly misses some. Clearing residual faults after utilities return can extend restart time while each is investigated. Resolving lingering control, alarm, and interlock faults demands skilled troubleshooting judgement. Unresolved faults delay production and can force rework, adding cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of an unsealed zone boundary after material passage
rows.plain: Close the boundary of the clean zone as soon as possible after materials pass through. A shorter opening reduces the chance of contamination entering.
rows.imp_rat: Duration of an unsealed boundary after material passage extends breach exposure; relevant to CFJ and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Airlock interlocks reliably limit boundary-open duration after transfer. Interlocked doors reclose promptly after material passage, minimizing open time. Automated interlocks reduce reliance on operator timing. Interlock hardware adds cost but is standard.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of processing-zone locations failing decontamination acceptance criteria during the recovery cycle
rows.plain: Keep as many processing-area locations as possible within the required cleanliness limits during recovery. This helps ensure the area is properly cleaned before work resumes.
rows.imp_rat: Locations failing decontamination acceptance during recovery indicate residual contamination risk; relevant to CFJ readiness and moderately-high severity since it flags incomplete decontamination.
rows.sat_rat: Location-by-location acceptance is validated but a minority of hard-to-reach spots recurrently fail, so the failure percentage is controlled rather than eliminated. Recovery-cycle sampling and readout add turnaround time that is accepted as routine. Placement of indicators at worst-case locations still relies on validation expertise that operators depend on. Repeated indicator placement and analysis consume consumables and labor each cycle.

rows.source_job: Verify containment integrity of assembled isolator
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a breach in an installed glove or sleeve remains undetected before first use
rows.plain: Make sure any tear or hole in an installed glove or sleeve is found before the equipment is used. This helps keep harmful contamination from entering the enclosed work area.
rows.imp_rat: An undetected glove/sleeve breach before first use is central because it silently negates the barrier at the outset (relevance high); the undetected nature means contamination or exposure proceeds unnoticed across a whole campaign, a severe latent failure (severity high).
rows.sat_rat: Automated glove-leak testers and physical inspection catch most breaches, but small pinholes below tester sensitivity remain a recognized cause of undetected pre-use failures. Glove and sleeve integrity checks are quick and add little time before first use. Reliable detection of pinholes still depends on operator technique and diligence during visual and pressure inspection. Glove testers are inexpensive and gloves themselves are consumables budgeted routinely.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of pressure differential excursions beyond sterile limits during a run
rows.plain: Reduce how often air-pressure differences move outside the limits needed to keep the process sterile. Stable pressure helps protect the product from contamination during the run.
rows.imp_rat: Pressure differential excursions beyond sterile limits during a run threaten barrier protection; relevant to CFJ and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Differential pressure excursions beyond limits are rare with qualified control loops. Excursions are corrected quickly by automated control. Minimal operator skill needed for routine pressure regulation. Control hardware cost is standard.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time between excursion recognition and containment initiation
rows.plain: Start containing a problem as soon as it is recognized. Acting quickly can limit its effect on people, materials, and production.
rows.imp_rat: Time between excursion recognition and containment initiation determines how long contamination spreads; relevant to CFJ response and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Response procedures exist but time-to-containment varies widely by situation. Containment initiation speed after recognition depends on procedure clarity and operator readiness. Rapid, correct containment decisions require trained personnel. Delay increases product-at-risk cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of damaging gloves, seals, or other boundary components during restoration
rows.plain: Reduce the chance of tearing gloves, damaging seals, or harming other protective barriers while restoring the area. Keeping these barriers intact helps prevent contamination and protects workers.
rows.imp_rat: Damaging gloves/seals during restoration creates a new breach; relevant to CFJ and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Procedures reduce restoration damage but manual handling still causes occasional harm to boundary components. Careful restoration takes time but is not the main issue. Avoiding damage to gloves and seals during cleaning/restoration depends on operator care. Damaged components incur replacement cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of hazardous residue leaving the enclosure on removed materials or tools
rows.plain: Keep as much hazardous material as possible from leaving the enclosed area on removed parts or tools. This helps protect people and the surrounding work area.
rows.imp_rat: Hazardous residue leaving on removed materials or tools is central to preventing spread of potent compounds outside containment (relevance high); such carryover contaminates downstream areas and exposes personnel, a severe outcome (severity high).
rows.sat_rat: Wash-down and wipe procedures reduce carried residue but surface contamination on removed tools remains variable and hard to guarantee to zero. Surface wipe-down and decontamination of removed items adds handling time before transfer out. Effective decontamination of removed materials requires disciplined technique and correct agent selection. Cleaning agents, wipes and validation consume recurring resources.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of closed-zone pressure deviation from required conditions during manipulation
rows.plain: Keep the air pressure in the enclosed work area close to the required level while someone works inside or handles materials. Stable pressure helps protect the area from unwanted air movement or contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Closed-zone pressure deviation during manipulation weakens the outward-protective barrier; relevant to CFJ and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Closed-loop pressure control holds differentials tightly during manipulation, a mature capability. Pressure control responds fast to disturbances. Automated pressure control reduces skill dependence. Control systems are embedded capital cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of pressure reversal between the decontaminated zone and its surroundings
rows.plain: Keep the period of backward pressure between the cleaned area and its surroundings as short as possible. This helps protect the cleaned area from losing its protection.
rows.imp_rat: Pressure reversal between the decontaminated zone and surroundings draws in contamination; relevant to CFJ barrier and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Active differential-pressure control keeps reversals short and infrequent. Pressure reversals are brief and auto-corrected, costing little time. Automated pressure control removes operator involvement. Control system operation is a low incremental cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the installed glove or sleeve assembly fails containment integrity verification
rows.plain: Make sure the replacement glove or sleeve assembly keeps the enclosed area sealed when it is checked.
rows.imp_rat: An installed glove/sleeve that fails integrity verification signals a compromised barrier; catching it is important though the failed verification here at least surfaces the problem, unlike a false pass, limiting downstream severity somewhat.
rows.sat_rat: Immediate post-install pressure testing reliably catches improperly seated or defective assemblies before use. Post-installation integrity verification via glove leak testing is fast. Automated verification reduces skill dependence for confirming a proper install. Test consumables and occasional re-installs are modest costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of critical controlled-zone locations not represented by environmental monitoring
rows.plain: More of the important locations in controlled clean areas should be included in regular checks of the surrounding conditions. This helps reveal contamination risks where they could occur.
rows.imp_rat: Critical locations not represented by monitoring create undetected contamination blind spots; relevant to CFJ assurance and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Risk-based EM site selection covers designated critical points well, but some critical locations remain unrepresented due to access constraints. Location sampling is scheduled and timely. Risk-based site selection requires expert judgement about critical locations. Adding monitoring locations increases sampling cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of operator exposure to contaminated or hazardous residue during process waste discharge
rows.plain: Protect the worker from touching or breathing in dangerous or contaminated waste while it is being emptied.
rows.imp_rat: Exposure to hazardous residue during waste discharge is a recurring, routine containment risk directly tied to the job. Severity is high because waste streams carry concentrated hazardous material and discharge points are common breach locations.
rows.sat_rat: Bag-out systems and continuous liners are established but waste discharge remains an exposure-prone step where residue contact still occurs with meaningful frequency. Waste discharge through bag-out or WIP ports is a slower, deliberate step in the workflow. Continuous-liner bag-out and waste handling require careful technique to avoid residue exposure. Continuous liners, waste containers and disposal consumables are recurring cost items.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of uncertainty about the excursion source, affected zone, and potential product exposure
rows.plain: Quickly determine what caused an unexpected problem, which area it affected, and whether the product may have been exposed to harm. Clear answers support the right response.
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about excursion source, affected zone, and product exposure hampers correct response; relevant to CFJ decision quality and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Root-cause and exposure attribution remain highly uncertain and inconsistent in practice. Diagnosing source and scope takes investigation time. Resolving uncertainty about source, affected zone and product exposure demands considerable expertise and often lacks tooling. Uncertainty drives conservative, costly product dispositions.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that the processing-zone pressure differential remains outside its validated range during the cycle
rows.plain: Keep the pressure difference between the processing area and its surroundings within the allowed range throughout the run. This helps keep the process under control.
rows.imp_rat: Processing-zone pressure differential outside validated range during the cycle threatens barrier integrity; relevant to CFJ and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Pressure-differential control is mature and excursions are typically brief and self-correcting. Pressure differential recovers quickly under active control, adding little time. Differential pressure is automatically regulated, requiring minimal operator skill. Blower and control operation is a standard low incremental cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of aseptic-zone interventions remaining unassessed before batch authorization
rows.plain: Review all actions taken in the sterile area before approving the production lot. This helps ensure no important activity is left unchecked.
rows.imp_rat: Unassessed interventions before batch authorization leave contamination risk unevaluated at the gate; relevant to CFJ assurance and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Intervention logs and mandatory assessment are established, but completeness depends on accurate logging that is sometimes lacking. Assessing every intervention before authorization adds review time proportional to intervention count. Judging whether each intervention preserved asepsis requires trained review of technique and timing. An unassessed high-risk intervention can force batch rejection, driving the review discipline.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of decontamination runs that fail approved acceptance criteria
rows.plain: Reduce the number of cleaning and treatment cycles that fail the required checks. This helps ensure the area or equipment has been properly treated.
rows.imp_rat: Decontamination runs failing acceptance criteria mean the zone was not properly prepared; relevant to CFJ and severe if allowed to proceed.
rows.sat_rat: Once validated, automated decon cycles pass acceptance criteria consistently, with occasional failures from load or sensor faults. Routine decon runs complete on defined cycle times. Routine execution is automated, requiring limited operator skill. Failed runs waste time and consumables but are infrequent.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that environmental monitoring activity compromises separation between product and the surrounding environment
rows.plain: Make sure checking the surrounding conditions does not weaken the separation between the product and the outside environment. This helps prevent outside contamination from reaching the product.
rows.imp_rat: Environmental monitoring activity that compromises separation is a self-inflicted breach; relevant to CFJ and moderately-high severity since monitoring should protect not harm.
rows.sat_rat: Established aseptic EM procedures minimize monitoring-induced contamination, though the sampling act itself introduces intrinsic residual risk. EM sampling adds handling time inside the zone but is scheduled. Performing monitoring without introducing contamination itself relies on careful technique. Monitoring media and sampling devices are standard consumables.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician exposure to hazardous residue while accessing the enclosure boundary
rows.plain: Prevent the technician from touching dangerous residue while checking whether the enclosure keeps air from leaking. This helps protect the technician during the test.
rows.imp_rat: Technician exposure to hazardous residue while accessing the enclosure boundary is a common maintenance-phase risk relevant to worker safety (moderate-high relevance) with real toxic-exposure consequences (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Pre-access decontamination is standard, but breaching the containment boundary inherently exposes surfaces with residual hazard, so protection depends on verification quality that varies. Accessing the enclosure boundary usually follows a decontamination cycle, adding time before opening. Judging when the boundary is safe to open and doing so without exposure relies on technician experience and discipline. PPE, decontamination agents and downtime for access are recurring costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a processing-zone containment breach during the decontamination cycle
rows.plain: Make it very unlikely that contaminated material escapes from the controlled work area during the cleaning cycle. This helps protect people, products, and nearby areas.
rows.imp_rat: A containment breach during the decontamination cycle exposes the zone mid-treatment; relevant to CFJ and severe as it disrupts decontamination integrity.
rows.sat_rat: Sealed isolator boundaries with pressure monitoring make containment breaches during decontamination rare. Breach detection is immediate via pressure alarms, avoiding wasted time. Integrity is maintained by design rather than operator skill during the cycle. Isolator construction cost delivers durable containment.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of an out-of-limit internal condition remaining unrecognized during a run
rows.plain: Shorten the time that a condition outside the allowed range goes unnoticed during a production run. Finding it quickly helps protect the product from becoming unsafe.
rows.imp_rat: An out-of-limit internal condition unrecognized during a run extends undetected product-at-risk exposure; relevant to CFJ detection and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Continuous sensors and alarms shorten unrecognized duration for most parameters. The need is shortening how long an out-of-limit condition goes unrecognized, aided by continuous monitoring. Recognizing subtle out-of-limit conditions requires attention. Prolonged out-of-limit exposure raises risk cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the processing-zone boundary is unsealed at cycle initiation
rows.plain: Make sure the boundary around the processing area is fully sealed before the cleaning and germ-removal cycle begins. An unsealed boundary could allow contamination to enter.
rows.imp_rat: An unsealed boundary at cycle initiation means the zone starts compromised, undermining the cycle; relevant to CFJ and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Door and boundary interlocks reliably prevent cycle start with an unsealed boundary. Seal-status interlocks prevent initiation without added operator time. Automated door and seal interlocks remove reliance on operator vigilance. Interlock hardware is a standard built-in cost.

rows.source_job: Decontaminate the isolator prior to disposal
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician exposure to hazardous residues or decontaminating agents during isolator decontamination
rows.plain: The technician should be able to clean the sealed enclosure before it is discarded without touching harmful leftovers or cleaning chemicals. This helps protect the technician from injury or illness.
rows.imp_rat: Technician exposure to residues or decontaminating agents during decontamination is closely tied to the containment job's protective mandate. Severity is high because both toxic residues and aggressive decontaminants (e.g., vaporized H2O2) pose real health risks.
rows.sat_rat: Established VHP/chemical decon with residual monitoring is well proven, so technician exposure to residues or agents is uncommon when procedures are followed. Decontamination cycles and aeration take defined hours that are built into planning without much variability. Handling agents like VHP or oxidizers safely requires trained technicians who understand gas-out and neutralization steps. Decontaminant agents, monitoring and PPE add recurring cost that is a normal part of decommissioning budgets.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that enclosure conditions leave qualified limits after a setpoint adjustment
rows.plain: After a setting is changed, the conditions inside the enclosure should remain within the approved safe range. This helps keep the process working as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Setpoint adjustments that push enclosure conditions outside qualified limits reintroduce excursion risk during a controllable operation; central to maintaining validated state, with severity moderated because adjustments are typically monitored and reversible.
rows.sat_rat: Change-control and interlocks reduce bad setpoint moves, but transient excursions right after adjustments still occur with some regularity. After a setpoint change the system re-stabilizes within a defined settling period, so exposure to out-of-limit states is time-bounded. Setpoint adjustments require operator judgement about acceptable changes, introducing skill-dependent risk. Control hardware absorbs the adjustment without added cost unless an excursion forces a product hold.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a cycle phase advances before its validated parameter criteria are satisfied
rows.plain: Do not let one stage of a process move forward until its required settings have actually been reached. This helps keep the process consistent and safe.
rows.imp_rat: A cycle phase advancing before parameter criteria are met risks incomplete decontamination; relevant to reliable cycle assurance and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe interlocks reliably block premature phase advance, a well-established feature of modern isolator controls. Phase-gating logic advances only on criteria, adding no avoidable operator time. Interlocked phase transitions remove the need for operator judgement about when to advance. Recipe programming and validation cost is a one-time fixed investment.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required processing-zone surfaces obstructed by positioned items
rows.plain: Keep required work-area surfaces clear of items that block cleaning or treatment. This helps the cleaning process reach all the necessary surfaces.
rows.imp_rat: Required surfaces obstructed by positioned items leave un-decontaminated zones; relevant to CFJ and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Load maps help but positioning errors still occur, leaving surfaces obstructed at a meaningful rate. Arranging items to avoid obstruction adds load-preparation time. Correct item positioning to avoid shadowing depends on operator training and diligence with limited automated checking. Poor positioning causing reruns wastes decontaminant and time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of airborne particle excursions above validated limits inside the enclosed zone
rows.plain: Reduce how often the amount of airborne particles rises above the allowed level inside the enclosed work area. This helps keep the area clean and protect the product.
rows.imp_rat: Airborne particle excursions above validated limits signal potential contamination and airflow issues; relevant to CFJ and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Continuous non-viable particle monitoring reliably detects airborne excursions in real time, a mature and well-served capability in isolators. Continuous particle counters give immediate excursion signals. Alarm response is procedurally defined requiring modest interpretation. Continuous particle monitoring instruments are standard capital cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of an unisolated utility connection releasing hazardous energy during dismantling
rows.plain: Prevent an un disconnected utility line from releasing stored pressure, electricity, or other dangerous energy while equipment is taken apart. This protects the people doing the dismantling.
rows.imp_rat: An unisolated utility releasing hazardous energy during dismantling is peripheral to routine processing but central to safe decommissioning (moderate-high relevance); an energy release can cause serious injury (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Mature energy-isolation and LOTO practice makes uncontrolled hazardous-energy release during dismantling rare. Lockout/tagout and isolation verification take modest time that is standard in any dismantling job. LOTO is a well-trained, widely understood competency among maintenance crews. Isolation procedures cost little beyond routine labor and tags.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of sterilant leakage from the reservoir after replenishment
rows.plain: Prevent the disinfecting chemical from leaking out of its container after it is refilled. This protects people, equipment, and the surrounding area.
rows.imp_rat: Sterilant leakage from the reservoir after replenishment introduces hazardous chemical exposure and can compromise decontamination dosing; relevant to safe operation with high severity given the toxicity of sterilants like hydrogen peroxide vapor.
rows.sat_rat: Sealed connectors, O-rings and post-fill leak checks make leaks uncommon under standard practice, though a residual rate of missed reseating errors persists. Replenishment and reservoir reconnection add modest time to a service visit but rarely delay the maintenance window. Correct reservoir sealing depends on a trained technician following the OEM procedure, and torque/seating errors still occur with less experienced staff. Leak-related spills waste sterilant and can force decontamination reruns, but the material cost per event is limited.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of operator exposure to hazardous product residues or decontamination agents during interior cleaning and decontamination
rows.plain: Protect workers from dangerous leftover product or cleaning chemicals while they clean the inside of the enclosed work area. This reduces the chance of harmful exposure.
rows.imp_rat: Operator exposure to product residues or decontamination agents during interior cleaning is central to protecting personnel who routinely perform this task. Severity is high given repeated close contact with both hazardous residues and caustic agents.
rows.sat_rat: Established pre-cleaning decontamination and glove-port access substantially reduce exposure, but manual wipe-down and aggressive agents leave a recurring residual exposure risk. Automated vapor-phase decontamination cycles run before manual cleaning add substantial but scheduled cycle time. Interior cleaning through gloveports or via full entry demands careful, trained technique and disciplined agent handling to avoid direct contact. Decontamination agents, PPE and downtime are meaningful recurring costs of every interior clean.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time between operator recognition of the boundary breach and containment response activation
rows.plain: Start containing the breach as soon as the operator notices it. A faster response can limit the spread or effects of the problem.
rows.imp_rat: Delay between recognizing a boundary breach and activating containment prolongs uncontrolled release; timeliness is important to limiting the breach (high relevance) and longer delay proportionally increases exposure severity (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Automated activation is fairly consistent, though the manual recognition step means response times vary between shifts and operators. Modern isolators use automated pressure and alarm systems that trigger containment responses within seconds of a detected breach, but operator recognition and manual escalation still introduce lag. Fast response depends on well-trained operators recognizing what an alarm means and reacting decisively, so skill gaps still slow the loop. The monitoring and interlock hardware needed for rapid activation is already embedded in qualified isolators, so no extra spend is incurred per event.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of zone pressure differential deviations from validated limits
rows.plain: Shorten the time that the air-pressure difference between work areas is outside its allowed range. This helps prevent unwanted material from moving between areas.
rows.imp_rat: Zone pressure differential deviations threaten the barrier that keeps contamination out; relevant to CFJ and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Automated differential-pressure control with alarms keeps deviations brief and consistent, a well-established reliable function. Automated pressure control and alarms shorten deviation duration by triggering rapid correction. Control loops handle most response with minimal operator skill needed. Pressure control instrumentation is a standard designed-in cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of aseptic-state alarms or deviations unresolved at authorization
rows.plain: Resolve all alarms and problems related to keeping the work germ-free before approving the production run. This helps ensure the run is ready to proceed.
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved aseptic-state alarms/deviations at authorization means known concerns are ignored at the gate; relevant to CFJ assurance and severe.
rows.sat_rat: Alarm-management and open-item tracking are standard, so unresolved items at authorization are uncommon though not eliminated. Alarms and deviations must be closed before sign-off, which extends the authorization timeline. Deciding an alarm is truly resolved rather than merely silenced needs qualified judgement. Carrying unresolved alarms into production risks batch loss, so closure is enforced despite the effort.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of operator exposure to hazardous material while activating the containment response
rows.plain: Protect operators from dangerous material while they start the response to contain a breach. They should be able to act without being exposed.
rows.imp_rat: Operator exposure while activating the containment response is relevant but relates to an abnormal/emergency mode rather than routine containment, giving moderate relevance. Severity is high because exposure during a fault condition typically involves already-escaping hazardous material.
rows.sat_rat: Containment-response procedures for breaches are less standardized than routine operation, so exposure during activation is only partially mitigated and varies by facility. Activating a containment response during an upset takes time during which exposure risk persists. Operators must recognize the upset and execute the correct response under stress, demanding judgement and drilled training. Emergency response provisions add modest cost but spill kits and PPE are inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of unintended access or transfer opening events before product separation
rows.plain: Have fewer accidental openings or transfers between areas before the medicine has been separated from the surrounding area. This helps keep protected areas from being exposed.
rows.imp_rat: Unintended access/transfer opening events before separation create breach windows; relevant to CFJ and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Interlocks and alarms make unintended opening events before separation uncommon but not impossible. Access-opening events are logged automatically without added time. Operators must remember not to open unintended access, a vigilance dependence. Interlocks limiting openings are built into design cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the incorrect containment zone is activated after the breach
rows.plain: Make sure the response activates the correct area for containing a leak or breach. This helps keep the hazard from spreading to the wrong place.
rows.imp_rat: Activating the wrong containment zone after a breach means the response fails to contain the actual hazard; highly consequential when it occurs since the breach remains unaddressed, but conditional on a breach event already happening.
rows.sat_rat: Predefined zone mapping usually activates the right containment area, though misconfiguration or overlapping zones can occasionally misdirect the response. Zone activation is triggered near-instantly by the control logic once a breach is classified. Correct zone selection is programmed into interlock logic rather than left to operator choice, lowering skill dependence. Zoning logic is part of the fixed control system, so correct activation adds no marginal cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that protective-control testing compromises isolator containment
rows.plain: Make sure testing the safety controls does not break the sealed barrier that keeps harmful material inside. This protects people and the surrounding area during testing.
rows.imp_rat: Testing that itself breaches containment introduces contamination during a routine assurance activity; relevance is high because it corrupts the very safety verification, though such events are bounded to test windows and often detectable.
rows.sat_rat: Most protective-control tests are designed to run without compromising the barrier, so containment loss during testing is uncommon under standard procedure. Control testing is normally done without breaching containment and adds little time. Following validated test sequences requires moderate skill to avoid inadvertently opening the barrier. Minimal consumables make this a low-cost activity.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of uncontrolled internal conditions after loss of a required utility
rows.plain: Spend less time in uncontrolled conditions after an essential service, such as power or air supply, stops working. This helps keep the medicine and work area protected from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Uncontrolled internal conditions after utility loss extends the compromised window; relevant to CFJ and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Backup power/HVAC and alarms limit uncontrolled duration but recovery is not instantaneous. Utility loss recovery time drives how long conditions stay uncontrolled. Managing utility-loss response requires trained operators. Backup utilities and downtime carry cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of spatial variation in critical cycle conditions across the processing zone
rows.plain: Keep important treatment conditions as even as possible throughout the processing area. This helps ensure that all parts of the material receive the intended treatment.
rows.imp_rat: Spatial variation in critical cycle conditions can leave zones under-decontaminated; relevant to uniform CFJ delivery and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Spatial non-uniformity remains a persistent challenge; cold and shadowed spots recur despite fan-assisted distribution. Achieving spatial uniformity may require longer exposure to cover cold spots. Predicting and mitigating spatial variation demands considerable validation and CFD-style expertise. Additional distribution hardware and extended cycles add cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that aseptic-zone conditions leave established bounds during the first post-resumption batch
rows.plain: Prevent conditions in the clean work area from going outside their accepted limits during the first production batch after a restart. This helps show that production is safe to resume.
rows.imp_rat: Conditions leaving established bounds during the first post-resumption batch is a high-risk restart window; relevant to CFJ and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Heightened monitoring after resumption is standard, yet conditions still occasionally drift during the vulnerable first batch. The first post-resumption batch is monitored continuously so excursions are seen quickly, but not prevented in advance. Judging whether recovered conditions will hold through the first batch requires expert interpretation of trends. An early excursion loses the batch and re-triggers recovery, a notable recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of maintenance technicians being exposed to hazardous residues retained by exhausted filtration media
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that maintenance workers come into contact with harmful substances trapped in used air filters. This helps protect them while they remove and replace the filters.
rows.imp_rat: Maintenance technician exposure to residues in exhausted filter media is a well-known containment hazard, central to protecting service personnel. Severity is high due to concentrated hazardous accumulation, though it affects a narrower maintenance window.
rows.sat_rat: Combining BIBO change-out with pre-decon reliably limits technician exposure, though the retained residue on spent media remains an inherently hazardous variable. Pre-decontamination cycles plus donning PPE add time before filters can be safely handled, but this is a routine planned duration. Recognizing residual-hazard risk and executing safe-change with full PPE requires trained, attentive technicians familiar with the specific compound. Respiratory protection, suits and decontamination agents are recurring costs that are nonetheless standard for this task.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of product or cleaning residue on processing-zone surfaces before decontamination
rows.plain: Remove as much product and cleaning residue as possible from work surfaces before the area is cleaned and made safe. This helps the cleaning step work effectively.
rows.imp_rat: Product/cleaning residue on surfaces before decontamination can shield organisms and reduce efficacy; relevant to CFJ and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Residue on surfaces before decontamination is inconsistently controlled and depends on operator diligence with limited verification. Cleaning and drying before decontamination add appreciable time. Adequate residue removal depends heavily on manual cleaning skill and thoroughness. Cleaning agents, wipes and labor are recurring costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which validation conditions differ from the most challenging permitted isolator conditions
rows.plain: Make the testing conditions match the most difficult allowed operating conditions as closely as possible. This helps show whether the process can keep the product free of germs in challenging situations.
rows.imp_rat: Validation conditions differing from the most challenging permitted conditions means worst-case is unproven; relevant to CFJ assurance and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Worst-case validation is standard doctrine, but truly bounding every permitted condition is difficult, leaving residual gaps between validated and real worst cases. Worst-case study design extends validation timelines. Identifying and reproducing the most challenging permitted conditions requires seasoned validation judgement and is error-prone. Worst-case load testing consumes extra resources.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of incorrectly assembled product-path connections inside the processing zone
rows.plain: Reduce the number of connections for the product’s flow path that are put together incorrectly inside the work area. Correct connections help prevent leaks, blockages, or contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrectly assembled product-path connections is an assembly defect that can introduce leaks/contamination; relevant to CFJ and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Sterile connectors and procedures reduce misassembly, but manual glove work still yields occasional errors. Connection assembly through gloves is slow but the error is the issue. Correct product-path connections inside gloves require dexterity and care. Misconnections can scrap batches.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician exposure to hazardous residues while servicing air-handling components
rows.plain: Protect the technician from dangerous leftover material while working on the parts that move or control air. This reduces the chance of harmful exposure during servicing.
rows.imp_rat: Exposure to hazardous residues while servicing air-handling components is a maintenance-specific risk peripheral to routine operation (moderate relevance) but carries serious toxic-exposure consequences when it happens (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Decontamination reaches primary surfaces reliably but ductwork and plenum internals can retain residues that pre-cleaning does not fully address, leaving a recurring exposure risk. Air-handling service often requires full decontamination and access, extending the maintenance window. Servicing fans, ducts and plenums with residual hazard demands experienced technicians and careful PPE discipline. PPE, decontamination and downtime for HVAC access are notable recurring costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of zone conditions leaving acceptance limits between confirmation and step initiation
rows.plain: Make sure conditions in the work area stay within the allowed range after they are checked and before the next step starts. This prevents work from beginning under unsuitable conditions.
rows.imp_rat: Zone conditions leaving acceptance limits between confirmation and step initiation means a confirmed-good state degrades before use; relevant to CFJ and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Continuous monitoring means conditions rarely leave limits between confirmation and start, but transient drift is possible. Minimizing the gap between confirmation and step start reduces drift window. Timing steps to confirmed conditions requires procedural discipline. Drift-caused reruns cost time and product.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of monitoring observations collected under conditions unrepresentative of critical fill-finish activity
rows.plain: Make sure monitoring checks are carried out under conditions that truly represent the important filling and finishing work. This makes the results more trustworthy for judging whether the work area is clean and safe.
rows.imp_rat: Monitoring observations under unrepresentative conditions produce misleading assurance about the critical operation; relevant to CFJ detection validity and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Guidance requires monitoring under dynamic conditions and practice mostly complies, but some observations still occur under unrepresentative states. Aligning monitoring timing to dynamic operations is schedulable. Designing representative dynamic sampling requires validation expertise. Dynamic-condition monitoring adds modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing contaminants into the isolator during air-handling service
rows.plain: Prevent dirt or other unwanted material from entering the sealed work area while its air-handling parts are being serviced.
rows.imp_rat: Introducing contaminants during air-handling service risks sterility of subsequent operation, relevant since air handling defines the clean environment (moderate-high relevance), though effects are usually caught by revalidation before production (moderate-high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Opening air-handling components inherently exposes the system to ingress, and there is no strong engineering control to guarantee cleanliness during service, so outcomes vary with practice. Re-establishing clean conditions and requalifying after air-handling service adds substantial time. Preventing contamination ingress during HVAC service depends heavily on technician aseptic discipline and controlled work practices. Post-service cleaning, filters and requalification are meaningful recurring costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contaminants escaping the isolator during removal and disposal of exhausted filtration media
rows.plain: Keep harmful particles from escaping the enclosed work area while used air-filter material is removed and discarded.
rows.imp_rat: Contaminants escaping during removal/disposal of exhausted filters is a recognized containment failure mode, so relevance is high though it occurs at end-of-life rather than routine operation. Severity is substantial given accumulated hazardous load in spent media.
rows.sat_rat: Safe-change bag-in/bag-out technology is a mature, widely deployed engineering control that reliably contains filter media, though bag tears or technique lapses occasionally breach containment. Bag-in/bag-out (BIBO) filter housings and safe-change procedures are established practice that take moderate time but are well-rehearsed. Trained maintenance technicians follow validated safe-change SOPs, but proper bag manipulation still demands careful manual technique and vigilance. BIBO housings, disposable bags and PPE are recurring consumables but are a standard, budgeted part of isolator upkeep.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of sterile product exposure during required intervention manipulations
rows.plain: Keep the sterile product exposed for as little time as possible when a worker must interrupt the normal process. This lowers the chance of contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Duration of sterile product exposure during interventions correlates with contamination probability; relevant to CFJ and moderately-high severity as prolonged exposure raises risk.
rows.sat_rat: Procedures cap allowable exposure but actual duration varies operator to operator. Intervention duration depends heavily on operator dexterity through gloves, which is inherently slower than open handling. Minimizing exposure time under manipulation demands practiced, efficient technique. No major consumable cost, but slow interventions reduce throughput.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of humidity excursions outside the validated range during conditioning and exposure
rows.plain: Prevent moisture levels from going outside the approved range while materials are being prepared and exposed. This helps keep the process conditions consistent and suitable.
rows.imp_rat: Humidity excursions during conditioning/exposure can impair decontaminant efficacy and condensation control; relevant to reliable cycle and moderate-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Humidity is a common source of excursions in H2O2 cycles and is only moderately well controlled across varied loads. Humidity settling can delay conditioning progression. Humidity control during conditioning is automated but sensitive to load, requiring some setup expertise. Conditioning consumes utilities and time when humidity is hard to stabilize.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of credible intended-use conditions absent from qualification evidence
rows.plain: Make sure the qualification evidence covers all realistic ways the product is expected to be used. This helps show that it will work safely in the situations people may actually encounter.
rows.imp_rat: Credible intended-use conditions absent from qualification evidence leaves scenarios unproven; relevant to CFJ assurance and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Risk assessments cover common conditions but credible edge conditions are routinely omitted, leaving noticeable gaps. Enumerating all credible use conditions extends planning, and completeness is hard to bound. Anticipating the full set of credible use conditions leans heavily on experienced risk assessors. Missing conditions surface as costly late findings or field issues.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of particulate or chemical residue carried into the processing zone during transfer
rows.plain: Keep particles and chemical residue from being carried into the work area during a transfer. This helps protect the material being processed from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Particulate/chemical residue carried in during transfer adds contamination burden; relevant to CFJ and moderate severity depending on quantity.
rows.sat_rat: Cleaning practices limit particulate and chemical carry-in but residue control is imperfect. Wipe-down and airlock dwell add time before entry. Residue removal quality depends on operator thoroughness. Wipes and cleaning agents are recurring consumables.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of the enclosure boundary omitted from the pre-use integrity check
rows.plain: Check all of the enclosure’s edges before use so no part of it is missed. This helps confirm that it can safely contain what is inside.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting portions of the boundary from the pre-use integrity check is relevant because gaps in verification allow undetected breaches, but it is a test-coverage metric rather than a containment failure itself. Severity is notable since an unchecked region can hide a real leak that later exposes operators.
rows.sat_rat: Global pressure tests inherently exercise the full boundary, but localized methods and complex geometries mean some boundary sections are routinely under-checked with no systematic assurance of complete coverage. Ensuring every boundary segment is exercised takes extra planning time but is not a schedule driver. Whether the whole boundary is covered depends heavily on the engineer's understanding of the enclosure's geometry, making omissions an experience-dependent error. Comprehensive coverage costs little beyond additional test-point setup time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of particulate contamination remaining in the enclosed zone after restoration
rows.plain: As little dust or other small-particle contamination as possible should remain in the enclosed area after it has been restored. This helps make the area clean and ready for use.
rows.imp_rat: Particulate contamination remaining after restoration adds particle burden to product exposure; relevant to CFJ and moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Cleaning plus filtered airflow keeps residual particulate low, though not perfectly. Cleaning and airflow purge take defined time. Thorough particulate removal depends on operator technique. Cleaning consumables add cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in the proposed isolator-zone boundary and its separation functions
rows.plain: The proposed boundary around the enclosed work area and the ways it keeps areas separate should be clearly defined. This makes it easier to find rule violations or missing protections.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in the proposed boundary and separation functions is a definition/scoping clarity issue; relevant to defining the CFJ but severity moderate as a specification matter.
rows.sat_rat: Proposals commonly describe zones loosely, so residual ambiguity about the boundary persists into qualification. Clarifying boundary and separation-function ambiguity requires iterative vendor exchanges that stretch the timeline. Pinpointing where a boundary definition is ambiguous takes deep aseptic-design literacy that is unevenly available. Ambiguity resolution is cheap on paper but costly if it surfaces as a design flaw after commitment.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation from validated starting conditions in the processing zone
rows.plain: Bring the work area back as close as possible to its approved starting condition before cleaning or disinfection begins. This makes the process predictable and effective.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation from validated starting conditions can invalidate the cycle from the outset; relevant to reliable CFJ and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-cycle interlocks catch many deviations from validated starting conditions, though not all parameters are automatically enforced. Verifying starting conditions adds a brief pre-cycle check. Start-condition verification is partly automated but still depends on operator setup discipline. Correcting starting conditions before initiation avoids costly failed cycles.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of critical-utility interruption during the cycle
rows.plain: Shorten interruptions to essential services, such as power, air, or water, while the process is running. This helps keep the process within its required conditions.
rows.imp_rat: Critical-utility interruption during the cycle can halt or degrade decontamination and barrier maintenance; relevant to CFJ reliability and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Facility utility redundancy makes critical interruptions during a cycle uncommon in qualified sites. Utility interruptions directly halt or lengthen the cycle. Little operator skill can prevent utility loss during the cycle. Backup utilities and UPS carry capital cost to avoid interruptions.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the isolator configuration differs from its qualified configuration at authorization
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that the enclosed work area is set up differently from the setup that was previously shown to work safely when the batch is approved to start. Matching the approved setup helps ensure the batch is made under suitable conditions.
rows.imp_rat: Isolator configuration differing from qualified at authorization means the validated state is not what runs; relevant to CFJ assurance and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Change-control and setup verification procedures catch most configuration drift, but undocumented minor changes still occasionally reach authorization. Configuration verification against the qualified state is a checklist step that adds modest time before sign-off. Confirming the isolator matches its qualified configuration relies on operators recognizing deviations, which demands familiarity with the validated setup. An unnoticed configuration difference can void qualification and forfeit the batch, a costly outcome the checks aim to prevent.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in allowable leakage criteria for containment boundaries, closures, and penetrations
rows.plain: Make the allowed leakage limits clear for walls, closures, and openings that are meant to contain a hazard. Clear limits make it easier to tell whether the containment is working.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in allowable leakage criteria for boundaries and penetrations undermines integrity verification; relevant to defining the CFJ but severity moderate as it is a specification-clarity issue.
