rows.sat_rat: Guidance offers ranges rather than crisp pass/fail limits, so ambiguity in allowable leakage persists as a recurring gap the role routinely eats. Debating leakage criteria for closures and penetrations drags out specification reviews because no single authoritative threshold ends the discussion. Interpreting leak-rate acceptance for glove ports and penetrations demands scarce specialist judgement not codified in one clear standard. Ambiguity forces conservative over-specification or extra testing that inflates procurement cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that the decontaminated zone remains beyond its approved hold limit
rows.plain: Reduce how long the cleaned and treated area stays beyond its approved use time. This helps preserve the area's clean condition.
rows.imp_rat: The decontaminated zone remaining beyond its approved hold limit risks re-contamination over time; relevant to CFJ and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Hold-limit timers and procedures keep overruns fairly rare where discipline is maintained. Approved hold limits are tracked, and exceeding them triggers requalification delays. Hold-time tracking is procedural and low-skill. Exceeding hold limits forces costly re-decontamination.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation from validated ranges among critical recovery-cycle conditions
rows.plain: Keep important recovery-cycle conditions as close as possible to their approved limits. This helps the recovery process work as it was tested and approved.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation from validated recovery-cycle conditions can undermine decontamination efficacy; relevant to reliable CFJ delivery and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Control loops constrain deviation from validated ranges well, though sensor placement gaps leave some recovery conditions imperfectly held. Holding recovery conditions in range does not itself add operator time beyond the validated cycle. Automated regulation limits operator dependence for maintaining critical conditions. Sensor and control maintenance is a recurring but manageable cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the recovery-cycle configuration differs from the validated configuration
rows.plain: Make sure the recovery cycle uses the same setup that was tested and approved. This helps ensure the process works as expected after recovery.
rows.imp_rat: Recovery-cycle configuration differing from validated means the cycle's efficacy is unproven for the actual run; relevant to CFJ and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe locking and setup checklists keep configuration deviations reasonably rare. Confirming configuration against the validated setup adds a short check. Configuration verification relies on operator adherence to setup documentation. Misconfiguration reruns waste resources but are infrequent.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation from qualified processing-zone conditions before run release
rows.plain: Keep the processing area as close as possible to its approved condition before work begins. This helps ensure the production run starts in a suitable environment.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation from qualified conditions before run release means the zone is not in its proven state at the gate; relevant to CFJ assurance and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-release condition qualification is well established, keeping deviations at release low. Qualification checks before release take defined time. Interpreting qualification data needs trained review. Release qualification consumes resources.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of insufficient usable decontaminant being available for the cycle
rows.plain: Make sure enough usable cleaning chemical is available before the cleaning cycle starts. This helps the cycle work as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Insufficient usable decontaminant means the cycle cannot achieve the required kill; relevant to reliable CFJ and moderately-high severity if undetected.
rows.sat_rat: Level checks and low-level alarms make insufficient decontaminant a rare occurrence. Running out of decontaminant mid-cycle forces time-consuming aborts, but pre-checks usually prevent this. Estimating decontaminant sufficiency is routine and low-skill with level indicators. Decontaminant is inexpensive and stocking adequate supply is straightforward.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in the functional separation criteria for the closed decontaminated zone
rows.plain: Make the rules for separating the sealed, cleaned area from other areas clear and easy to understand. Clear rules help prevent unwanted contact or movement between areas.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in the functional separation criteria undermines the definition of what the CFJ must achieve; relevant to specification but severity moderate as a clarity issue.
rows.sat_rat: Functional separation definitions remain interpretation-dependent across guidances, leaving a persistent ambiguity gap. Nailing down what 'closed' functionally means for a given process consumes iterative review cycles with no fast resolution path. Translating vague separation intent into concrete criteria leans heavily on experienced aseptic-processing judgement. Ambiguity risks specifying features that later prove unnecessary or missing, both costly to correct.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of treating unsupported isolator-zone separation claims as regulator-defensible
rows.plain: Make sure claims that separated work areas are safe and properly divided are supported by evidence that regulators would accept. This helps prevent compliance problems later.
rows.imp_rat: Treating unsupported separation claims as regulator-defensible is a compliance/defensibility defect; relevance moderate and severity moderate-high due to regulatory exposure rather than direct product harm.
rows.sat_rat: Today's practice relies heavily on vendor assurances and subjective confidence, so unsupported claims frequently slip through as if defensible. Reviewers can spend weeks probing claims but still miss the distinction between asserted and evidenced defensibility under schedule pressure. Correctly recognizing an unsupported claim as unsupported demands rare senior regulatory judgement that few purchase influencers reliably possess. The cost of accepting an unsupported claim only materializes later as inspection findings, making it invisible at purchase-decision time.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of condensation contacting exposed sterile product or critical surfaces
rows.plain: Prevent water droplets from touching exposed sterile products or important clean surfaces. This helps keep them free from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Condensation contacting exposed product/critical surfaces can carry residue or support microbial growth; relevant to CFJ and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Modern VHP cycles with humidity control largely prevent condensation on critical surfaces, a fairly mature capability. Aeration and equilibration times after decontamination guard against condensation but extend cycles. Managing decontaminant dwell and humidity needs process understanding. Extended cycles consume time and utilities.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that processing-zone temperature remains outside its validated range during the cycle
rows.plain: Keep the work-area temperature within its approved range throughout the process. This helps ensure the process works as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Temperature outside validated range during the cycle can compromise decontamination efficacy; relevant to reliable CFJ and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Chamber temperature is generally well controlled, with excursions uncommon outside extreme ambient conditions. Temperature stabilization can add conditioning time in cold environments. Temperature control is largely automated with little operator finesse needed. Heating utilities and stabilization time carry modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of damaging a glove, sleeve, or boundary seal during integrity testing
rows.plain: Avoid damaging gloves, sleeves, or protective seals while checking that they keep contaminants out. This helps protect both the worker and the product.
rows.imp_rat: Damaging a glove, sleeve, or seal during integrity testing is ironic self-inflicted damage to the barrier during a supporting QA task (moderate relevance); a created breach could compromise later containment if undetected (moderate-high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Standardized test pressures and procedures keep test-induced damage uncommon, though aggressive or repeated testing still occasionally harms boundaries. Careful testing to avoid damage adds little time to the integrity check. Applying test pressures without over-stressing gloves or seals depends on operator technique and adherence to limits. An occasional glove or seal damaged during testing is a low-cost consumable replacement.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of glove and sleeve integrity losses that remain undetected during inspection
rows.plain: Find damaged gloves or sleeves during the inspection instead of leaving the damage unnoticed. This helps keep contaminants from reaching the product or worker.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected glove/sleeve integrity losses are a leading containment-breach pathway, so detection is central and severity is high when a leak goes unnoticed. Capped at the 5.5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Visual inspection and intermittent testing routinely fail to catch pinholes and micro-leaks, so a meaningful fraction of integrity losses go undetected until damage is done. Manual glove leak inspection and periodic pressure-decay testing add appreciable time yet still miss defects between checks. Reliable detection of small integrity losses depends on inspector attentiveness and interpretation, making outcomes highly operator-dependent. Undetected breaches that surface later cause expensive batch rejections and investigations, a substantial hidden cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of decontamination validation runs that fail defined sterility criteria
rows.plain: Reduce the number of cleaning and germ-removal tests that fail the required standard for keeping items free of germs.
rows.imp_rat: Decontamination validation runs failing sterility criteria indicate the cycle cannot be trusted; relevant to CFJ validation and moderately-high severity, though caught at validation stage.
rows.sat_rat: Mature decontamination validation with BIs reliably meets sterility criteria when cycles are properly developed, with failures usually traceable to load configuration issues. Validation runs are lengthy but planned events. Designing runs to meet sterility criteria demands specialized validation expertise. BI-based validation studies are costly but standard.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of deviation between aseptic-zone conditions and qualified limits at authorization
rows.plain: At the time of approval, keep the clean work area as close as possible to its allowed conditions. This helps show that the batch can proceed safely.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation between zone conditions and qualified limits at authorization means the zone is released off-spec; relevant to CFJ assurance and moderately-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Continuous environmental monitoring with alarm limits reliably flags out-of-bound conditions, keeping deviation at authorization small in most cases. Environmental readings are compared to qualified limits at authorization, a quick automated check. Judging whether a marginal reading truly deviates requires trained interpretation of trends, not just threshold checks. Authorizing outside limits risks the batch, so conservative margins add some avoidable rework cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician exposure to contained material during integrity testing
rows.plain: Protect the technician from contact with contained material while checking whether the gloves and sleeves are intact. This helps prevent exposure during the test.
rows.imp_rat: Technician exposure during integrity testing is a specific test-phase scenario rather than the core production job (relevance moderate); when it occurs it means direct exposure to contained potent material, a severe health consequence (severity high).
rows.sat_rat: Pre-test decontamination plus PPE reliably limits exposure during testing, though residual contamination on tested components remains a low-frequency risk. Integrity testing is typically done after a decontamination cycle, so protective sequencing adds routine time. Technicians follow validated test protocols with PPE, requiring moderate skill but well-supported by procedures. PPE and test consumables are modest recurring costs for this task.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that escaped purge gas creates oxygen-deficient conditions in the operator area
rows.plain: Prevent gas used to clear the enclosure from escaping and lowering the oxygen level where workers stand, which could make it unsafe to breathe.
rows.imp_rat: Escaped purge gas creating oxygen-deficient conditions is a narrower inert-gas edge case relative to the core containment job (relevance moderate); an oxygen-deficient atmosphere can asphyxiate operators, a potentially fatal consequence (severity very high).
rows.sat_rat: Inert-purge isolators are commonly fitted with room oxygen depletion monitors and alarms, making undetected oxygen-deficient conditions relatively rare where installed. Oxygen monitoring is continuous and does not impose delay on operation. Interpreting and responding to O2 alarms requires minimal specialized skill given standard alarms. Room O2 sensors and inert-gas management add modest capital and monitoring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of containment integrity being compromised during calibration activities
rows.plain: Keep the sealed area intact while pressure-measuring devices are adjusted, so nothing can leak out or get in.
rows.imp_rat: Compromising containment during calibration introduces a breach during a routine maintenance task; relevant to sustaining integrity but bounded in scope and typically performed under controlled, planned conditions.
rows.sat_rat: Most calibration is performed through sealed access points or externally, so containment compromise during calibration is uncommon. Calibration of sensors is usually done via ports or non-invasively, adding minimal time. Calibration technicians follow standard procedures that rarely require breaching containment, keeping skill demand routine. Calibration tooling and technician time are predictable low costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cycle-parameter excursions beyond validated limits
rows.plain: Reduce the number of times the settings of a cleaning and germ-removal cycle go outside their approved limits. This helps ensure the cycle works as intended every time.
rows.imp_rat: Cycle-parameter excursions beyond validated limits can undermine decontamination efficacy; relevant to reliable cycle performance and moderately-high severity depending on parameter.
rows.sat_rat: PLC-driven cycles with alarming keep excursions beyond validated limits rare in well-maintained systems. Automated control keeps parameters in range without operator time to correct excursions. Closed-loop control reduces the need for skilled manual intervention during the cycle. Instrumented control loops carry capital cost but prevent costly reruns.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that isolator containment leakage exceeds the defined acceptance limit during requalification
rows.plain: When checking the enclosed equipment again, the amount of material escaping from it should stay within the allowed limit. This helps keep harmful material contained.
rows.imp_rat: Leakage exceeding the acceptance limit during requalification is central to demonstrating ongoing containment fitness, giving solid relevance. Severity is meaningful but the failure is caught in a controlled test setting rather than during live hazardous processing.
rows.sat_rat: Automated pressure-decay leak testing against ISO 10648 class limits is a mature, repeatable method that reliably detects when leakage exceeds acceptance limits. Pressure-decay and leak-rate requalification tests are standardized and can be run in a defined test window. Automated leak-test instrumentation reduces reliance on operator judgement, though setup and interpretation still need a qualified technician. Test equipment and qualified-tester time are recurring but predictable requalification costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that facility power or pressurized gas remains live when isolator disconnection begins
rows.plain: Before disconnecting the enclosed equipment, the person should make sure that electricity and pressurized gas have been safely turned off. This prevents electric shock, sudden gas release, or unexpected equipment activity.
rows.imp_rat: Live facility power or pressurized gas at disconnection is a decommissioning safety error, peripheral to processing (moderate relevance) but capable of causing serious injury when it occurs (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: LOTO and permit systems make live power or pressurized gas at disconnection an uncommon, well-controlled event. Confirming power and gas are dead adds only brief verification time. Verifying zero energy is a routine, well-trained electrical/utility task. Verification uses inexpensive standard tools and labor.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of hazardous utilities remaining connected to the isolator when dismantling begins
rows.plain: Before taking apart the enclosed equipment, the person should make sure that no dangerous pipes, wires, or other supplies are still connected. This prevents unexpected exposure to danger during dismantling.
rows.imp_rat: Hazardous utilities remaining connected when dismantling begins is a decommissioning-phase error, less central to core processing (moderate relevance), but if it occurs it can cause serious energy or material release (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Permit-to-work and disconnection checklists reliably ensure utilities are off before dismantling begins. Verifying all utilities are disconnected before dismantling is a quick checklist step. Utility disconnection is standard facilities work needing common, not scarce, skills. Disconnection incurs only routine labor cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that inspection handling damages a glove or sleeve assembly during active operation
rows.plain: Avoid tearing or damaging the attached gloves or protective sleeves while checking them during operation. This helps keep the operator and the work area protected.
rows.imp_rat: Inspection handling damaging a glove/sleeve during active operation can breach containment mid-process, a severe sterility event, though it is one specific damage pathway. Capped at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Even with SOPs, inspection handling still occasionally introduces micro-tears or weakens seams, so containment integrity during active operation is not consistently protected. Routine visual and pressure-decay glove inspections take only minutes, but doing them without snagging or over-flexing a glove during active operation still slows operators down as they work cautiously. Avoiding inspection-induced damage depends heavily on operator finesse and trained handling technique, since gloves are thin and vulnerable and there is little tooling to make careful handling foolproof. Accidental damage during inspection forces glove replacement, batch holds, and potential product loss, so the recurring cost exposure remains meaningful.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of misclassifying observed deterioration or damage during containment boundary inspection
rows.plain: Make sure visible wear or damage to the protective enclosure is identified correctly. Accurate checks help prevent leaks or unsafe exposure.
rows.imp_rat: Misclassifying deterioration during containment boundary inspection is moderately relevant since accurate boundary assessment supports containment, but it is one step removed from live containment; severity is elevated because a misjudged breach could allow undetected hazardous exposure.
rows.sat_rat: Subjective acceptance criteria and inconsistent reference standards make misclassification a persistent, poorly controlled failure mode. Classifying findings adds little time but pauses for judgement calls do occur. Deciding whether observed damage is acceptable, monitor, or reject demands experienced judgement against often-vague criteria. Misclassification either scraps good parts or passes bad ones, both costly, but the act itself is cheap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of loss of containment while the operator diagnoses or resolves an alarm or deviation
rows.plain: Prevent material or harmful substances from escaping while the operator investigates or fixes a warning or problem. The equipment should remain safely closed during this work.
rows.imp_rat: Loss of containment while diagnosing an alarm is directly tied to the core containment job and carries real exposure risk; capped here per batch ceiling though its live-breach nature keeps both proxies moderately high.
rows.sat_rat: Because resolution can involve interventions and door or transfer actions, containment is genuinely vulnerable while an alarm is being worked, and current practice does not fully protect it. Prolonged diagnosis extends the window during which containment is at risk. Maintaining containment while troubleshooting demands calm, trained operators who follow procedure under pressure. A containment loss during resolution can scrap a batch and force decontamination, a large cost hit.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of decontaminating agent escaping from the isolator chamber into the surrounding work area
rows.plain: Prevent the chemical used for cleaning and removing contamination from escaping the enclosed chamber. This protects people working nearby from exposure.
rows.imp_rat: Decontaminating agent (e.g., VHP) escaping into the work area is a supporting-process concern rather than the primary containment job (relevance moderate); sterilant escape harms operators and can breach exposure limits, a meaningful but often reversible consequence (severity moderate-high).
rows.sat_rat: Sealed chambers with catalytic aeration and area H2O2 monitoring reliably keep agent escape below occupational limits during normal cycles. Aeration and monitored release are timed steps that do not materially delay the preparation stage beyond expectation. Setting up catalytic aeration and area sensors is largely engineered into the system, reducing reliance on operator judgement. Catalytic converters and area monitors add capital cost but are standard containment provisions.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between tested conditions and intended qualified conditions
rows.plain: Make sure the conditions tested are the same as, or closely match, the conditions the process is meant to be approved for. This makes the test results useful for the intended situation.
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between tested and intended qualified conditions is a validation-scope defect; relevance moderate as it undermines confidence but not directly product, severity moderate for compliance/quality risk.
rows.sat_rat: Protocol-to-intended-condition mapping is established practice but drift between what was tested and what is claimed remains a common residual gap. Comparing tested versus intended conditions adds review cycles but is a routine step. Judging whether test conditions truly represent intended qualified conditions needs seasoned process understanding. Mismatches force re-testing at real expense, though good protocol design limits this.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of renewed supporting-utility loss during recovery
rows.plain: Prevent the services needed to run the equipment from failing again while an interrupted operation is being restarted. This helps recovery continue smoothly.
rows.imp_rat: Repeated utility loss during recovery prolongs instability and complicates return to validated state; relevant to reliable restoration but a secondary, recovery-phase concern rather than the primary containment function.
rows.sat_rat: Backup power and utility redundancy reduce repeat failures, but cascading or common-cause utility faults during recovery still recur. Renewed utility loss during recovery restarts the clock, extending downtime unpredictably. Avoiding repeat loss relies on maintenance and facility staff diagnosing the root cause correctly under pressure. Redundant utilities and backups exist but represent significant capital, and repeated losses still drive rework cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of material isolator-zone separation claims lacking objective supporting evidence
rows.plain: Make sure claims that materials were kept separate from the clean processing area are supported by clear, measurable evidence. This matters because others must be able to verify that the separation really worked.
rows.imp_rat: Separation claims lacking objective supporting evidence is an evidentiary defect; relevance moderate and severity moderate due to compliance/defensibility rather than direct product harm.
rows.sat_rat: Checklists exist but coverage of evidence-backing is patchy, so many material claims still travel unsupported. Assembling objective evidence for every material claim is slow and often truncated before the buy decision. Distinguishing objective evidence from persuasive narrative requires validation expertise not consistently on the review team. Chasing missing evidence adds review labor cost but avoids far larger remediation costs later.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of residual materials with unconfirmed identity before handling
rows.plain: Reduce the amount of leftover material whose identity has not been confirmed before anyone handles it. This helps prevent mix-ups and unsafe handling.
rows.imp_rat: Handling residual materials of unconfirmed identity is a cleaning/handling ambiguity somewhat peripheral to the containment job (lower relevance), but mishandling unknown residues can cause cross-contamination or exposure (moderate-high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Residues of unconfirmed identity are a chronic problem that routinely forces worst-case handling assumptions with little standard remedy. Identifying unknown residual materials requires analytical turnaround that delays handling. Confirming unknown residue identity depends on analytical chemistry expertise rarely embedded in disposal crews. Lab identification of unknowns adds significant testing cost per case.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of dropping or misplacing intervention items inside the closed processing zone
rows.plain: Avoid dropping or losing tools and other items inside the closed processing area during an intervention. This matters because misplaced items can disrupt the work or contaminate the area.
rows.imp_rat: Dropping/misplacing items inside the zone can disrupt airflow or introduce contamination; relevant to CFJ and moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Good technique reduces drops but they still occur inside the constrained enclosure. Retrieving dropped items interrupts the intervention. Avoiding drops through stiff gloves depends on operator handling skill. Lost items may require batch impact assessment.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of decontaminant condensate on processing-zone surfaces during the cycle
rows.plain: Keep the amount of cleaning liquid that collects on processing-area surfaces as low as possible during the cycle. This matters because excess liquid can interfere with the process or leave unwanted residue.
rows.imp_rat: Decontaminant condensate on surfaces during the cycle can leave residue and uneven treatment; relevant to CFJ and moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Condensate on surfaces is a known variable in H2O2 cycles, controlled but not fully eliminated across loads. Managing condensate can lengthen conditioning and aeration. Avoiding condensation requires careful humidity and temperature setup expertise. Micro-condensation cycles balance efficacy and residue at some utility cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of zone-separation requirements lacking traceable qualification evidence
rows.plain: Make sure there is clear, checkable proof that areas needing separation have been tested and shown to stay separate. This helps people trust that the separation will work as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Zone-separation requirements lacking traceable qualification evidence is a documentation/traceability defect; relevance moderate and severity moderate for compliance exposure.
rows.sat_rat: Requirements frequently lack linked qualification evidence, and this gap is a recurring inspection weakness. Building a full requirement-to-qualification trace matrix is time-consuming and rarely complete before recommendation. Traceability mapping is a specialized quality-engineering skill that purchase influencers seldom apply themselves. Trace gaps discovered post-purchase drive expensive re-qualification, though the upfront tracing itself is modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of damaging a product container or closure during an aseptic intervention
rows.plain: Avoid damaging a container or its seal while working in the sterile area. This helps keep the product protected from contamination or leaks.
rows.imp_rat: Damaging a product container/closure during intervention can compromise the unit and release contents; relevant to product integrity and moderately-high severity, though localized.
rows.sat_rat: Trained technique limits damage but glove-constrained handling still causes occasional breakage. Careful handling adds modest time. Avoiding container/closure damage through gloves relies on operator dexterity. Damaged containers are direct product loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a degraded air-handling component remains undetected during inspection
rows.plain: Make it very unlikely that an air-moving part that is wearing out goes unnoticed during a check. Finding problems early helps keep air movement safe and reliable.
rows.imp_rat: An undetected degraded air-handling component threatens the pressure/airflow that maintains containment (moderate relevance), and its failure could silently breach containment (moderate-high severity), but this is an inspection-stage safeguard.
rows.sat_rat: Periodic inspection and airflow checks catch gross degradation, but incipient faults frequently go undetected between PMs. Assessing air-handling components thoroughly extends the maintenance window. Detecting early fan, damper or filter degradation depends on experienced diagnostic judgement. An undetected degraded component can cause a larger failure and costly unplanned downtime.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical separation claims lacking measurable pass-or-fail acceptance criteria
rows.plain: Make sure every important claim about keeping areas apart has a measurable pass-or-fail rule. This makes it possible to test and verify whether the separation works.
rows.imp_rat: Separation claims lacking measurable pass/fail criteria is a specification-quality defect; relevance moderate and severity moderate as it affects verifiability not immediate product.
rows.sat_rat: Critical separation claims frequently arrive without crisp acceptance criteria, undermining objective evaluation. Defining measurable pass/fail criteria for each claim is laborious and often deferred to protocol writing later. Setting defensible acceptance criteria demands specialist knowledge of standards and statistics not always present. Late criteria definition drives rework cost, while upfront definition is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between assessed decontamination methods and sterile fill-finish requirements
rows.plain: Choose enclosed work chambers whose cleaning and germ-removal methods closely meet the needs of making and sealing sterile products. This helps prevent ineffective cleaning or sterilization.
rows.imp_rat: A mismatch between decontamination methods and sterile fill-finish requirements can leave residual bioburden, which is relevant to sterility assurance; severity is elevated because inadequate decontamination risks contaminated product, though it is caught upstream in method selection.
rows.sat_rat: Decontamination-to-requirement mismatches are a well-known recurring problem, and current practice catches many but not all before commitment. Matching decontamination methods to process requirements adds analysis time but fits within normal evaluation cycles. Judging fit between VHP or other decontamination methods and sterile requirements needs specialized microbiological and materials knowledge that is scarce. The assessment cost is modest, but a mismatch discovered post-purchase forces costly cycle redevelopment or hardware change.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of zone-separation requirements whose acceptance criteria cannot be objectively verified
rows.plain: Make sure every requirement for keeping work areas separate can be checked with clear, measurable evidence. Requirements that cannot be checked objectively may allow problems to go unnoticed.
rows.imp_rat: Requirements whose acceptance criteria cannot be objectively verified is a specification defect; relevance moderate and severity moderate for verifiability/compliance.
rows.sat_rat: Many criteria remain worded in ways that cannot be objectively tested, a persistent gap in current practice. Checking whether each criterion is objectively verifiable adds review cycles that time pressure often curtails. Judging verifiability requires measurement and metrology expertise scarce among purchase influencers. Unverifiable criteria cost heavily when they fail inspection but little to flag early.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material containers damaged while crossing the zone boundary
rows.plain: Prevent containers from being damaged while they are moved through the boundary between work areas. This helps keep their contents safe and usable.
rows.imp_rat: Material containers damaged crossing the boundary can spill or breach containment; relevant to CFJ and moderate severity, typically localized.
rows.sat_rat: Transfer ports and packaging protect containers well, so boundary-crossing damage is uncommon. Careful transfer handling adds little time. Avoiding container damage at the boundary depends on handling care. Damaged containers are wasted material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of damage to processing-zone surfaces or components from cycle exposure
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that the cleaning process damages surfaces or parts in the processing area. This helps keep the equipment safe and usable.
rows.imp_rat: Damage to surfaces/components from cycle exposure can create hidden crevices or breaches over time; relevant to CFJ durability and moderate-high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Validated material selection makes cycle-induced surface damage uncommon over normal service life. Preventing surface damage does not add cycle time. Material compatibility is designed in, requiring little operator skill. Compatible materials cost more upfront but avoid replacement.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that validation samples become contaminated during handling
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that samples used to prove the process keeps products free of germs become contaminated while being handled. This helps make the test results trustworthy.
rows.imp_rat: Validation samples contaminated during handling produce false results, undermining decontamination confidence; relevant to CFJ validation integrity and moderate-high severity (false positives disrupt but false negatives dangerous).
rows.sat_rat: Negative controls and trained handling keep sample contamination low, though occasional false positives from handling still occur. Sample handling adds minor time to validation. Preventing sample contamination during handling depends on careful aseptic technique by lab staff. Negative controls and handling supplies are low incremental cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that production resumes before the overseer's authorization is issued
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that production starts again before the person in charge gives permission. This helps ensure work resumes only after the situation has been reviewed.
rows.imp_rat: Production resuming before overseer authorization is a procedural-gate defect; relevance moderate as it bypasses oversight, severity moderate-high depending on underlying condition.
rows.sat_rat: Access interlocks and permit-to-operate practices make premature resumption uncommon, though procedural-only controls leave residual risk. Interlocks and procedural holds stop production from resuming instantly without authorization, so timing is enforced. Where interlocks exist little skill is needed, but manual-hold zones depend on operator discipline to wait for sign-off. Unauthorized resumption risks losing product, motivating the engineered and procedural holds already in place.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of filled units containing a product quantity outside specification
rows.plain: Make sure each filled unit contains the required amount of product. This prevents units from having too much or too little.
rows.imp_rat: Filled units with out-of-spec product quantity is a fill-accuracy defect that is relevant to product quality but only tangential to the isolator's core containment/sterility job; severity is moderate since affected units are typically rejected rather than causing irreversible harm.
rows.sat_rat: Statistical fill control and in-process checkweighing keep the vast majority of units within spec, a mature and dependable practice. In-line weight checks correct fill deviations quickly without slowing the line materially. Automated fill-weight control removes most operator dependence for hitting the target quantity. Out-of-spec fills waste expensive product, and checkweighing directly limits that loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the initial cycle is interrupted by loss of required chamber conditions
rows.plain: Make sure the first cleaning cycle is not stopped because the chamber loses a condition it needs. Completing the cycle is important for removing unwanted contamination.
rows.imp_rat: An interrupted initial cycle from loss of chamber conditions wastes the cycle and delays qualification; moderately relevant to reliable operation, but an aborted cycle typically fails safe rather than causing a breach.
rows.sat_rat: Closed-loop control of pressure, humidity and temperature holds conditions reliably, with interruptions usually traceable to identifiable equipment faults. An interrupted first cycle forces a restart that costs hours, but such interruptions are infrequent. Maintaining chamber conditions is largely automated, reducing dependence on operator intervention during the cycle. A failed cycle wastes agent and energy but is an occasional rather than routine loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of decontamination exposure damaging chamber surfaces or internal components
rows.plain: Prevent the cleaning process from damaging the inside surfaces or parts of the chamber. This helps keep the equipment safe and usable.
rows.imp_rat: Decontamination damaging chamber surfaces degrades the barrier over time and can create harder-to-clean surfaces; moderately relevant since it undermines long-term integrity, but damage is usually gradual and detectable during inspection.
rows.sat_rat: Established material selection guidance limits exposure damage, though gradual degradation of seals and sensitive components remains a known long-run issue. Material compatibility assessment happens in design/prep and does not delay cycles. Judging which components tolerate repeated H2O2 exposure requires materials knowledge that is moderately specialized. Replacing degraded gaskets, sensors or coatings over time is a recurring but modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing contamination into the sterilant supply during replenishment
rows.plain: Keep dirt or other unwanted material from getting into the supply of sterilizing liquid while it is being refilled. This helps the cleaning process work safely and properly.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination of the sterilant supply during replenishment undermines decontamination effectiveness but is a supporting activity rather than the primary sterile/containment operation (moderate relevance); consequences degrade sterility assurance but are usually detectable and correctable (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Sealed, single-use sterilant cartridges and closed transfer connections make supply contamination during refill quite unlikely under standard practice. Replenishment via sealed cartridges is fast and adds negligible time. Keyed sealed connections require little specialized skill to keep the supply clean. Pre-packaged sterilant cartridges cost more per unit but eliminate contamination-related waste.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a sterilant spill or operator exposure during reservoir replenishment
rows.plain: Make it as unlikely as possible for sterilizing liquid to spill or touch the worker while a supply tank is being refilled. This protects both the worker and the workplace.
rows.imp_rat: A sterilant spill during reservoir replenishment is a peripheral consumable-handling task (relevance moderate); a spill or operator exposure to concentrated sterilant causes chemical injury but is a localized, manageable event (severity moderate-high).
rows.sat_rat: Modern closed refill connectors and sealed cartridges make spill-free replenishment reliable, though older manual-fill designs still risk splashes. Sterilant reservoir replenishment is a quick routine task with minimal downtime. Closed-loop or cartridge-based sterilant refill systems reduce the skill needed, though manual pouring of H2O2 still demands care. Sterilant consumables and occasional spill-kit use are low recurring costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that isolator environmental conditions fail defined sterility acceptance criteria during requalification
rows.plain: Make sure the enclosure’s conditions still meet the required cleanliness standards when its protective performance is checked again. This confirms it can continue to prevent contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Failing sterility acceptance criteria during requalification relates to product/environmental integrity rather than the isolator's primary hazardous-containment job, so relevance is moderate. Severity is moderate — it compromises product quality and batch validity but is detected in a controlled requalification rather than causing acute harm.
rows.sat_rat: Routine viable/non-viable monitoring and smoke studies are established, but delayed microbial results and sampling variability mean sterility failures still surface unpredictably. Environmental requalification requires incubation of viable samples, adding days of lag before pass/fail is known. Sampling, media handling and aseptic technique demand skilled microbiology and QC personnel to avoid false results. Media, incubation, particle counters and QC labor are significant recurring requalification costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that enclosure pressure moves outside the validated range during purging
rows.plain: Keep the air pressure inside the enclosure within the approved range while it is being cleared of unwanted material. This helps maintain the enclosure’s protective conditions.
rows.imp_rat: Enclosure pressure moving outside the validated range during purging is relevant to maintaining the containment envelope (moderate relevance); consequences are typically transient excursions that can be corrected or revalidated (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Closed-loop pressure regulation keeps enclosure pressure within validated bands consistently, with only occasional transient excursions during rapid transitions. Automated purge sequences ramp pressure over controlled intervals, so timing itself is well managed. Pressure control during purge is automated by the PLC, reducing dependence on operator finesse. The control hardware is built into the isolator, so maintaining validated pressure during purge adds no incremental cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that inspection handling transfers contamination within the enclosure during active operation
rows.plain: Make sure checking or handling items does not spread unwanted material inside the enclosure while it is operating. This helps protect the work and the products inside.
rows.imp_rat: Inspection handling transferring contamination within the enclosure during active operation directly threatens the aseptic core job, with serious sterility consequences, but is a specific handling subactivity. Capped at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Glove ports and first-air rules help, but manual inspection handling remains a leading source of intra-enclosure contamination transfer. Inspection handling adds manipulation time inside the enclosure but does not primarily concern delay. Contamination-free handling during inspection hinges almost entirely on operator aseptic technique and manual dexterity through gloves, a demanding and error-prone skill. A single handling-induced contamination can scrap sterile units, a costly outcome the current practice only partly prevents.

rows.source_job: Connect utilities and ducting to isolator
rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of contamination introduced through open isolator utility interfaces during connection
rows.plain: Keep dirt and other unwanted material from getting inside when workers connect the outside services to the sealed enclosure.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination introduced through open utility interfaces during connection directly undermines the sterile/containment job, making it fairly relevant; severity is meaningful because ingress can compromise batch sterility, though it is bounded to connection events.
rows.sat_rat: Capped fittings and clean-connection practices help, but open interfaces during connection remain a recognized ingress point that is hard to fully assure. Connecting utilities cleanly and verifying takes modest added time during integration. Keeping open utility interfaces contamination-free during connection depends heavily on technician technique and cleanroom discipline. Contamination through utility ports is cheap to prevent but costly if it forces re-decontamination.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contamination being transferred between isolator components during segregation handling
rows.plain: Prevent unwanted material from spreading from one sealed-enclosure part to another while contaminated parts are separated.
rows.imp_rat: Cross-transfer of contamination between components during segregation handling is relevant to keeping the isolator clean (moderate relevance), but this handling step is occasional and consequences are usually localized (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: There is no widely standardized disposal-stage protocol for inter-component transfer, so residual contamination migrating between parts remains a recurring and hard-to-verify failure. Segregation handling during disposal is a slow manual process, and cross-contamination checks add further delays that today's crews absorb piecemeal. Preventing transfer of residual active compound between isolator parts leans heavily on the judgement of experienced decontamination technicians who are scarce at the disposal stage. Extra PPE, wipe-down consumables and disposable liners are used to limit transfer, adding modest recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of hazardous materials or process steps omitted from the containment basis
rows.plain: Make sure all dangerous materials and all steps that could release them are included when planning how to contain them. This helps prevent people and the surrounding area from being exposed.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting hazardous materials or steps from the containment basis means the design may not protect against real hazards, which is relevant to the job's foundation; severity is elevated because an under-scoped basis can leave true exposure risks uncovered.
rows.sat_rat: Established OEB banding and hazard-review practice captures most inputs, but omitted materials or edge-case process steps remain a real residual risk. Building the containment basis from hazard data takes effort but is a defined upfront step. Identifying every hazardous material and process step demands industrial-hygiene and toxicology expertise that is often thinly staffed. Hazard characterization is inexpensive relative to containment failure consequences.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of air-handling components incorrectly reassembled after service
rows.plain: Reduce the number of air-moving parts that are put back together incorrectly after service. Correct assembly helps the equipment work safely and properly.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrectly reassembled air-handling components after service can degrade the airflow essential to containment (moderate relevance), and a faulty reassembly risks a containment failure (moderate-high severity), though it is a post-service check point.
rows.sat_rat: Work instructions and post-service airflow checks catch many errors, but reassembly mistakes still occur and are not always caught before startup. Careful reassembly and verification slow the return-to-service. Correct reassembly of air-handling components relies on technician knowledge of orientation and seating. Incorrect reassembly wastes rework labor and can damage parts.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing contamination into the isolator controlled environment during physical examination
rows.plain: Lower the chance that germs or dirt enter the protected work area while seals are being examined. This helps keep the area suitable for making sterile medicine.
rows.imp_rat: Introducing contamination during physical examination directly undermines the containment isolator's sterility job (moderate-high relevance), and a contaminated controlled environment can spoil product batches (moderate severity), though examination is an occasional activity.
rows.sat_rat: Standard aseptic controls and re-decon cycles manage the risk, yet physical intrusion still leaves a recurring contamination risk. Physical examination inside the controlled environment demands time-consuming aseptic setup. Preventing contamination during hands-on examination relies on strong operator aseptic discipline. Contamination excursions are expensive to remediate and revalidate.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of approving a supplier unable to fulfill containment isolator delivery and lifecycle support commitments
rows.plain: Avoid approving a supplier that cannot deliver a sealed safety enclosure or support it throughout its useful life. This helps ensure the equipment and its ongoing support will be available as promised.
rows.imp_rat: Approving an incapable supplier is an upstream procurement error affecting long-term lifecycle support rather than the immediate containment function; consequences unfold over time and can be mitigated, making it less central than operational breach errors.
rows.sat_rat: Predicting future delivery and support capability is inherently uncertain, and standard vetting only weakly forecasts a supplier's later performance. Financial and capacity due diligence adds weeks but runs in parallel with technical qualification, so it rarely dominates the schedule. Assessing a supplier's ability to sustain delivery and long-term lifecycle support demands seasoned commercial and technical judgement that many buying teams lack. Reference checks and financial vetting are inexpensive relative to the isolator purchase, but a wrong call carries large downstream cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree to which residual oxygen or moisture remains above the specified threshold after purging
rows.plain: After clearing the sealed work area, keep oxygen and moisture below the required levels. This helps maintain the conditions needed for the work.
rows.imp_rat: Residual oxygen/moisture above threshold after purging affects sensitive product quality, relevant to the isolator's atmosphere-control job, though many products tolerate brief excursions. Held to ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: O2/moisture sensors verify endpoints dependably, but reaching very low thresholds consistently in complex geometries still shows residual variability. Reaching target oxygen or moisture levels requires a defined purge duration that extends setup time. Purge endpoint judgement is increasingly instrument-based, reducing reliance on operator skill. Achieving low residual levels consumes inert gas, an ongoing consumable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of ambient-air ingress into the enclosure during or after purging
rows.plain: Prevent outside air from entering the sealed work area during or after the air-clearing process. This helps keep the inside conditions protected from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Ambient-air ingress after purging directly undermines the controlled atmosphere central to containment, and severity is meaningful since it can compromise product sterility. Kept at or below the 5.5 mean ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Overpressure and sealed glove/port design reliably resist ambient ingress, though door and glove breaches remain occasional ingress paths. Positive-pressure recovery after purge re-establishes quickly, limiting the ingress window. Maintaining seal integrity that prevents ingress is largely designed-in rather than operator-dependent. Gas consumption to maintain overpressure is an ongoing but modest cost.

rows.source_job: Connect utilities and ducting to isolator
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of leakage from a gas or exhaust connection after hookup
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that gas or exhaust will leak after the connection is made. A tight connection helps protect workers and keeps the system working as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Leakage from a gas or exhaust connection after hookup directly threatens containment integrity and operator/product safety (moderate-high severity), and is fairly relevant to the job, though the ceiling caps it below the higher-ranked batch.
rows.sat_rat: Pressure-decay and leak testing are mature standard practice that reliably detects connection leakage before use. Leak testing gas and exhaust connections adds a defined but manageable time block. Standard leak-test procedures make this largely a procedural task requiring modest skill. Leak test consumables and rework are inexpensive relative to a containment breach.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of containers filled outside the specified target due to mismatched scale and filling responses
rows.plain: As few containers as possible should be filled with too much or too little product because the weighing and filling equipment do not respond consistently. This helps make sure each container has the intended amount.
rows.imp_rat: Fill inaccuracy from mismatched scale and filling responses is a dosing-quality error that is somewhat peripheral to the isolator's containment job; severity is moderate as mis-filled containers are usually detected and rejected, though under-dosing could reach patients.
rows.sat_rat: Servo-driven fillers with feedback keep responses well matched, though lag and calibration drift still produce occasional off-target containers. Feedback between scale and filler adjusts in near real time, so mismatch corrections are timely. Calibration and tuning of the scale-filler loop require technician skill to keep responses matched. Mismatched responses waste product until corrected, but the loss is bounded by automated feedback.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of unexpected filling-equipment stoppages during sterile filling
rows.plain: Prevent the filling equipment from stopping unexpectedly during germ-free filling. This helps the filling work continue without interruptions.
rows.imp_rat: Unexpected filling-equipment stoppages during sterile filling interrupt the core aseptic process, but the containment barrier itself is not breached; consequences are yield loss and batch delay rather than direct sterility failure. Capped per batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Preventive maintenance and robust filler design reduce stoppages, but jams, sensor faults, and component wear still cause recurring unplanned stops. Unexpected stoppages themselves consume line time and require restart sequences. Minimizing stoppages relies on operator and maintenance expertise to anticipate jams and faults. Each stoppage risks product loss and reduces throughput, a direct cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between displayed pressure, airflow, humidity, and particulate values and actual enclosure conditions
rows.plain: Make the displayed readings for pressure, airflow, moisture, and particles match the real conditions inside the enclosure. This helps people make decisions based on accurate information.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation between displayed and actual pressure/airflow/humidity/particulate values can mask a real containment breach, making it moderately central and consequential, though redundant monitoring and alarms usually mitigate the worst outcomes.
rows.sat_rat: Routine calibration keeps displayed pressure, airflow, humidity and particulate values close to actual, though drift between calibrations introduces some deviation. Calibrated sensors update displays in near real time with negligible lag. Operators trust displayed values without needing skill to interpret sensor accuracy, relying on periodic calibration. Sensor drift causing false readings can trigger unnecessary interventions or accepted bad product, a moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of approving a prospective containment isolator supplier with undetected quality-system or regulatory deficiencies
rows.plain: Prevent approval of a supplier whose quality practices or legal compliance problems have not been found. This helps ensure the supplier can provide safe and reliable enclosed equipment for handling clean products.
rows.imp_rat: Approving a supplier with undetected quality/regulatory deficiencies is upstream and administrative, so relevance to the immediate containment job is moderate. Severity is meaningful because a bad supplier can propagate systemic defects, but the link is indirect and mediated by later inspections.
rows.sat_rat: Mature GMP supplier-qualification programs and standardized audit checklists catch most deficiencies, yet undetected latent issues still occasionally slip through. Supplier audits and quality-system reviews take weeks to schedule and complete, but this is standard practice built into procurement timelines. Detecting hidden quality-system or regulatory deficiencies depends heavily on scarce experienced auditors whose judgement varies, leaving real residual gaps. On-site audits and third-party assessments cost real money and travel, though this is an accepted, budgeted cost for critical suppliers.

rows.source_job: Assemble isolator modules into complete unit
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of isolator module interfaces incorrectly oriented, aligned, or seated during assembly
rows.plain: Make sure the connecting parts of the sealed work modules are facing the right way, lined up, and firmly joined during assembly. This helps the completed unit work properly and remain sealed.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrectly oriented or seated module interfaces during assembly can create leak paths that defeat containment; relevance is moderate since assembly errors are typically detected at commissioning, but consequences of an undetected seating fault are meaningful.
rows.sat_rat: Keyed connections, alignment marks and factory pre-assembly make gross mis-seating uncommon, with subtle misalignment being the remaining error. Correct orientation and seating during modular assembly adds little time when done right, but rework on misalignment costs hours. Proper alignment and seating of module interfaces depends on assembler skill, though keying and alignment features reduce this dependence. Misassembled interfaces are usually correctable at low material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of damaging or deforming a seal during physical examination
rows.plain: Prevent a seal from being cut, crushed, or misshaped while it is being examined. Keeping it in good condition helps it continue to block leaks.
rows.imp_rat: Damaging a seal during examination degrades containment integrity, which matters to the isolator's core job (moderate relevance); a breached seal risks contamination leakage (moderate-high severity) but is often caught and repaired.
rows.sat_rat: Careful practice keeps handling-induced seal damage relatively infrequent, with occasional lapses. Cautious examination technique adds marginal time. Avoiding seal deformation during handling takes trained finesse but is not rare expertise. Damaged seals must be replaced, an avoidable material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the revised software or firmware causes incorrect isolator control behavior after installation
rows.plain: Make sure a software update does not make the enclosed equipment behave incorrectly. This helps keep its controls working safely after the update.
rows.imp_rat: Faulty software/firmware causing incorrect isolator control behavior is relevant because control logic governs containment and process integrity, but it occurs only after updates; severity is moderate-to-high since erroneous control could compromise a run, though capped by the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Vendor factory testing plus site IQ/OQ catches most regressions, but edge-case control faults still slip through into installed systems. Validation testing of new firmware extends the update window but is planned for. Confirming correct control behavior after a software revision requires specialized controls and validation expertise not present at every site. A bad firmware release causing wrong control behavior can halt production expensively, though vendor validation usually intercepts it first.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing contamination into the controlled environment during containment boundary inspection
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that dirt or other unwanted material will enter the controlled area during the inspection. Keeping the area clean helps protect the work and products inside.
rows.imp_rat: Introducing contamination during containment boundary inspection directly compromises the controlled environment the isolator exists to protect; it is a meaningful risk, though confined to inspection events and mitigated by procedure, so it stays within the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Gowning, wipe-down and post-work bio-decontamination limit contamination introduction, but breaches during open-boundary inspection still occur. Gowning and controlled-entry protocols add time to any boundary inspection. Contamination-avoiding technique requires disciplined aseptic practice from the technician. A contamination event triggers costly re-decontamination and downtime.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of residual contamination on isolator surfaces at release from active production service
rows.plain: Leave as little contamination as possible on the surfaces of the enclosed work area when it is taken out of active production. This helps make the area safe and clean after production ends.
rows.imp_rat: Residual contamination at release from active service risks cross-contamination and operator exposure, which is serious, but this end-of-service transition is a bounded, procedurally controlled event rather than the ongoing containment job, so relevance is capped.
rows.sat_rat: Cleaning validation practice reliably reduces surface contamination, with residual variability on complex geometries. Cleaning surfaces to release criteria at end of production adds a defined cleaning window. Meeting surface-residue limits needs trained cleaning technicians and swab interpretation. Cleaning agents, swabs and analysis add routine cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of misidentifying the condition causing an alarm or deviation
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that the cause of an alarm or problem will be mistaken. Correctly identifying the cause helps workers fix the right issue safely and quickly.
rows.imp_rat: Misidentifying an alarm's root cause delays proper corrective action, which matters to sustaining containment but is diagnostic support rather than a primary containment barrier; consequences are moderate as most alarms allow safe interim states.
rows.sat_rat: Generic alarm messages and overlapping causes make misidentification common without expert interpretation. Diagnosing an alarm's true cause can take significant time as operators check multiple parameters and logs. Correctly identifying the condition behind an alarm relies heavily on experienced operator judgement and process knowledge, a scarce and error-prone dependency. Misdiagnosis leads to wrong corrective actions and potential batch loss, a notable cost driver.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of failing to detect deterioration or damage on examined containment surfaces
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that damage or wear on the surfaces that keep material contained will go unnoticed. Finding problems early helps prevent leaks or loss of containment.
rows.imp_rat: Failing to detect deterioration on examined containment surfaces overlaps with barrier-integrity assurance and can permit an undetected breach, moderately central with meaningful severity. Kept at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Manual visual examination without instrumentation misses early or hidden surface deterioration at a nontrivial rate. Full surface examination is time-consuming and constrained by production schedules. Spotting deterioration on containment surfaces relies on trained eyes and consistent technique that varies substantially between technicians. Inspection itself is low-cost; undetected damage carries the real downstream expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between the recommendation and applicable qualification evidence
rows.plain: Make sure the proposed choice is supported by the available testing and approval results. This helps ensure the recommendation is based on evidence rather than guesswork.
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between a recommendation and qualification evidence is a documentation/defensibility defect, only indirectly tied to physical separation; relevance moderate and severity moderate as it affects compliance more than immediate product safety.
rows.sat_rat: Formal validation review and approval workflows catch many mismatches, yet subtle evidence gaps still slip through, leaving this well but not fully served. Reviewers spend significant time cross-checking recommendations against qualification packages, so timeliness is moderate at best. Detecting mismatch between a recommendation and its supporting evidence demands experienced quality and validation judgement that is not evenly distributed. Rework when a mismatch surfaces late is costly, but structured review gates limit how often that happens.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of separation claims lacking traceable qualification evidence
rows.plain: Make sure claims that two things are properly separated have clear records showing that the separation was tested and approved. This avoids relying on unsupported claims.
rows.imp_rat: Separation claims lacking traceable qualification evidence is an evidentiary/documentation gap; relevance moderate as it does not itself contaminate product, severity moderate due to compliance exposure.
rows.sat_rat: Traceability matrices and validation protocols are standard practice, but claims lacking evidence still recur, keeping this only moderately served. Tracing every separation claim to evidence is slow when documentation is scattered across systems. Establishing traceability requires disciplined documentation practice that experienced validation staff supply but juniors often miss. Untraceable claims trigger expensive audit findings and re-qualification, a recurring cost driver.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of enclosure pressure excursions during glove-and-sleeve movement
rows.plain: Prevent the pressure inside the enclosure from changing too often while workers move their hands and sleeves through its built-in gloves, so the protected conditions stay stable.
rows.imp_rat: Pressure excursions during glove-and-sleeve movement are routine transients that can momentarily challenge the differential central to containment, with moderate severity if brief. Capped at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Well-tuned PID pressure control on current isolators substantially damps glove-and-sleeve excursions, yet brief overshoots during rapid movements still occur. Modern pressure control loops recover from glove-movement disturbances within seconds, so the transient rarely lingers. Fast-acting automatic pressure regulation removes most of the burden from operators, though careful, slow glove movements still help. Occasional excursions rarely cause direct loss but can trigger alarms and investigations that consume time and resources.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of activating an incorrect operating mode for the current sterile fill-finish process
rows.plain: Make sure the correct operating mode is turned on for the current clean filling process.
rows.imp_rat: Activating an incorrect operating mode for the current process can misalign conditions with sterile requirements, moderately relevant; severity is moderate because mode errors typically produce detectable anomalies before harm.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe-based automation and access restrictions make wrong-mode activation fairly uncommon, though selection errors are still possible. Selecting an operating mode is a quick recipe-load action with little delay. Mode selection is menu-driven, reducing skill demand though selecting the right one still requires process awareness. An incorrect mode can spoil a fill-finish run, a significant potential loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of adjusted setpoints that do not match approved process requirements
rows.plain: Make sure the operating settings that workers change match the approved process requirements. Incorrect settings can affect how the process performs.
rows.imp_rat: Adjusted setpoints not matching approved process requirements can push operation out of validated space, moderately relevant to correct functioning; severity is moderate since deviations are often bounded and monitored.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe management and access controls reduce mismatches, but manual entry still allows transcription errors to slip through occasionally. Verifying an adjusted setpoint against approved values adds a brief manual check step. Ensuring the entered value matches process requirements depends on operator accuracy and second-person verification. A mismatched setpoint can produce out-of-spec product, creating real scrap risk.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an authorized setpoint change fails to take effect
rows.plain: Make sure an approved change to a machine setting actually takes effect. Otherwise, the machine may continue operating at the wrong setting.
rows.imp_rat: An authorized setpoint change failing to take effect means the isolator runs on stale parameters, moderately relevant to maintaining correct conditions; severity is moderate since it may drift from spec but is often detectable via monitoring.
rows.sat_rat: Validated PLC/HMI control systems reliably apply authorized setpoint changes, with confirmation feedback, so silent failures are uncommon. Setpoint changes take effect essentially immediately in modern control systems. Making a setpoint change is a simple HMI action requiring little skill. A failed setpoint change is rare and seldom causes direct waste when it does occur.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of differential pressure instruments outside approved tolerance after calibration
rows.plain: Make sure pressure-measuring devices give readings within the approved range after they are checked and adjusted. Accurate pressure readings help confirm that the process is operating properly.
rows.imp_rat: Differential pressure out of tolerance after calibration undermines the pressure-cascade evidence central to containment, but the calibration miss is a controllable QA step and is usually caught before impacting production, capping severity.
rows.sat_rat: Mature calibration practice and traceable standards keep the vast majority of DP instruments in tolerance, making this among the best-served needs. Calibration of DP instruments is a defined, scheduled activity with predictable duration. Metrology technicians follow established procedures, so skill dependency is well-managed. Calibration labor and reference standards cost money but are budgeted routine.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that pressure measurements fall outside calibration tolerance during the enclosure leak test
rows.plain: Make sure the pressure readings used during the leak test are accurate enough. Inaccurate readings could give a false result about whether the enclosure leaks.
rows.imp_rat: Pressure measurements outside calibration tolerance during leak testing undermines confidence in containment verification, moderately relevant; severity is moderate because it corrupts test validity rather than causing an immediate breach.
rows.sat_rat: Traceable calibration programs and pre-test verification keep measurement instruments within tolerance the large majority of the time. Confirming instrument calibration before the leak test is a quick pre-check. Using calibrated reference gauges is routine and low-skill once trained. Out-of-tolerance instruments cause repeat tests, a limited cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that excessive test pressure damages an enclosure boundary component
rows.plain: Keep the pressure used to check for leaks low enough that it does not damage the enclosure or its parts. Too much pressure could cause damage during the test.
rows.imp_rat: Excessive test pressure damaging an enclosure boundary is a testing-phase concern only tangential to routine operation (lower relevance), though a damaged boundary component can compromise containment (moderate-high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Regulated test rigs, relief settings and procedural pressure caps make boundary damage from excessive test pressure rare. Ramping pressure within controlled limits adds only minor test time. Following defined pressure limits during the leak test is low-skill with proper equipment. Overpressure damage would be costly, so it is actively guarded against.

rows.source_job: Integrate process equipment within isolator enclosure
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of surfaces requiring decontamination that are made inaccessible by the equipment arrangement
rows.plain: Arrange the equipment so workers can reach every surface that needs cleaning and treatment. This helps prevent hidden areas from staying contaminated.
rows.imp_rat: Surfaces made inaccessible for decontamination by equipment arrangement can leave contamination reservoirs relevant to the isolator's clean-environment job (moderate relevance), with moderate severity since operators can often reach them with effort.
rows.sat_rat: Decon-coverage studies and airflow visualisation are maturing practice, yet inaccessible surfaces still recur and undermine cycle validation. Assessing decontamination accessibility during preparation is a modest addition to schedule. Judging which surfaces become shadowed or inaccessible for VHP or manual decon requires specialised decontamination-cycle expertise. Rearranging equipment to expose surfaces late is costly, but early review keeps costs low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of critical isolator zones omitted from representative monitoring coverage
rows.plain: Make sure all important enclosed work areas are included in the environmental checks, leaving as few areas as possible unmonitored. This helps detect conditions that could affect product safety.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting critical zones from monitoring coverage leaves contamination potentially undetected, relevant to assurance of containment; severity is moderate since gaps create latent blind spots rather than active failures.
rows.sat_rat: Risk-based monitoring layouts per Annex most critical zones, but omission of some critical locations from representative coverage remains a recurring inspection finding. Planning representative monitoring locations is a one-time design activity that does not delay preparation. Selecting locations that truly represent critical zones requires experienced contamination-control judgement and risk assessment. Additional monitoring points add modest sensor and media cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of monitoring points configured with parameters unsuitable for expected isolator conditions
rows.plain: Make sure each monitoring location uses settings that fit the conditions expected inside the protected area. This helps detect environmental problems accurately.
rows.imp_rat: Monitoring points configured for unsuitable conditions can mask deviations relevant to maintaining containment (moderate relevance), with severity limited because misconfiguration is typically detected during commissioning (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Standard configuration checklists catch many mismatches but unsuitable parameters routinely slip through until commissioning reveals them. Configuring monitoring parameters is a well-scheduled setup task that rarely creates delay. Correctly matching sensor parameters to expected isolator conditions requires specialist metrology and process knowledge that is often thinly spread. Reconfiguring a mis-set monitoring point is cheap in materials though it can waste engineering hours.

rows.source_job: Define and document material transfer protocols
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical transfer or decontamination steps omitted from the documented procedures
rows.plain: Leave out as few important steps as possible from the written instructions for moving materials and removing harmful contamination, so workers can follow the process safely and consistently.
rows.imp_rat: Critical transfer or decontamination steps omitted from documented procedures affects execution of containment-critical tasks (moderate relevance), and missing steps can lead to contamination if followed literally (moderate-high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Template-driven SOPs and review cycles catch most steps, but omissions of critical transfer or decon steps still surface during execution. Procedure authoring is time-consuming but scheduled well ahead of use. Capturing every critical transfer and decon step in documentation requires seasoned process authors who understand containment risk. Document revision is cheap relative to a missed step causing a deviation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of operator contact with critical surfaces during rehearsed transfers
rows.plain: Avoid touching important clean surfaces during practice transfers, so workers can learn the task without contaminating them.
rows.imp_rat: Operator contact with critical surfaces during rehearsed transfers raises contamination risk relevant to the CFJ (moderate relevance), but rehearsals are non-production and the consequences are contained (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Established mock-up and glove-port rehearsal practice reliably surfaces contact points, though residual operator-dependent contacts still occur. Rehearsal transfers add setup time but do not slow the core preparation once the ergonomic layout is validated.
