Inability to restore extends downtime and may force costly rebuild, an infrequent but heavy loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of measurement error caused by enclosure temperature drift during the test
rows.imp_rat: Measurement error from enclosure temperature drift affects test accuracy, moderately relevant to qualification; consequences are re-testing rather than product harm, so severity is low. Ceiling-bound.
rows.sat_rat: Environmental stabilization periods and temperature-compensated instruments keep drift error small in most controlled test conditions. Allowing enclosure temperature to stabilize before testing adds waiting time but is standard practice. Recognizing and controlling for temperature drift requires metrology awareness during the test. Drift-induced measurement error may force a retest, a limited occasional cost.

rows.source_job: Connect utilities and ducting to isolator
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of misalignment between facility ducting or utility lines and corresponding isolator ports
rows.imp_rat: Misalignment between facility ducting and isolator ports triggers rework and fit-up effort but does not directly defeat containment; relevance is moderate and severity low under the batch cap.
rows.sat_rat: Facility-to-equipment interface misalignment is a persistent commissioning problem because facility and vendor drawings often diverge, so it recurs often. Reconciling facility ducting and utility lines to isolator ports frequently causes field delays while mismatches are reworked. Coordinating multi-trade interfaces requires experienced field engineers reading combined MEP and equipment drawings. Field modification of ducting or ports to close alignment gaps is expensive rework.

rows.source_job: Connect utilities and ducting to isolator
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a required utility feed is unavailable at the isolator connection point during commissioning
rows.imp_rat: An unavailable utility feed at commissioning is a scheduling/readiness delay peripheral to the containment CFJ; severity is modest since it delays startup rather than compromising containment.
rows.sat_rat: Commissioning readiness checklists help, but utility feeds unavailable at the connection point remain a common commissioning-day surprise. Confirming utility availability at connection points is a scheduled commissioning check. Verifying utility readiness requires facility coordination rather than deep specialist skill. A missing feed at commissioning causes costly standby delays but avoidance is cheap.

rows.source_job: Verify containment integrity of assembled isolator
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required test instrumentation lacks valid calibration when containment testing is scheduled
rows.imp_rat: Lacking valid calibration when containment testing is scheduled mainly causes delay rather than containment loss; relevance to the core job is indirect and severity is low since it is a reschedulable administrative snag.
rows.sat_rat: Established calibration-management systems make a lapsed instrument at test time uncommon, though manual coordination gaps do still let it happen. Calibration due-date tracking via CMMS or calibration schedules usually flags instruments in time, though last-minute scheduling still causes occasional scrambles. Metrology and QA staff routinely manage calibration cycles, so little unusual expertise is required beyond following established SOPs. Recalibrating or renting a substitute instrument at short notice adds modest cost, but this is a well-budgeted routine expense.

rows.source_job: Complete installation and operational qualification documentation
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of IQ or OQ checks that fail predefined acceptance criteria
rows.imp_rat: IQ/OQ checks failing acceptance criteria are expected findings surfaced by design; relevance is moderate and severity low since failures are meant to be caught and corrected before use.
rows.sat_rat: Even with pre-qualification checks, a nontrivial fraction of IQ/OQ items fail acceptance criteria on first pass, making this a recurring pain. Failed IQ/OQ checks trigger investigation and retest cycles that extend the qualification timeline significantly. Anticipating and preventing failures requires seasoned validation engineers whose judgement is in short supply. Each failed check consumes retest labor, consumables, and sometimes vendor callbacks, adding meaningful cost.

rows.source_job: Complete installation and operational qualification documentation
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of discrepancies between the installed isolator configuration and the drawings or specifications referenced by the qualification protocols
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancies between installed configuration and qualification drawings create documentation ambiguity but are usually reconciled in protocol review; relevance is moderate and severity low relative to containment integrity.
rows.sat_rat: As-built drift from design is a chronic source of qualification findings despite red-line and change-control practices, so discrepancies recur regularly. Walk-down comparisons of installed configuration against drawings are time-consuming and often compressed into tight commissioning windows. Detecting drawing-vs-as-built discrepancies demands experienced engineers who can read P&IDs and spot subtle deviations. Late-discovered discrepancies force rework and change orders that are expensive to correct on site.

rows.source_job: Integrate process equipment within isolator enclosure
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of process equipment colliding with enclosure features or blocking door closure during installation
rows.imp_rat: Collision of process equipment with enclosure features or blocking door closure during installation is a one-time setup defect, only peripherally tied to the CFJ of ongoing containment (low relevance); consequences are typically caught and remedied during commissioning (moderate-low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Clash detection and layout review reduce collisions, but interference and blocked doors still surface where coordination was incomplete. Collisions or door-closure blockages discovered during install stall work while clearances are resolved. Anticipating clearance conflicts benefits from experienced planners doing 3D/clash review, a skill not always applied. Moving equipment or modifying enclosures to restore clearance is costly late in installation.

rows.source_job: Configure user access and role assignments
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an isolator action cannot be attributed to a specific authorized individual
rows.imp_rat: Inability to attribute an isolator action to a specific individual is a data-integrity/audit concern peripheral to physical containment (low relevance); it can create compliance exposure but not immediate product or personnel harm (moderate-low severity).
rows.sat_rat: During Preparation, attribution controls are being set up rather than proven, so gaps in role/account provisioning still leave some actions unattributable. Attributing actions via login/audit trails is instantaneous once access-control systems are configured. Operating within a badge or login attribution scheme requires little added skill from users. Access-control and audit-trail systems have upfront cost but low marginal cost per action.

rows.source_job: Calibrate monitoring sensors against traceable references
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a reference standard has expired or unverifiable traceability during isolator sensor calibration
rows.imp_rat: An expired or untraceable reference standard during sensor calibration can bias readings, but this ambiguity is peripheral to the containment job and severity is limited as it is typically caught during calibration checks.
rows.sat_rat: Formal reference-standard control programs keep most references valid and traceable, though expiry surprises still occur when logs are not tightly maintained. Verifying reference-standard validity is a quick documentary check when traceability records are organized, but chasing certificates can delay setup. Metrology technicians know how to read NIST-traceable certificates, so the judgement required is standard rather than scarce. Maintaining certified reference standards carries recurring recertification cost that is normally planned for.

rows.stk: Receiving/logistics staff and procurement or quality personnel
rows.source_job: Unpack, inspect, and verify delivered isolator
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a mismatch between the delivered and ordered isolator configurations remains unidentified before installation
rows.imp_rat: An unidentified mismatch between delivered and ordered configuration (relevance) is a one-time procurement/installation verification issue, peripheral to routine containment operation; severity is contained because it is caught during commissioning validation. Ceiling-limited.
rows.sat_rat: FAT and receiving checks catch most mismatches, but subtle configuration deviations from the order still occasionally go unidentified. Verifying delivered configuration against the purchase order is a bounded receiving activity. Detecting configuration mismatches requires someone who understands the ordered spec well enough to spot subtle differences. An unnoticed configuration mismatch drives expensive late rework or rejection, so verification pays off.

rows.stk: Receiving/logistics staff and procurement or quality personnel
rows.source_job: Unpack, inspect, and verify delivered isolator
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that missing required isolator components or documents remain unidentified before installation
rows.imp_rat: Unidentified missing components or documents before installation is mainly a readiness/logistics error, moderately relevant to commissioning; consequences are schedule slips, not containment breach. Ceiling-bound.
rows.sat_rat: Packing-list and document-checklist practices catch most shortfalls, though incomplete manifests still let some omissions slip through. Checking deliverables against packing lists is a routine receiving task that adds little time. Reconciling components and documents against a manifest requires attention but not scarce skill. Finding missing parts late causes costly delays, so the inexpensive check has high value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of withdrawn samples assigned to the wrong batch or sampling point
rows.imp_rat: Misassigning withdrawn samples to the wrong batch or sampling point is a sampling-traceability error tangential to the isolator's containment CFJ; severity is moderate given potential mix-ups but not catastrophic to containment.
rows.sat_rat: Manual labeling and sample-log practices leave a persistent mixup rate that barcode/LIMS integration only partially closes. Labeling and logging sample-to-batch assignment adds minor time at each withdrawal point. Correct assignment depends on operator care in labeling and tracking multiple sampling points, an attention-heavy task. A misassigned sample can invalidate test results and force resampling or batch reassessment, incurring notable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of withdrawn samples that are not representative of the process material at the designated sampling point
rows.imp_rat: Non-representative samples affect quality-testing accuracy rather than the isolator's containment job; severity is moderate as bad data could pass defective batches but is tangential to containment.
rows.sat_rat: Sampling representativeness inside constrained isolator workspaces is hard to guarantee and remains a recurring source of questionable data. Obtaining a representative sample through glove ports can require careful timing and technique that slows sampling. Representative withdrawal at the designated point demands considerable operator technique and process understanding, a real skill dependency. Non-representative samples yield misleading data and can trigger unnecessary or missed rejections, an expensive quality risk.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of product or component transfer blockages during sustained high-volume operation
rows.imp_rat: Transfer blockages during high-volume operation reduce productivity but do not compromise containment; relevance is modest and severity is low as blockages are recoverable.
rows.sat_rat: Transfer systems are not fully optimized for sustained high-volume flow, so blockage frequency stays a chronic operational gap. Repeated blockages during high-volume runs create cumulative stoppages that erode throughput. Sustained smooth transfer relies on operator technique and load management more than on the equipment. Frequent blockages under high volume drive lost output and labor for clearing, a recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of blockage at the contained discharge interface during process waste removal
rows.imp_rat: Blockage at the contained discharge interface during waste removal slows throughput but is an operational delay; its relevance to the containment job is secondary and severity low as it is clearable.
rows.sat_rat: Discharge interfaces such as continuous liners clog unpredictably, and current designs offer limited built-in anti-blockage mitigation. Blockages at the contained discharge interface directly stall waste removal and can idle the whole operation until cleared. Clearing a contained discharge blockage through glove ports without breaching containment demands significant manual finesse. Downtime and potential batch impact from a stuck discharge carry real productivity cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required operating-state checks that remain inconclusive during recovery
rows.imp_rat: Inconclusive operating-state checks during recovery (relevance) delay restart but rarely cause direct containment failure; severity is limited because a check that remains inconclusive typically triggers a conservative hold rather than a release. Below the batch importance threshold.
rows.sat_rat: Sensor coverage and self-diagnostics leave gaps, so some operating-state checks remain inconclusive and force conservative repeat actions. Inconclusive state checks stall recovery while operators re-run or manually verify conditions. Interpreting ambiguous sensor readings during recovery demands seasoned judgement not evenly distributed across shifts. Re-verification consumes labor and sometimes wasted material when a check cannot confirm a safe state.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a candidate judged suitable does not fit the allocated space or access path
rows.imp_rat: A candidate not fitting the allocated space or access path is a pre-purchase sizing error peripheral to run-time containment (low relevance); it forces reselection or rework before use, a moderate-low consequence, kept under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Dimensional and access-path checks are mature and usually reliable, though rigging and door-clearance surprises occasionally occur. Verifying fit and access path against dimensions is a quick, well-defined check. Comparing candidate dimensions to space and access routes needs basic drafting and logistics skill that is commonly available. The check is very low-cost versus the expense of a unit that cannot be installed.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that reference claims about containment performance cannot be authenticated
rows.imp_rat: Inability to authenticate reference containment-performance claims is largely an administrative/verification concern, peripheral to the day-to-day containment job; severity is modest as it affects trust in documentation rather than immediate containment.
rows.sat_rat: Reference claims are hard to authenticate independently and often taken at face value, so unverifiable claims regularly persist. Contacting references and authenticating claims takes time and is often abbreviated. Authenticating containment-performance references requires knowing what to ask and how to interpret answers, a specialized skill. Reference verification is low-cost but frequently skipped due to effort rather than money.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of candidate isolator configurations with missing sterile fill-finish suitability information
rows.imp_rat: Missing sterile fill-finish suitability information on candidate configurations is a selection-phase data gap; it affects early planning more than containment performance, so both proxies remain low.
rows.sat_rat: Vendor data on fill-finish suitability is frequently incomplete, so candidates with missing information are a recurring problem. Gathering suitability information from vendors on each configuration can be slow and iterative. Recognizing which sterile fill-finish suitability information is missing requires aseptic-process familiarity. Information gathering is largely a labor cost with little material expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material and personnel flow conflicts missed during layout evaluation
rows.imp_rat: Material and personnel flow conflicts missed during layout evaluation is a planning-stage defect peripheral to the containment barrier itself (low relevance); missed conflicts create workflow inefficiency or rework (moderate-low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Manual layout reviews miss subtle personnel and material flow conflicts fairly often, making this a recurring gap. Flow analysis across material and personnel paths is time-consuming and often compressed. Detecting flow conflicts requires experienced process-flow and cleanroom-layout designers whose availability is limited. Layout evaluation is cheap relative to the operational cost of crossed flows discovered in operation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of operator reach and maintenance-access limitations missed before purchase
rows.imp_rat: Operator reach and maintenance-access limitations missed before purchase is a procurement-stage oversight tangential to run-time containment (low relevance); consequences are ergonomic and maintenance difficulties, moderate-low, kept under the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Standard practice relies on drawing reviews and vendor claims rather than mock-ups, so reach and access limitations are routinely missed until hardware arrives. Ergonomic and maintenance-access reviews are typically deferred until factory acceptance or install, so the time to catch problems balloons well past purchase. Spotting reach-envelope and maintenance-access limits pre-purchase demands scarce human-factors and containment-engineering judgement that most acquisition teams lack. Missed access limitations surface as costly rework or retrofits after commitment rather than as line-item costs during acquisition.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of mandatory quality, containment, acceptance, or liability obligations omitted from the executed contract
rows.imp_rat: Omitted contractual obligations are administrative/liability errors peripheral to the physical containment job, so relevance is low; severity can be meaningful in disputes but does not affect operation. Ceiling-limited.
rows.sat_rat: Mature URS-to-contract mapping and QA checklists catch most mandatory obligations, but consistency gaps mean some still slip through. Legal and quality contract review adds time but is a routine part of the acquisition cycle for GMP equipment. Capturing all containment, acceptance, and liability obligations depends on experienced quality and contracts staff who are unevenly available across buyers. Omitted obligations translate into uncovered liability and expensive later disputes, though standard templates limit gross omissions.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that factory acceptance test results are invalid because measurement instruments lack current calibration status
rows.imp_rat: Invalid FAT results due to uncalibrated instruments are a pre-delivery quality concern; relevance is modest since containment job itself is unaffected, and severity is low because errors are typically re-tested before acceptance.
rows.sat_rat: Calibration-certificate checks before FAT are routine practice, so invalid FAT results from out-of-calibration instruments are infrequent. Confirming instrument calibration before FAT is a brief pre-test verification. Checking measurement-instrument calibration status is a standard procedural task requiring modest skill. Verification is inexpensive against the cost of repeating a FAT.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required calibration records or approved protocol prerequisites are unavailable when requalification testing begins
rows.imp_rat: Missing calibration records or protocol prerequisites at requalification start delays work; relevance is indirect to the containment job and severity is low as it is a reschedulable prerequisite gap.
rows.sat_rat: Document management systems help, but fragmented records still frequently leave prerequisites missing at the moment testing should begin. Chasing missing calibration records or protocol approvals at test start causes significant delay. Assembling the correct prerequisite documentation depends on QA coordination rather than technical skill. A halted requalification for want of paperwork wastes scheduled resources, a recurring moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of requalification records invalidated by protocol deviations or incomplete evidence
rows.imp_rat: Requalification records invalidated by protocol deviations is an administrative rework issue only loosely connected to the containment CFJ (low relevance); consequences are repeated paperwork and delay rather than contamination (moderate-low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Protocol deviations and incomplete evidence remain a chronic source of invalidated records despite review steps. Records invalidated by deviations force re-testing that consumes substantial extra time. Producing deviation-free, complete evidence demands experienced execution and thorough documentation discipline. Invalidated requalification records mean repeated test campaigns, a significant recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of readiness confirmations with missing attribution, status, or sequence context
rows.imp_rat: Readiness confirmations with missing attribution weakens the record, but ranked below prior batch and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Signature/timestamp requirements ensure most confirmations carry full attribution context. Capturing attribution and sequence adds documentation time. Complete attribution requires procedural discipline. Minor documentation labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of aseptic interventions omitted from processing-zone records
rows.imp_rat: Aseptic interventions omitted from records leaves gaps in the traceability of separation events, but the ceiling caps importance at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Batch record requirements capture most interventions, though occasional omissions occur. Recording each intervention adds time. Complete documentation requires procedural discipline. Documentation labor is a routine cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of recorded zone activities containing incorrect execution details
rows.imp_rat: Recorded activities with incorrect execution details misleads reviewers, but ranked below prior batch and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Review and verification catch most incorrect details, but manual entry errors persist. Accurate detail entry takes time. Recording correct execution details relies on attention. Errors trigger investigation cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of zone activities linked to an incorrect batch, location, or intervention
rows.imp_rat: Activities linked to the wrong batch/location undermines traceability, but the ceiling caps importance at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Batch record structure keeps most activity linkages correct, with occasional errors. Correct linkage takes documentation time. Linking activities to correct batch/location requires care. Mislinks cause investigation cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conflicts between processing-zone records and related batch documentation
rows.imp_rat: Conflicts between zone records and batch documentation creates evidentiary inconsistency, but capped at 2.0 by ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Cross-checks during batch review resolve most record conflicts, though some surface late. Reconciling records takes review time. Detecting conflicts between records requires attentive review. Conflicts trigger investigation cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of processing-zone records being unavailable during batch review or investigation
rows.imp_rat: Processing-zone records unavailable during review impedes disposition, but ranked below prior batch and capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Controlled document systems make records reliably available for review and investigation. Retrieving records is quick with electronic systems. Record retrieval requires little skill. Document management systems cost money but are standard.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required tools, components, or consumables missing or incorrect at run start
rows.imp_rat: Missing tools/consumables at run start is a readiness/logistics concern with low relevance to containment; low severity.
rows.sat_rat: Kitting and setup checklists make missing/incorrect items at start uncommon. Pre-run kitting checks add startup time. Verifying tools and consumables follows checklists. Missing items cause delays and rushed substitutions.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of materials, components, or waste items left after restoration
rows.imp_rat: Leftover materials after restoration is largely housekeeping with low relevance to the CFJ; low severity, easily remedied.
rows.sat_rat: Reconciliation checklists catch most left-behind items, though occasional misses happen. Item reconciliation adds time at restoration. Counting and removing all items needs procedural discipline. Left items may force re-cleaning cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of restored-zone conditions remaining outside specified readiness limits
rows.imp_rat: Restored-zone conditions remaining outside readiness limits touches contamination control, but the ordering caps importance at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Recovery to specified limits is predictable, though duration varies with cycle. Time for restored conditions to reach limits (aeration, equilibration) is a defined but real delay. Managing recovery to limits requires process knowledge. Extended recovery consumes utilities and time.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of discrepancy between recorded and actual internal environmental conditions
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancy between recorded and actual environmental conditions affects monitoring reliability, but ranked below prior batch so capped.
rows.sat_rat: Continuous automated environmental logging keeps recorded and actual conditions closely aligned. Automated recording eliminates delay in capturing conditions. Automated data capture reduces skill dependence. Data systems add cost but are standard.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of production runs with missing or inconsistent boundary-integrity records
rows.imp_rat: Missing boundary-integrity records leaves separation unverified, but capped at 2.0 by the batch ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Integrity results are routinely documented, so missing boundary-integrity records are uncommon. Recording integrity results adds time. Consistent record-keeping requires discipline. Missing records force reruns or holds, a cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of doubt about which approved instruction governs the current aseptic intervention
rows.imp_rat: Doubt about which instruction governs the current intervention risks execution errors, but ranked below prior batch and capped.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of uncertainty that product-environment separation was maintained after an aseptic move
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty that separation was maintained after a move relates to contamination assurance, but the ceiling caps importance at 2.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of identifying an incorrect cause for the cycle deviation
rows.imp_rat: Identifying an incorrect cause for a cycle deviation risks recurrence, but this diagnostic error is ranked below prior batch and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Incorrect-cause identification is common given noisy, incomplete cycle data. Chasing an incorrect cause wastes investigation time. Distinguishing correct from incorrect causes requires strong analytical skill and data. Misdiagnosis drives costly ineffective fixes.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required cycle-data fields that are missing or conflicting
rows.imp_rat: Missing or conflicting cycle-data fields hampers deviation diagnosis, but the ceiling limits both proxies to 2.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic batch records reduce missing fields but conflicts and gaps still appear during diagnosis. Missing or conflicting data fields slow diagnosis while gaps are chased. Recognizing and reconciling conflicting fields needs data expertise. Incomplete data drives costly re-investigation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of residual-decontaminant removal after exposure
rows.imp_rat: Duration of residual-decontaminant removal is a throughput/timing concern with low relevance to actual containment; consequences are minor delay, low severity.
rows.sat_rat: Catalytic aeration reliably removes residual within predictable times. Residual-decontaminant removal after exposure is a significant timed aeration phase. Aeration is automated to an endpoint, needing little operator skill. Long aeration consumes energy and isolator occupancy.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of repeat decontamination cycles required after failed acceptance
rows.imp_rat: Repeat decon cycles after failure is a throughput/rework concern with low relevance to the CFJ; low severity.
rows.sat_rat: Repeat cycles are an accepted fallback but their frequency reflects unresolved efficacy variability. Repeat cycles after failed acceptance are a major source of lost time. Deciding on and setting up repeat cycles requires experienced judgement. Repeat cycles waste decontaminant, utilities and occupancy.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that selected decontamination requirements conflict with the planned product separation
rows.imp_rat: Decontamination requirements conflicting with planned separation could undermine the zone, but the ceiling caps importance at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Requirement conflicts are usually caught in design but occasionally surface during execution. Late discovery of a conflict between decontamination and separation forces rework delays. Foreseeing conflicts between decontamination requirements and product separation needs process-design expertise. Conflicts discovered late waste materials and time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved discrepancies between physical zone conditions and readiness records
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancies between physical conditions and readiness records risks proceeding on false status, but capped at 2.0 by ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Discrepancies between actual zone conditions and readiness records recur and are only manually reconciled. Resolving discrepancies between physical state and records delays readiness sign-off. Detecting mismatches requires diligent cross-checking skill. Reconciliation effort is a labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of gaps or conflicts in evidence required to confirm zone integrity
rows.imp_rat: Gaps or conflicts in zone-integrity evidence undermines confirmation of separation, but ranked below prior batch and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Integrity evidence is usually complete but occasional gaps or conflicts require resolution before confirmation. Gaps in integrity evidence delay release while filled. Assembling complete integrity evidence needs procedural knowledge. Integrity testing consumables and time are recurring costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of gaps or conflicts in evidence supporting restored decontamination capability
rows.imp_rat: Evidence gaps supporting restored decontamination capability weakens resumption defensibility, but the ceiling limits importance to 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Evidence supporting restored decontamination capability often contains gaps that slow confident restart. Gaps in restored-capability evidence delay return to service. Judging sufficiency of restoration evidence requires expert review. Requalification testing to close gaps is costly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of processing-zone unavailability after cycle failure
rows.imp_rat: Zone unavailability after cycle failure is a throughput concern with low relevance to the CFJ; low severity.
rows.sat_rat: Recovery from cycle failure is slow and variable with limited standardized fast-path practice. Zone unavailability after cycle failure directly delays production for extended periods. Restoring availability quickly needs troubleshooting expertise. Downtime is a significant lost-capacity cost.

rows.source_job: In Command of the Running Cycle
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of being unable to explain whether the running cycle remains controlled
rows.imp_rat: Being unable to explain whether the running cycle is controlled undermines real-time assurance, but capped at 2.0 by ordering.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of efficacy indicators supporting conflicting release conclusions for the same cycle
rows.imp_rat: Efficacy indicators supporting conflicting release conclusions risks disposition errors, but ranked below prior batch and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Different indicators for the same cycle can support conflicting conclusions, a recurring release-review complication. Conflicting indicators trigger additional review time before release. Adjudicating conflicting efficacy indicators requires expert judgement. Conflict resolution and possible reruns add cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required cycle records missing or unreadable during deviation diagnosis
rows.imp_rat: Missing cycle records during diagnosis obstructs root-cause work, but capped at 2.0 by the batch ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Automated data capture makes fully missing records uncommon, though readability and completeness gaps persist. Missing or unreadable records delay deviation diagnosis. Locating and interpreting records requires familiarity with data systems. Data-management systems cost money but reduce record loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of using the wrong validated parameter set as the diagnostic baseline
rows.imp_rat: Using the wrong validated baseline for diagnosis misdirects investigation, but capped at 2.0 by the ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Version-controlled recipes reduce but do not eliminate wrong-baseline selection during diagnosis. Using the wrong baseline set delays diagnosis when discovered. Selecting the correct validated parameter set as baseline requires familiarity with validation records. Wrong baselines cause misdirected effort and cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of discrepancy between recorded cycle values and actual isolator conditions
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancy between recorded and actual cycle values undermines trust in decontamination data, but the batch ceiling limits both proxies to 2.
rows.sat_rat: Sensor drift and placement mean recorded values can diverge from actual isolator conditions, a recurring accuracy gap. Reconciling recorded versus actual conditions consumes investigation time. Detecting discrepancies between records and reality needs instrumentation expertise. Sensor calibration to keep records accurate is a recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of chronological misalignment across cycle, utility, and environmental records
rows.imp_rat: Chronological misalignment across records complicates deviation reconstruction, but ranked below prior batch and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Chronological misalignment across separate record systems is a frequent obstacle to accurate reconstruction. Aligning misaligned timestamps across systems adds investigation time. Correlating records across cycle, utility and environmental systems needs data-handling skill. Time synchronization infrastructure carries modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of transient diagnostic evidence being overwritten or lost before review
rows.imp_rat: Transient diagnostic evidence overwritten before review loses causal data, but capped at 2.0 by ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Transient diagnostic signals are frequently overwritten before review due to limited data resolution and retention. Lost transient evidence forces slower reconstruction from other sources. Preserving transient diagnostic evidence requires foresight and system knowledge. High-resolution data retention adds storage cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that cascading alarms obscure the initiating event of the cycle deviation
rows.imp_rat: Cascading alarms obscuring the initiating event complicates deviation diagnosis, but ranked below prior batch and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Alarm-cascade obscuring of the root event is a common, poorly mitigated problem in current alarm systems. Cascading alarms lengthen the time to isolate the initiating event. Cutting through alarm floods to find the trigger requires expert interpretation. Delayed diagnosis extends costly downtime.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sterility records with unverifiable origins or alteration histories for the production lot under review
rows.imp_rat: Sterility records with unverifiable origins undermine data integrity, but ranked below prior batch and capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Audit-trailed sterility records make unverifiable origins or alteration histories rare under modern data-integrity practice. Audit trails enable quick origin verification. Data-integrity governance requires trained oversight. Validated systems carry standard cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of sterility evidence records attributed to the wrong production lot
rows.imp_rat: Sterility evidence attributed to the wrong lot risks disposition errors, but capped at 2.0 by the batch ordering.
rows.sat_rat: LIMS lot tracking and barcoding make misattribution of sterility evidence to the wrong lot quite rare. Lot attribution is automated at capture, saving reconciliation time. Lot labeling discipline is procedural and low-skill. LIMS and barcode systems are standard cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required isolator compliance records that are missing, inaccurate, or inconsistent
rows.imp_rat: Missing or inconsistent compliance records affect defensibility, but the batch ceiling limits importance to 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Structured compliance record systems keep most required records complete and consistent, with periodic audits catching gaps. Record completeness checks are built into workflows. Maintaining compliance records is procedural once trained. Document management systems are standard cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of isolator compliance deviations unresolved after mandated deadlines
rows.imp_rat: Deviations unresolved after deadlines is an administrative-timing concern with low relevance to containment; low severity.
rows.sat_rat: CAPA systems track deadlines and escalate overdue items, keeping most deviations resolved on time though backlogs persist. CAPA deadline tracking drives timely closure but overdue items still accumulate. Managing resolution requires coordination skill but not scarce expertise. CAPA management systems are standard overhead.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of contamination-control records with missing, invalid, or conflicting entries
rows.imp_rat: Missing or conflicting contamination-control records affect defensibility, but the batch ceiling limits this to 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Contamination-control records under validated systems with entry checks reliably minimize missing or conflicting entries. Electronic entry validation flags issues immediately. Record discipline is trained and procedural. EDMS and MES systems are standard overhead.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required readiness records missing or incomplete at review
rows.imp_rat: Missing readiness records at review leaves status unconfirmed, but the ceiling limits importance to 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Batch-record checklists catch most gaps, but missing or incomplete records at review still surface regularly. Missing readiness records force chasing documents, delaying review. Assembling complete records is administrative rather than skill-intensive. Incomplete records cause investigation and delayed release, a cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of aseptic-disruption recurrence after production resumption
rows.imp_rat: Aseptic-disruption recurrence after resumption bears directly on maintaining the closed zone, but the batch ceiling limits both proxies to 2.
rows.sat_rat: CAPA processes address recurrence but frequently fail to eliminate it, so repeat disruptions are common. Recurrence after resumption is a repeated-event problem, not a per-instance timing issue. Preventing recurrence requires strong root-cause analysis, a skill unevenly applied. Each recurrence re-triggers downtime and loss, compounding cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required rationale and supporting evidence absent from the production-resumption authorization record
rows.imp_rat: Missing rationale in the resumption authorization record weakens defensibility, but capped at 2.0 by ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Structured authorization forms prompt required fields, so absent rationale is fairly uncommon though gaps still occur. Completing the authorization record with full rationale adds documentation time at sign-off. Capturing adequate rationale requires the overseer to articulate reasoning clearly, a moderate skill demand. Missing rationale triggers audit findings and rework, a compliance cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conflicting production directions communicated across personnel or shifts
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting production directions across shifts risks execution errors, but the ceiling limits both proxies to 2.
rows.sat_rat: Shift handoff protocols reduce conflicts, but cross-shift direction conflicts remain a recurring reality. Conflicting directions across shifts cause confusion that delays coordinated action. Avoiding conflicting directions depends on communication skill and coordination discipline. Conflicting directions waste effort and can spoil product.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of aseptic interventions having incomplete compliance records
rows.imp_rat: Aseptic interventions with incomplete compliance records leaves gaps, but ranked below prior batch and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Intervention logging is standard, but records are frequently incomplete when interventions are frequent or rushed. Incomplete intervention records force reconstruction, delaying review. Complete compliance recording is a discipline task more than a skill task. Incomplete records cause rework and possible batch impact, a cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of conflict among approved instructions governing the same aseptic activity
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting instructions governing the same aseptic activity risks execution errors, but ranked below prior items so capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Document control aims for consistency, but conflicting approved instructions for the same activity still occur. Conflicts among instructions surface at use and slow execution while operators seek clarification. Recognizing that two approved instructions conflict requires familiarity with the full instruction set. Conflicting instructions cause errors and rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved procedural deviations omitted from shift handoffs
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved deviations omitted from handoffs risks continuity failures, but ranked below prior batch and capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Handoff checklists and logs help, but unresolved deviations are still occasionally omitted under time pressure. Deviations dropped from handoffs surface later, delaying their resolution. Ensuring all open deviations transfer relies on the outgoing overseer's thoroughness. Missed deviations can advance batches and cause loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of recurrence for previously identified procedural deviations across shifts and batches
rows.imp_rat: Recurrence of previously identified deviations signals ineffective control, but capped at 2.0 by ordering.
rows.sat_rat: CAPA and trending target recurrence, but previously identified deviations still recur across shifts and batches. Recurrence of known deviations is a repeated pattern, not a single-event timing concern. Preventing recurrence depends on effective corrective action and training reinforcement, unevenly delivered. Repeat deviations generate repeated investigation and loss cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of assigned personnel lacking qualification for their planned aseptic activities
rows.imp_rat: Personnel lacking qualification for aseptic activities risks contamination, but the ceiling limits importance to 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Training-record systems and assignment checks make unqualified assignment uncommon where records are maintained. Qualification checks before assignment add little delay when records are current. Verifying qualification is a straightforward records check needing little expertise. Assigning unqualified personnel risks batch loss and remediation cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of assigned personnel whose qualification scope excludes their assigned isolator tasks
rows.imp_rat: Personnel qualification scope excluding assigned tasks is a similar competency gap, but capped at 2.0 by ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Role-based qualification matrices generally catch scope gaps, though task-specific exclusions are occasionally overlooked. Confirming qualification scope covers the specific task is a quick pre-assignment step. Matching qualification scope to task requires knowing task-specific requirements, a modest skill. Scope mismatches can invalidate work and force repeats, a cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a batch is scheduled to begin before required aseptic recovery conditions can be restored
rows.imp_rat: Scheduling a batch before recovery conditions are restored risks contamination, but ranked below prior batch and capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Scheduling tools rarely reflect real recovery status, so mismatches between schedule and readiness recur. Scheduling a batch before recovery conditions can be restored guarantees a wait, directly wasting scheduled time. Estimating whether recovery will complete in time takes planning judgement that is often optimistic. Premature scheduling idles resources and disrupts sequences.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sequencing decisions based on conflicting batch-readiness evidence
rows.imp_rat: Sequencing decisions on conflicting readiness evidence risks errors, but ranked below prior batch and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Batch-readiness data is fragmented, so conflicting-evidence sequencing decisions occur with some regularity. Sequencing decisions on conflicting evidence cause rescheduling churn that costs time. Sorting out which readiness evidence to trust needs experienced judgement. Wrong sequencing wastes changeover effort and hold time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of zone decontamination cycles failing acceptance criteria and requiring repetition
rows.imp_rat: Decon cycles failing acceptance and requiring repeat is a rework/throughput concern with low relevance to the CFJ; low severity.
rows.sat_rat: Validated decontamination cycles pass reliably when set up correctly, so failures needing repetition are occasional. A failed decontamination cycle forces a full repeat, adding hours of delay. Achieving a passing cycle depends on correct load configuration and setup, requiring competent preparation. Repeat cycles consume sterilant, energy, and time, a direct waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of process requirements translated into incorrect functional separation criteria for the closed decontaminated zone
rows.imp_rat: Requirements translated into incorrect separation criteria could misdefine the zone, but the batch ceiling caps importance at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Requirements are sometimes translated into incorrect separation criteria without systematic verification. Verifying correct translation of requirements into criteria adds review cycles. Correct translation requires expert judgement to avoid subtle misinterpretation. Incorrect criteria drive costly wrong specifications.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of relevant operating and upset states excluded from the separation criteria for the closed decontaminated zone
rows.imp_rat: Excluding operating/upset states from separation criteria weakens the closed-zone definition, but the batch ceiling limits both proxies.
rows.sat_rat: Upset and transient states are frequently under-represented in separation criteria, a persistent weakness. Enumerating all operating and upset states lengthens criteria development. Anticipating upset states demands experienced hazard-analysis judgement. Excluded states cause failures whose remediation is costly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of product and material transfer interfaces with undefined separation conditions
rows.imp_rat: Undefined separation conditions at transfer interfaces relates to ingress control, but the ranking holds importance at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Some transfer interfaces still reach specification with undefined separation conditions, a recurring but partly-managed gap. Defining separation conditions for every transfer interface takes deliberate specification time. Specifying transfer-interface conditions requires aseptic-transfer expertise. Undefined interfaces cause costly integration rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of functional separation criteria lacking measurable acceptance conditions
rows.imp_rat: Separation criteria lacking measurable acceptance conditions impedes verification, but ranked below prior batch and capped.
rows.sat_rat: A meaningful share of separation criteria still lack measurable acceptance conditions. Adding measurable acceptance conditions to each criterion takes deliberate effort. Making criteria measurable requires skill in defining testable limits. Unmeasurable criteria cause disputes and rework costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved conflicts among process, product-protection, decontamination, and facility requirements affecting separation
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved conflicts among requirements affecting separation is an instability concern, but capped by the ordering at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: No standard mechanism reconciles these competing requirements, so conflicts routinely persist unresolved. Resolving multi-domain conflicts affecting separation stretches the timeline. Reconciling process, product-protection, decontamination and facility demands needs experienced negotiation and judgement. Unresolved conflicts drive compromises that raise lifecycle cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of critical sterile fill-finish requirements absent from the evaluation criteria
rows.imp_rat: Critical fill-finish requirements absent from evaluation criteria leaves coverage gaps, but the ceiling limits importance to 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Critical fill-finish requirements are sometimes absent from evaluation criteria, a recurring coverage gap. Ensuring all critical fill-finish requirements are in the criteria adds review time. Knowing the full set of critical requirements demands process expertise. Missing requirements cause expensive post-purchase gaps.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved conflicts among sterility, operator access, material flow, and fill-finish requirements
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved conflicts among sterility/access/flow requirements is an instability concern relevant to separation, but capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: These conflicts are chronically under-managed, leaving recurring unresolved tensions. Balancing sterility, access, flow and fill-finish demands prolongs specification. Trading off these competing needs requires scarce integrative expertise. Poorly resolved trade-offs raise operating cost or force redesign.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which criterion weightings misrepresent sterile fill-finish priorities
rows.imp_rat: Criterion weightings misrepresenting priorities is a selection-quality issue with low relevance to running containment; low severity.
rows.sat_rat: Weighting schemes are typically ad hoc and rarely validated against true priorities, so misrepresentation is common. Reworking weightings that misrepresent priorities adds evaluation iterations. Correctly weighting fill-finish priorities is a judgement-heavy exercise dependent on experienced decision-makers. Mis-weighted criteria can steer the purchase toward the wrong system at significant cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of candidate ranking reversals when uncertain evaluation inputs vary within supportable bounds
rows.imp_rat: Candidate ranking reversals under input variation is a selection-robustness concern with low relevance to containment; low severity.
rows.sat_rat: Evaluations seldom test robustness to input variation, so ranking reversals are an under-addressed reliability weakness. Investigating ranking reversals under input uncertainty adds iterative analysis time. Sensitivity analysis of rankings requires analytical skill rarely applied in practice. An unstable ranking can drive the wrong costly purchase.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of alternative evaluation delay caused by late required inputs
rows.imp_rat: Evaluation delay from late inputs is a scheduling concern with low relevance to containment; low severity.
rows.sat_rat: Input timeliness is inconsistent across contributors, making delay a recurring, poorly-controlled problem. Late-arriving required inputs chronically delay alternative evaluation with no reliable expediting mechanism. Chasing inputs depends on individual coordination effort rather than any systematic tool. Evaluation delays extend project timelines and carry opportunity cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of critical process requirements not traceably linked to the recommended separation approach
rows.imp_rat: Process requirements not traceably linked to the separation approach weakens the design basis, but capped at 2.0 by ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Traceability from critical process requirements to the separation approach is frequently incomplete. Building traceability from requirements to the approach is a time-consuming documentation task. Maintaining traceability requires disciplined systems-engineering practice often lacking. Untraced requirements cause costly gaps discovered late.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of inconsistency in applying evaluation criteria across candidate separation approaches
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent evaluation criteria across candidate approaches is a selection-quality concern with low relevance to running containment; low severity as it precedes operation.
rows.sat_rat: Without a common rubric, criterion application varies between reviewers and candidates, producing inconsistent and hard-to-defend comparisons. Reconciling how criteria are applied across competing vendors extends the evaluation timeline noticeably. Consistent application depends on the individual evaluator's discipline rather than a standardized scoring framework, making it skill-bound. Inconsistent evaluation can lead to a suboptimal purchase whose cost surfaces only after installation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material technical assumptions supporting the recommendation that remain unverified
rows.imp_rat: Unverified technical assumptions in the recommendation is a reliability gap, but capped by the batch ceiling at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Recommendations routinely rest on assumptions that go formally unverified, a common reliability gap. Verifying each technical assumption adds work to the recommendation phase. Distinguishing verified from unverified assumptions needs disciplined technical judgement. Unverified assumptions that fail later trigger expensive corrections.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of unresolved conflict among production, quality, engineering, and contamination-control criteria
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved conflict among stakeholder criteria is an organizational concern with low relevance to physical containment; low severity.
rows.sat_rat: Cross-functional conflict is a chronic, poorly-structured process with no standard mechanism, so criteria clashes routinely persist. Resolving cross-functional criterion conflicts drags specification through repeated meetings. Mediating production, quality, engineering and contamination-control priorities demands facilitation and technical judgement that no tool supplies. Unresolved conflicts lead to compromises that raise lifecycle cost or rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that process or facility changes invalidate the technical rationale before the purchase decision
rows.imp_rat: Changes invalidating rationale before purchase is a pre-decision concern with low relevance to operating containment; low severity.
rows.sat_rat: Change control rarely feeds back cleanly into a pending specification, so late-change invalidation is a frequent, poorly-managed pain. Late process or facility changes force rationale rework that stalls the decision timeline. Anticipating change impact on the technical rationale requires foresight and judgement that is hard to sustain. Rationale invalidation late in procurement wastes prior evaluation effort and money.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of separation interfaces that permit microbial ingress during sterile production
rows.imp_rat: Interfaces permitting microbial ingress during production strikes at the heart of the closed zone, yet the batch ceiling still caps importance at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Integrity testing controls most interface ingress, though some paths remain difficult to fully verify. Identifying ingress-permitting interfaces is part of standard integrity assessment time. Assessing interface ingress risk requires established aseptic engineering skill. Leaky interfaces found late are costly to fix.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of transferred items entering the closed zone with residual microbial contamination
rows.imp_rat: Transferred items entering with residual contamination breaches the closed zone, but the batch ceiling still caps importance at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Surface decontamination of transferred items is standard but complex-geometry items still occasionally carry residual contamination. Pre-transfer decontamination of items adds process steps but is scheduled routine. Item surface decontamination follows established procedures with routine skill. Transfer decontamination consumes agent and time as a defined cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of separation-boundary surfaces remaining inadequately decontaminated at production start
rows.imp_rat: Boundary surfaces inadequately decontaminated at start directly threatens the decontaminated zone, but the ceiling caps importance at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Validated cycles with BI coverage make adequate surface decontamination at start largely reliable, with shadowed surfaces the residual. Verifying full surface decontamination at start is part of standard cycle validation time. BI-based surface coverage assessment uses established validation skill. Surface mapping and BI studies add moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical separation requirements lacking objective acceptance criteria
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in acceptance criteria for separation requirements is relevant to defensible qualification, but per the batch ceiling it ranks below prior items; severity is bounded as missing criteria are correctable in documentation.
rows.sat_rat: Many critical separation requirements still carry qualitative or missing acceptance criteria, a persistent gap. Defining objective acceptance criteria for each requirement takes deliberate effort. Formulating measurable criteria requires specialist judgement to translate intent into testable limits. Vague criteria lead to disputes and rework that cost money.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sterility-critical interventions lacking defined recovery conditions before sterile production resumes
rows.imp_rat: Interventions lacking defined recovery conditions before resumption risks contamination, but the ordering holds importance at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Many sterility-critical interventions still lack defined recovery conditions, a notable recurring gap. Defining recovery conditions for interventions adds procedural development time. Specifying valid recovery conditions requires contamination-control expertise. Undefined recovery leads to costly line stoppages or discards.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of hard-to-reach isolator locations receiving inadequate decontamination exposure
rows.imp_rat: Hard-to-reach locations getting inadequate decontamination directly threatens the decontaminated zone, but the ceiling limits both proxies to 2.
rows.sat_rat: BI mapping reliably surfaces most poorly-exposed locations, though complex geometries still challenge coverage. Mapping hard-to-reach decontamination spots is part of standard cycle development time. BI placement to find cold spots requires validation expertise that is established. Extra BI mapping and cycle tuning add moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved evidence gaps in the validation basis for the recommended decontamination approach
rows.imp_rat: Evidence gaps in the decontamination validation basis weaken defensibility of the zone, but the ceiling limits importance to 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Decontamination validation is mature, but evidence gaps in the specific recommended approach still recur. Closing validation evidence gaps for decontamination adds study time. Identifying evidence gaps requires validation expertise that is specialized. Unresolved gaps cause costly late validation failures.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of sterile fill-finish interruption attributable to decontamination
rows.imp_rat: Fill-finish interruption from decon is a throughput concern with low relevance to the CFJ; low severity.
rows.sat_rat: Established fast H2O2 cycles make decontamination-driven interruption predictable and moderately well-controlled. Faster decontamination cycles reduce fill-finish interruption, and cycle times are now well-optimized. Cycle scheduling to minimize interruption is a standard planning skill. Downtime from decontamination carries real production cost that shorter cycles mitigate.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of environmental contamination pathways omitted from the isolator design basis
rows.imp_rat: Omitted contamination pathways in the design basis touches the core job, but the ranking caps this; severity of an omitted pathway is capped here though undetected ingress routes are serious.
rows.sat_rat: Design-basis pathway coverage is expertise-dependent and commonly incomplete. Completing the design-basis pathway list adds up-front analysis time. Identifying environmental pathways for the design basis relies on scarce contamination-control expertise. Omissions in the design basis become costly retrofits.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of monitoring-data gaps during interventions, transitions, and losses of normal service
rows.imp_rat: Monitoring-data gaps during interventions/service loss leave blind spots, but the batch ceiling limits importance to 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Continuous monitoring reduces gaps, yet interventions and service losses still create recurring data holes. Closing monitoring-data gaps during interventions and service losses is precisely a duration-reduction need often unmet. Minimizing gaps requires monitoring-system design expertise that is moderately available. Data gaps are cheap to tolerate but costly when they undermine batch defensibility.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of monitoring records that are incomplete, corrupted, unattributable, or disconnected from the applicable batch state
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete or disconnected monitoring records reduce evidentiary value, but the batch ceiling holds importance at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Modern monitoring systems and ALCOA+ practices make record integrity reasonably reliable though gaps persist. Verifying monitoring-record integrity and batch linkage takes ongoing review time. Detecting corrupted or disconnected records needs data-integrity familiarity that is increasingly common. Bad records cost little to overlook but heavily in rejected batches or findings.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in criteria for classifying isolator-zone excursions and initiating investigations
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguous excursion-classification criteria affects investigation triggers, but the ordering caps importance at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Excursion classification criteria frequently retain ambiguity, causing inconsistent investigation triggering. Defining unambiguous excursion-classification criteria adds definitional time often deferred. Setting clear investigation triggers requires deviation-management expertise not always present. Ambiguous criteria are cheap to write but costly in inconsistent investigations.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that monitoring activities compromise zone separation or expose sterile product to contamination
rows.imp_rat: Monitoring activities that compromise separation directly conflict with the core job, but the batch ceiling still caps importance at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Isolator monitoring is generally engineered to avoid breaches, so this is fairly well handled by mature designs. Confirming monitoring won't breach separation adds design-review time but is manageable. Designing non-intrusive monitoring requires specialized aseptic-engineering skill. Intrusive monitoring is cheap to overlook but catastrophically costly if it contaminates product.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical zone conditions that cannot be measured without altering the isolator state
rows.imp_rat: Zone conditions unmeasurable without altering isolator state limits verification, but the batch ceiling caps importance at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Some critical conditions still cannot be measured without disturbing the isolator, a persistent limitation. Determining which conditions can't be measured non-invasively is a slow instrumentation analysis. Identifying non-perturbing measurement needs requires specialized sensor and design knowledge. Non-measurable conditions cost little to note but much when they hide failures.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of qualification records with incomplete source, test-condition, or result attribution
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete attribution in qualification records weakens traceability, but capped at 2.0 by the ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Data-integrity practices have improved, so attribution completeness is moderately well served but not fully. Checking every qualification record for complete attribution is tedious and time-heavy. Recognizing incomplete attribution requires documentation-review discipline more than deep expertise. Incomplete records are cheap now but costly in data-integrity findings.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of relevant production, intervention, transition, cleaning, decontamination, and failure states excluded from compliance review
rows.imp_rat: Excluding relevant states from compliance review reduces coverage, but this documentation-scope error is capped at 2.0 by the ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Compliance review commonly omits abnormal and transitional states, a recurring coverage gap. Enumerating every operational state for compliance review is broad and time-consuming. Complete state coverage requires operational expertise across cleaning, decon, and failure modes. Excluded states cost little now but drive gaps found later at inspection.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material contradictions among proposal descriptions of isolator-zone separation
rows.imp_rat: Contradictions among proposal descriptions is a documentation-quality concern with low relevance to containment; low severity.
rows.sat_rat: Internal proposal contradictions about separation recur and are inconsistently caught. Cross-checking proposal descriptions for internal contradictions is a slow read-through task. Catching inconsistent separation descriptions needs careful expert attention not always applied. Contradictions cost little to fix early but confuse qualification if left.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material proposal evidence gaps unresolved before the purchase recommendation
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved proposal evidence gaps before purchase is a procurement-quality concern with low relevance to containment; low severity.
rows.sat_rat: Purchase recommendations routinely proceed with known unresolved gaps, a common shortfall. Resolving every evidence gap before recommendation lengthens the decision cycle considerably. Judging which gaps are material requires experienced quality judgement not always on hand. Unresolved gaps are cheap to defer but expensive when they surface post-commitment.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that design or use changes invalidate the isolator-zone defensibility conclusion before inspection
rows.imp_rat: Changes invalidating the defensibility conclusion before inspection is an instability concern relevant to compliance standing, but the ceiling holds it at or below 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Change-control linkage to defensibility conclusions is weak, so invalidation often surfaces unexpectedly. Anticipating future changes that invalidate conclusions is forward-looking work rarely done at purchase time. Foreseeing change-driven invalidation requires broad lifecycle expertise that is scarce. Ignoring change risk is cheap now but expensive when conclusions collapse before inspection.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch in challenge conditions between successive qualification runs
rows.imp_rat: Mismatched challenge conditions between qualification runs undermines comparability, but capped at 2.0 by the ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Controlled protocols keep run-to-run challenge conditions largely consistent. Aligning challenge conditions between runs adds setup verification time. Reproducing identical challenge conditions requires disciplined execution skill. Mismatched challenges waste runs and money.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of deviations from the approved qualification sequence across successive runs
rows.imp_rat: Deviations from the approved qualification sequence undermines run comparability, but capped at 2.0 by the ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Approved sequences with execution oversight keep deviations relatively infrequent. Enforcing sequence adherence adds oversight time. Following the approved sequence precisely requires trained, attentive operators. Sequence deviations invalidate runs at cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required qualification records that are missing or invalid
rows.imp_rat: Missing or invalid required qualification records undermines the evidence base, but ranked below prior batch and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Record-control practices are mature, so missing or invalid records are relatively uncommon. Checking for missing or invalid records adds completeness-review time. Ensuring record validity requires trained quality reviewers. Missing records force re-testing at real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of separation requirements translated incorrectly into protocol steps
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect translation of separation requirements into protocol steps can undermine qualification integrity, yet the ordering caps importance at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Protocol templates and reviews catch many translation errors but some incorrect translations still ship. Translating requirements into protocol steps is a bounded authoring task. Correctly translating separation requirements into executable steps requires expert protocol authors. Translation errors force protocol amendments and re-runs at cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of acceptance criteria permitting conflicting pass-or-fail interpretations
rows.imp_rat: Acceptance criteria permitting conflicting pass/fail interpretations affects qualification reliability, but the batch ceiling caps its importance at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Criteria review helps but conflicting pass/fail interpretations recur regularly. Resolving conflicting criteria interpretations consumes negotiation time. Writing criteria that cannot be read two ways requires expert precision that is scarce. Conflicting interpretations cause disputed dispositions and rework cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required separation-evidence elements absent from data-collection requirements
rows.imp_rat: Missing separation-evidence elements from data requirements affects defensibility, but capped at 2.0 by the ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Data-collection templates cover standard elements but required elements are still sometimes omitted. Defining data-collection requirements completely adds planning time. Knowing which evidence elements are required demands experienced validation planning. Missing data elements force repeat runs to capture them.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved conflicts among source requirements for candidate-zone separation
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved conflicts among source requirements creates definitional ambiguity, but ranked below prior batch and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Requirement reconciliation happens but conflicts among sources routinely persist into execution. Reconciling conflicting source requirements consumes upfront time. Resolving requirement conflicts needs senior cross-functional expertise. Unresolved source conflicts cause expensive late rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that protocol execution compromises product-zone separation
rows.imp_rat: Protocol execution compromising product-zone separation directly conflicts with the core job, but capped at 2.0 by ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Trained execution and intervention control make execution-induced breaches uncommon. Designing execution to avoid compromising separation adds planning time. Executing without breaching separation requires skilled aseptic operators. Execution-caused breaches invalidate runs at cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a measurement or record error is mistaken for candidate-zone failure
rows.imp_rat: Mistaking a record error for candidate-zone failure risks false rejects, but this evaluation error is ranked below prior batch and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Good documentation and instrumentation reduce false failures, though errors are still occasionally mistaken for real ones. Distinguishing record errors from real failures adds investigation time. Telling a measurement artifact from a true failure requires experienced diagnostic skill. Misattribution triggers needless re-qualification cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a qualification shortfall cannot be reproduced under equivalent isolator conditions
rows.imp_rat: A qualification shortfall that cannot be reproduced hampers root-cause resolution, but capped at 2.0 by ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Controlled conditions aid reproducibility but some shortfalls still cannot be reproduced. Reproducing shortfalls requires repeat setup and run time. Achieving reproducibility under equivalent conditions requires skilled control. Non-reproducible shortfalls waste investigation resources.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of failed results lacking traceability to the as-tested isolator state
rows.imp_rat: Failed results lacking traceability to the as-tested state impedes interpretation, but the ceiling caps importance at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Execution records support traceability but failures still sometimes lack clear links to as-tested state. Linking failures to as-tested state requires documentation retrieval time. Establishing state traceability needs disciplined record-keeping skill. Untraceable failures prompt costly re-investigation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of causal uncertainty created by departures from specified test conditions
rows.imp_rat: Causal uncertainty from departures from test conditions weakens qualification, but capped at 2.0 by the batch ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Controlled test conditions limit departures, but when they occur causal uncertainty is a recurring problem. Investigating causal uncertainty from test-condition departures extends timelines. Attributing cause amid condition departures requires strong analytical skill. Causal ambiguity drives repeat testing costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of causal ambiguity created by concurrent changes to isolator conditions
rows.imp_rat: Causal ambiguity from concurrent changes complicates deviation diagnosis, but capped at 2.0 by ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Change control helps isolate variables but concurrent-change ambiguity still recurs. Untangling concurrent changes adds investigation time. Attributing cause amid concurrent changes requires strong analytical skill. Causal ambiguity forces repeat runs at cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of doubt that a consequential failure mode remains unexamined before endorsement
rows.imp_rat: Doubt that a failure mode remains unexamined before endorsement is a residual-risk concern, but ranked below prior batch and capped.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a facility or utility disturbance is misattributed to the candidate zone
rows.imp_rat: Misattributing a facility disturbance to the candidate zone misdirects investigation, but ranked below prior batch and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Facility monitoring aids attribution but misattribution of utility disturbances still occurs. Distinguishing facility disturbances from zone issues adds investigation time. Correctly attributing utility disturbances requires broad systems expertise. Misattribution causes unnecessary re-qualification cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of failed results left unexplained by the assigned root cause
rows.imp_rat: Failed results unexplained by the assigned root cause risks recurrence, but the ceiling limits importance to 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Root-cause processes are standard but a share of failures remain incompletely explained. Root-cause investigations to explain failures are time-consuming. Assigning correct root cause requires experienced investigators. Unexplained failures delay disposition and add cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved conflicts between qualification criteria and intended-use requirements
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved conflicts between qualification and intended-use requirements is relevant to a defensible zone, but ranked below the prior batch and thus capped.
rows.sat_rat: Requirement reviews exist but conflicts between qualification and intended-use requirements persist. Reconciling qualification criteria with use requirements adds cross-team time. Resolving these conflicts requires senior cross-functional judgement. Unresolved conflicts cause late redesign costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of qualification records lacking source, execution, or approval traceability
rows.imp_rat: Qualification records lacking traceability weakens defensibility, but capped at 2.0 by ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Controlled documentation systems keep most records traceable, though gaps still appear. Verifying full record traceability adds review time. Maintaining source-execution-approval traceability requires documentation discipline. Broken traceability triggers audit findings and rework cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of variation in separation results across equivalent evaluations
rows.imp_rat: Variation in separation results across equivalent evaluations undermines confidence, but ranked below prior batch and capped.
